phases.focus: Research & feasibility: prove the concept on lean foundations. Full design controls don't apply to research yet - set up the light framework that accelerates you and settle the gates (classification, intended purpose, scientific validity)
Development starts - now the full lifecycle applies: design controls, risk, software & usability under ISO 13485, generating verification evidence as you build
Run the management system: leadership, internal audit and management review that keep the QMS alive while the product matures
Prove, certify & launch: validation evidence, the technical file, Notified Body assessment and CE - then operate the post-market loop

activities.why: Design controls turn an idea into a verified, validated, transferable design with the evidence to prove it. ISO 13485 (7.3) and the FDA QMSR require design inputs to be defined and reviewed, design outputs to meet inputs, design reviews at suitable stages, verification that outputs meet inputs, validation that the device meets user needs and intended use, and a controlled transfer to production. Without rigorous design controls the technical file has gaps a Notified Body or the FDA will reject.
activities.what: Implement the full design-control loop: capture and approve design inputs (user needs, intended use, requirements, applicable standards), produce design outputs, hold design reviews, perform verification and validation, maintain the design history file, and control design transfer - all traceable input-to-output-to-V&V.
activities.how:
1. Capture & approve design inputs (DEV-TPL-01/08).
2. Produce design outputs (DEV-TPL-09); hold design reviews (DEV-FOR-06).
3. Build the traceability matrix (DEV-LIS-01).

steps.d: start from what the user actually needs and the intended use - not the feature you're excited about. Everything downstream traces back to these.
write the requirements (functional, performance, safety, regulatory, usability). Make each specific and testable, and resolve conflicts before building.
get the inputs reviewed and signed off before serious development - changing inputs later is the most expensive kind of rework.
create the outputs (specs, drawings, code, labelling) that meet the inputs, each with acceptance criteria so you can later prove they're met.

steps.t: Hold design reviews at the gates
steps.d: at each stage gate, hold a formal design review with the right people and record the issues and actions. These minutes are prime audit evidence.
test that each output meets its input requirement (did we build it right?). Record the verification with results, not just 'pass'.
prove the finished device meets the user's actual needs and intended use under real or simulated conditions (did we build the right thing?), with any clinical/performance evidence.
hand the verified design to production deliberately, with documented transfer, so manufacturing builds exactly what was designed.
maintain the design history file as you go - it's the story of how the device was designed, and the Notified Body will read it.
maintain traceability: need -> input -> output -> verification -> validation -> risk. If you can follow that thread for any requirement, your design controls work.

steps.t: Design inputs are traceable to design outputs.
Verification and validation results are recorded against acceptance criteria.
Design changes are controlled and re-verified.
A design history file is maintained.

tips: Traceability is the whole game. An auditor picks one requirement and follows it to its test - if the thread breaks anywhere, confidence in the entire design collapses.
Design reviews are not status meetings. They're decision gates with the right disciplines in the room, and the minutes are gold-standard audit evidence. Don't skip them to 'save time'.
Verification is not validation. Verification: you met the spec. Validation: you met the user's real need. Many first devices ace verification and never truly validate - that's the gap auditors find.
Build the design history file AS you go. Reconstructing it from memory and scattered emails before an audit is the single most painful task in a device project.

lean.start: A one-page D&D procedure, a single inputs → outputs → V&V traceability table, and dated design-review notes. The traceability matters more than the tooling.
lean.evolve: Add formal review boards, a structured design history file and ALM tooling as the design grows.
startDetail: A one-page design-control procedure (DEV-SOP-01) naming the stages, who approves each, and where verification, validation and design transfer sit.
A single traceability table linking each design input → design output → verification/validation evidence → the risk it controls.
Design inputs captured and approved: user needs, intended use, the applicable standards, and regulatory/performance requirements.
A design-review record — even dated meeting notes — at each major stage, listing attendees, decisions and actions.
A named design history file location (a folder is fine) where every version and review is retained.
Entry/exit criteria for design transfer written down, so 'done' is defined before you build.
evolveDetail: Formal design-review boards with defined membership and recorded minutes at each stage gate.
A structured, indexed design history file replacing the ad-hoc folder.
Application-lifecycle-management (ALM) tooling for live requirements-to-test traceability.

subs.why: Design inputs are the requirements your device must meet - user needs, intended use, safety, regulatory and performance. Weak inputs guarantee a weak device, because everything downstream traces back to them.
subs.what: Capture, review and approve the design inputs as a controlled, testable set.

how.d: User needs, intended use, the relevant standards and regulations, safety & performance requirements, and the risk-control requirements from the risk file.
Every input must be verifiable later - 'easy to use' is not an input; 'task X completed in under 30s by 95% of users' is.
Check the inputs are complete, unambiguous and not contradicting each other before you build on them.
Review and formally approve the input set as a controlled document - the frozen basis for the design.
So outputs and tests can trace back to it.

subs.why: Design outputs are what you actually make - specs, drawings, code, labelling. They must meet the inputs and give production what it needs to build the device consistently.
subs.what: Produce the design outputs and confirm they meet the inputs and contain the information needed for production.

how.d: Specifications, drawings, source code, labelling, IFU and manufacturing information.
Outputs must define how production checks conformity (tolerances, tests, criteria).
Enough information to make and inspect the device consistently.

how.t: Review each output against its inputs
how.d: Every output traces to an input it satisfies; flag any output with no input (scope creep).
Formally review and approve the outputs prior to design transfer to production.

subs.why: Traceability - inputs to outputs to verification to validation - is the single most powerful audit artefact you have. It proves nothing was forgotten and nothing was added without reason.
subs.what: Build and maintain a matrix linking each input to its output, verification and validation.

how.d: Every requirement maps to the design output(s) that satisfy it.
Every requirement maps to the test or evidence that proves it is met.
Show where risk-control measures appear as requirements, outputs and tests.
Update it on every design change - a gap in the matrix is a gap in your evidence.

subs.why: Verification asks 'did we build the device right?' - does each output meet its input. It is how you catch defects before they reach a patient.
subs.what: Verify that design outputs meet the design inputs, against pre-set acceptance criteria.

how.d: Each output against its input, with the method, sample size and acceptance criteria decided up front.
Run the verification and record results objectively against the pre-set criteria.
Feed failures back into design, document the change, and re-verify.
The evidence that outputs meet inputs - part of the design history file.

subs.why: Validation asks 'did we build the right device?' - does it meet the user's needs in real use. It is the evidence that the device is fit for its intended purpose.
subs.what: Validate that the device meets user needs and intended use, on representative product and users.

how.d: Production-equivalent devices with actual intended users, not engineers.

how.t: Validate in the real use environment
how.d: Or a realistic simulation of where the device will be used.

how.t: Confirm intended use is met safely
how.d: The device does what it claims for the patient population, under foreseeable conditions.
Validation feeds, and is fed by, the clinical evidence where required.
For the technical file - your proof the right device was built.

activities.statement: Intended purpose + D&D plan + stakeholder needs.
activities.why: Design and development is where the device's safety and performance are actually built in, so a planned, controlled D&D process is the backbone of the technical file and the core of conformity. ISO 13485 requires product realization and design & development to be planned (7.1, 7.3.2), and for software IEC 62304 requires a development plan keyed to the software safety class (5.1). Planning this now - the stages, reviews, verification and validation, and the records each produces - sets up Phase 2's detailed design controls and prevents an unstructured build that cannot be evidenced later.
activities.what: Plan the product-realization and design & development process: define the D&D stages, the reviews / verification / validation at each, responsibilities and the records to be produced; for software, establish the development plan and safety classification per IEC 62304. The output is an approved D&D / realization plan that drives Phase 2.
activities.how:
1. Map the core design-build-release process.
2. Prepare the D&D plan (DEV-TPL-07) with phases and review gates.
3. For software, prepare the development plan and safety class (SWD-TPL-01).
activities.leads: RSK.plan, TEF.classify.mdr, TEF.classify.ivdr, CLE.plan, PEV.plan, DEV.inputs, SWD.plan, AIM.plan

steps.d: break development into clear stages (concept, design, verification, validation, transfer) with a gate/review at the end of each. This is your design plan.
for each stage, say what design review, verification (did we build it right?) and validation (did we build the right thing?) happens, and who's responsible.
if there's software, determine its safety class (IEC 62304). This sets how rigorous the software work must be - get it right, it drives a lot of effort.
create the IEC 62304 development plan - processes, activities and deliverables - sized to that safety class.
plan the realization controls the device will need in manufacture, so design hands over cleanly to production rather than 'over the wall'.

steps.t: Plan what records each stage makes
steps.d: list the records (inputs, outputs, review minutes, V&V reports) each stage will generate, so evidence accrues as you go, not in a panic before audit.

steps.t: Map records to the technical file
steps.d: map each planned record to its place in the MDR/IVDR Annex II structure, so the technical file builds itself as you work.
confirm this plan is the input to Phase 2's design controls (P2.2), software lifecycle (P2.3) and risk (P2.1) - it's the hub they all start from.

steps.t: A design & development plan defines stages, reviews, verification and validation.
Design responsibilities and interfaces are assigned.
Where software is present, a development plan and safety class are defined.

records: Software development plan with safety classification (IEC 62304)
Mapping of planned records to the technical-file structure
tips: This is on the critical path for a reason: nearly every Phase 2 activity depends on the plan you make here. Rushing it pushes the delay everywhere downstream.
Software safety classification is the single highest-leverage decision in software development. Mis-class it low and an auditor will make you redo the lifecycle - when unsure, get expert input.
Plan the records, not just the work. The difference between a smooth audit and a nightmare is whether evidence was captured as you went or reconstructed afterwards.
Verification and validation are not the same word twice: verification = met the spec; validation = met the user's actual need. Auditors check you know the difference and did both.

lean.start: A written intended-purpose statement, a short list of stakeholder needs, and a one-page design & development plan. One page each is enough for a simple device.
lean.evolve: Grow the plan, the needs and the reviews into a formal, traceable design-control system as complexity rises.
startDetail: Intended-purpose statement: what the device does, the medical indication, the patient population, the intended user, the use environment, and its operating principle.
The boundaries written down — what the device is explicitly NOT for — to keep scope and classification tight.
A plain-language list of stakeholder and user needs (clinicians, patients, your own team), each phrased so it can later become a traceable design input.
A one-page D&D plan: the stages (planning → inputs → outputs → verification → validation → transfer), who owns each, and where the review and V&V gates sit.
An early note of the regulation and standards you already expect to apply (MDR/IVDR class, key harmonised standards), so classification is not a late surprise.
evolveDetail: Expand the plan into formal stage gates with a review board and recorded design-review minutes.
Grow user needs into a managed requirements set with full traceability to inputs, outputs, V&V and risk.
Add dedicated software/AI development, interface and human-factors/usability plans as the device grows.

subs.why: Design controls keep a product safe by structuring how it is developed. Defining the stages up front is what turns 'we built it' into 'we developed it under control'.
subs.what: Define the D&D stages and the gates between them, proportionate to your device.

how.d: Inputs -> design -> verification -> validation -> design transfer to production.
What must be true (and reviewed) to pass from one stage to the next.
What each stage must produce before its gate.
Lighter for a simple device, deeper for a complex or higher-risk one.

how.t: Capture it in the design plan
how.d: A controlled document you keep updated as the design evolves.

subs.why: Verification shows you built it right; validation shows you built the right thing. Planning where each happens stops you discovering at the end that you cannot prove either.
subs.what: Plan where verification and validation occur and against what acceptance criteria.

how.d: Where design outputs are checked against design inputs.
Where the device is shown to meet user needs and intended use, on representative product and users.
Decide pass/fail before you test, not after seeing results.
How each test is run and on how many units, with a rationale.

subs.why: Design fails at the handoffs. Assigning responsibilities and managing the interfaces between functions (and suppliers) keeps things from falling through the cracks.
subs.what: Assign responsibility for each design activity and define how the interfaces between groups are managed.

how.t: Assign an owner per design activity
how.d: Who is responsible for each part of the design.
How design groups, and suppliers, communicate and hand off - where most defects hide.

how.t: Define how decisions are made & recorded
how.d: So design decisions are traceable and not lost.
Ensure design feeds, and reflects, the risk file and usability work.

subs.t: Plan the software lifecycle & safety class
subs.why: If your device contains software, IEC 62304 requires a development plan and a safety classification that scales the rigour. Setting the class early sizes the whole software effort.
subs.what: Where software is present, write the software development plan and assign the safety class (A/B/C).

how.d: A, B or C, based on the harm a software failure could cause - this sizes the whole effort.
Scale the activities and documentation to the class.
Third-party / off-the-shelf software components, with their versions.
How software contributes to risk control and how anomalies are handled.
So every release is reproducible and traceable.

subs.why: Design reviews are the formal checkpoints where problems are caught cheaply. Scheduling them in the plan means they happen, rather than being skipped under deadline pressure.
subs.what: Schedule formal design reviews at the stage gates, with the right people and recorded outcomes.

how.t: Place reviews at the stage gates
how.d: At least one formal review per major stage.
People not directly doing the design, to catch what insiders miss.
Against inputs, risk, and the stage's deliverables.
Decisions, issues found and follow-ups - the design-review record.

activities.statement: Turn stakeholder needs into design inputs and outputs.
activities.why: Design inputs translate the intended purpose, user needs, regulatory and risk requirements into concrete, verifiable requirements; design outputs are the specifications that meet them and enable production. ISO 13485 7.3.3-7.3.4 require inputs to be complete, unambiguous and reviewed, and outputs to be verifiable against them. Weak inputs are the root cause of most late design problems and verification failures.
activities.what: Capture complete, unambiguous, verifiable design inputs from the intended purpose, stakeholder needs, GSPR and risk; produce the design outputs (product requirements and design specifications) that meet them and can be verified.
activities.how:
1. Capture & approve design inputs (DEV-TPL-01/08).
2. Produce design outputs (DEV-TPL-09); hold design reviews (DEV-FOR-06).
3. Build the traceability matrix (DEV-LIS-01).

steps.d: From intended purpose, user needs, GSPR, risk and standards - verifiable and reviewed.
Product requirements and design specifications meeting the inputs.
Inputs -> outputs -> verification, in the traceability matrix.

tips: Make every input verifiable - 'easy to use' is not an input; a measurable criterion is.
Pull GSPR and risk controls in as inputs, not afterthoughts.
Baseline and review inputs before designing - churny inputs cause churny outputs.
Traceability from input to output to verification is the spine a reviewer follows.

lean.start: A reviewed product-requirements spec covering intended use, key GSPR and risk controls, with each output traceable back to it.
lean.evolve: Grow to full bidirectional traceability across inputs, outputs, verification and validation.
startDetail: Design inputs captured from the intended purpose, stakeholder needs, applicable GSPR and the risk controls — each stated so it is verifiable.
A single product-requirements specification, reviewed and approved, as the controlled input baseline.
Design outputs (specifications, drawings, requirements) produced to meet the inputs.
A traceability line from each input → the output(s) that satisfy it.
Ambiguous or unverifiable inputs flagged and reworded before they are baselined.
Acceptance criteria noted for each input, so verification later has something to test against.
evolveDetail: Bidirectional traceability (input ↔ output ↔ verification ↔ validation) maintained in a tool.
Requirements baselined and change-controlled with impact analysis.
Standards and GSPR mapped item-by-item to the requirements that satisfy them.

subs.why: Complete, verifiable inputs are the foundation of a controllable design.
subs.what: Capture inputs from purpose, users, GSPR and risk.

how.d: Measurable acceptance for each.
Agreed before design proceeds.

subs.why: Outputs are what get verified and transferred to production.
subs.what: Specify product requirements and design specifications.

how.d: Outputs map to inputs.
Enough to manufacture and inspect.

subs.why: Traceability proves nothing was dropped between need and evidence.
subs.what: Build the input-output-verification traceability.

how.d: Each requirement to a spec.
Each output to its check.

activities.statement: Verify design outputs against inputs, then freeze the design.
activities.why: Design verification confirms that design outputs meet design inputs - that you built the device right. ISO 13485 7.3.6 requires planned verification with records; for a general/hardware device this is bench testing, analysis and inspection against the requirements specification (for an IVD, analytical performance plays this role). Verification precedes and gates the design freeze: once verification passes and open items are closed you freeze the design, so validation and transfer run on a stable, controlled baseline.
activities.what: Plan and execute design verification against the product requirements, demonstrating outputs meet inputs; close verification findings; then formally freeze the design (design freeze record) so validation and transfer proceed on a stable baseline.
activities.how: [TO AUTHOR in Phase 2]

steps.d: Methods (test/analysis/inspection) mapped to each input requirement.
Run verification, record results, close open items.
Baseline the design (freeze record) once verification is complete.

tips: Verification asks 'did we meet the inputs?'; validation asks 'does it meet user needs?' - keep the two distinct.
Freeze only when verification is complete and findings are closed, or validation runs on a moving target.
Keep verification traceable to each requirement - the traceability matrix is what a reviewer follows.

lean.start: Verification of the key requirements with records, then a documented design freeze.
lean.evolve: Full requirement-by-requirement verification traceability feeding the technical file.
startDetail: A verification plan naming what will be tested, the method, and the acceptance criteria per requirement.
Verification executed against the product requirements, with dated results and pass/fail recorded.
Verification findings triaged and closed (or risk-accepted) before freeze.
A design-freeze record fixing the baseline that validation and transfer will build on.
Traceability from each verified requirement to its evidence.
evolveDetail: Complete requirement-by-requirement verification coverage with no gaps.
Automated regression so re-verification is cheap after changes.
Verification evidence indexed straight into the technical file.

subs.why: Verification is only credible if every input requirement has a defined check.
subs.what: Map each requirement to a verification method and acceptance.

how.d: Test, analysis or inspection per requirement.
Every requirement has a verification.

subs.why: Open verification findings block a safe freeze.
subs.what: Run verification, record results and close open items.

how.d: Pass/fail traceable to the requirement.
Deviations resolved or justified.

subs.why: A stable baseline is what validation and transfer depend on.
subs.what: Formally baseline the verified design.

how.d: Design freeze record with the baseline configuration.
Post-freeze changes go through change control.

activities.statement: Validate against user needs, transfer to production, release.
activities.why: Design validation confirms the device meets user needs and intended use under actual or simulated use conditions - that you built the right device. ISO 13485 7.3.7 requires validation on representative product before release; for many devices this is where clinical / performance evidence and summative usability land. Design transfer (7.3.8) then verifies the design is correctly translated into production, and the product is released for market once validation, risk and transfer are complete.
activities.what: Validate the design against user needs and intended use (drawing on the clinical/performance evidence and summative usability), verify design transfer into production, and record the product development release once validation, risk and transfer are all closed.
activities.how: [TO AUTHOR in Phase 2]

steps.d: Against user needs / intended use, referencing clinical/performance + summative usability.
Confirm the design is correctly translated into production specs, processes and training.
Record release once validation, residual risk and transfer are complete.

tips: Validate on representative production units, not prototypes - validation on the wrong build does not count.
Validation pulls together clinical/performance evidence and summative usability - reference them, do not duplicate.
Design transfer is a common audit finding - prove the design actually reached production correctly (specs, process, training).
Do not release until residual risk / benefit-risk, validation and transfer are all closed.

lean.start: Validation against user needs on representative product, referencing your clinical/usability evidence, then a controlled release.
lean.evolve: Full transfer verification and release records, with post-market feedback re-validating over time.
startDetail: A validation plan tying user needs and intended use to the evidence that will demonstrate them.
Validation performed on representative (production-equivalent) product, not prototypes.
Clinical/performance and summative-usability evidence drawn in as validation inputs.
Design-transfer verification: production can reproducibly build to spec.
A product-development release record signed once validation, risk and transfer are all closed.
evolveDetail: Formal transfer protocols with process capability demonstrated before release.
Release governed by a gate that checks risk, V&V and transfer are complete.
Post-market feedback loops that re-open validation when real-world use shifts.

subs.why: Meeting requirements is not the same as meeting user needs; validation is the 'right device' check.
subs.what: Validate on representative product under actual/simulated use.

how.d: Clinical/performance evaluation + summative usability.
Production-equivalent, not prototypes.

subs.why: A validated design still fails if production does not reproduce it.
subs.what: Confirm the design is correctly translated into manufacturing.

how.d: Production specs match the design outputs.
Operators and controls in place.

subs.why: Release is the gate that says everything required is complete.
subs.what: Record the product development release.

how.d: Validation, risk and transfer all closed.
Product development release document.

subs.why: Release must reflect an acceptable residual-risk position.
subs.what: Confirm the risk management report's benefit-risk supports release.

how.d: From the RM report.
Benefit-risk referenced in the release.

activities.why: An in-vitro diagnostic is developed around its intended purpose and its analytical and clinical performance - what it measures, in whom, and how well. This activity stands up the IVD-specific design process so your performance claims are designed in and provable, not bolted on.
activities.what: Run the IVD design & development process under design controls: state the intended purpose, the intended-use population and setting; define the performance specification (the analytical and clinical claims you will prove); design the assay/system to meet it; and plan the analytical and clinical performance studies that generate the IVDR Annex XIII evidence.

steps.d: Take DEV-SOP-02 and write the intended-purpose and performance specification (DEV-TPL-03) - analyte, matrix, population, use.
Develop the assay/system with design inputs/outputs and reviews.
Plan analytical performance (DEV-TPL-04) and scientific validity / clinical performance (DEV-TPL-05).
Run analytical studies (precision, LoD, interference) and record results on DEV-FOR-02/03.
Finalise the performance specification and design outputs (DEV-TPL-06); log studies on DEV-FOR-04/05.

steps.t: Analytical performance (precision, LoD, interference) is documented.
The intended purpose is fixed before design proceeds.

tips: For an IVD the intended purpose drives everything - pin down analyte, matrix, population and use before you design.
Analytical performance is your foundation; weak analytical data sinks the clinical claims.
Specimen handling and stability are part of the design, not an afterthought.

lean.start: A clear intended-purpose statement and a performance specification, with an analytical performance plan tied to your design inputs.
lean.evolve: Add full clinical performance studies and broader matrix/interference coverage as the assay matures.
startDetail: Intended-purpose statement: the analyte measured, the clinical condition/indication, the specimen type, the target population, and the intended user/setting (lab, near-patient, self-test).
A performance specification listing the analytical and clinical claims you intend to prove (e.g. sensitivity, specificity, measuring range).
The measurand and measurement principle documented, with the reference method/material where one exists.
An analytical performance plan tied to design inputs: precision, trueness, LoD/LoQ, interferences and stability.
A note of the IVDR class expectation and whether a companion-diagnostic or self-test claim raises the evidence burden.
evolveDetail: Prospective clinical performance studies to establish clinical sensitivity/specificity in the intended population.
Broader interference, cross-reactivity and matrix-comparison panels.
Method-comparison against a reference or predicate, plus commutability and metrological traceability work.

subs.why: For an IVD the intended purpose drives everything - analyte, matrix, population and use must be fixed before design.
subs.what: Adapt DEV-SOP-02 and write the intended-purpose and performance specification.

how.d: Analyte, matrix, target population and clinical use - fixed before design.
The analytical and clinical claims (DEV-TPL-03).
Collection, stability and pre-analytics - part of the design, not an afterthought.
The performance the assay must hit.

subs.why: Designing under controls and planning analytical and clinical performance is how the claims become provable.
subs.what: Develop the assay under design controls and plan the analytical and clinical performance studies.

how.d: Inputs, outputs, reviews and verification.
Precision, LoD, linearity, interference (DEV-TPL-04).
Scientific validity and clinical performance (DEV-TPL-05).
How you will compare results to the truth standard.

subs.why: Running analytical verification and compiling the design outputs builds the evidence base for performance evaluation.
subs.what: Run analytical verification, record results, and compile the design outputs.

how.d: Precision, LoD, interference and the rest of the plan.
DEV-FOR-02/03.
Feed back into design and re-test.
DEV-TPL-06; log on DEV-FOR-04/05 - this feeds performance evaluation.

activities.why: IVD design and development follows the same design-control discipline but with assay-specific inputs and outputs: the intended purpose and analyte, the assay design, reagents and specimen requirements, and the design plan geared to the IVDR performance-evaluation evidence. Getting the IVD design right and traceable is what makes the analytical and clinical performance studies meaningful.
activities.what: Execute the IVD design and development: the IVD design plan, product requirements and design specification for the assay, design reviews and the freeze/transfer/release records - all geared to feed the IVDR performance evaluation.
activities.documents: DEV-SOP-02; DEV-TPL-04; DEV-TPL-05; DEV-TPL-06; DEV-FOR-02; DEV-FOR-03; DEV-FOR-04; DEV-FOR-05

steps.d: Assay design plan tied to intended purpose and analyte.
Reagents, specimen types, design specification.
Design reviews and the IVD freeze/transfer/release records.

records: IVD design review / freeze / transfer / release
tips: Anchor the IVD design in the intended purpose and analyte - everything (AP, CP) traces back to it.
Specify specimen types and reagents precisely - they define what the performance studies must cover.
Freeze the IVD design before analytical performance - a changed assay invalidates the data.
Keep the IVD design traceable to the performance-evaluation plan.

lean.start: An IVD design plan and assay specification traceable to the intended purpose, with design reviews.
lean.evolve: Full IVD design controls with freeze/transfer/release feeding the performance evaluation.
startDetail: An IVD design plan covering the assay/system stages, reviews and where performance studies sit.
Product requirements and a design specification for the assay, traceable to the intended purpose.
Design reviews at the key stages with recorded decisions.
Freeze / transfer / release records geared to feed the IVDR performance evaluation.
The analytical and clinical performance evidence needs identified against each claim.
evolveDetail: Complete design-control traceability from intended purpose to performance evidence.
Lot-to-lot and stability considerations designed in and verified.
Transfer and manufacturing controls validated for reproducible assay performance.

subs.why: The plan aligns the assay design with the intended purpose and evidence needs.
subs.what: Write the IVD design and development plan.

how.d: Design serves the intended claim.
Design enables AP/CP studies.

subs.why: Precise assay specs define what is verified and produced.
subs.what: Specify reagents, specimen types and design outputs.

how.d: Matrix, handling, stability.
Producible assay specification.

subs.why: Controlled progression is what keeps the design valid for the studies.
subs.what: Run design reviews and the freeze/transfer/release records.

how.d: Stable design for performance studies.
Into production.

activities.why: Risk management is the spine that runs through the entire device lifecycle - every design decision, software unit, security control and post-market signal connects back to it. ISO 14971 requires a planned process to identify hazards, estimate and evaluate risk, implement and verify risk controls, and judge overall residual risk, maintained from development through production and post-production. Integrating safety risk (ISO 14971) with security risk (AAMI TIR57) and, for AI, the AI Act's risk requirements avoids three disconnected risk systems and gives one coherent risk file that everything else feeds.
activities.what: Establish the integrated risk-management process and file: a risk-management plan, hazard and threat identification, risk estimation and evaluation against defined acceptability criteria, risk-control implementation and verification, overall residual-risk evaluation, and the link to production and post-production monitoring - covering safety, security and (where relevant) AI risk in one framework.
activities.how:
1. Complete the Risk Management Plan first (RSK-TPL-01): criteria & matrix.
2. Start the risk table (RSK-TPL-03) with hazards/threats/AI risks.
3. Define how security (TIR57) and AI (Art 9) risk branch into the same file.

steps.d: before analysing anything, write down HOW you'll do risk: scope, what risk level you'll accept, who's responsible, and when you'll review (ISO 14971 clause 4). Agree this first.
decide, before you look, what counts as acceptable vs unacceptable risk (a severity x probability matrix is fine). Deciding this after you see the risks is how bias creeps in.
systematically list hazards - how the device, its use, its data and its environment could cause harm. Use the intended use and known problems with similar devices as prompts.
for connected/software devices, add security threats (TIR57) to the SAME file - a hacked device is a safety hazard, so keep them together.
for each hazardous situation estimate how bad (severity) and how likely (probability), and compare against your acceptability criteria.

steps.t: Control risks in the right order
steps.d: reduce risk by (1) safe design first, (2) protective measures, (3) information/warnings last. Warnings are the weakest control - don't lead with them.
prove each control is implemented AND effective, and check it didn't create a new risk. An unverified control is just a hope.
step back and judge whether the device's total leftover risk is acceptable against its benefit. This benefit-risk conclusion is what a Notified Body scrutinises hardest.

steps.t: Connect it to production & the field
steps.d: wire the risk file to production and post-market so real-world data (complaints, incidents) flows back and keeps it true. Risk management never 'finishes'.

steps.t: Feed your area into the file
steps.d: design, software and AI leads each contribute their risks and implement the controls assigned to them, with traceability back to requirements.

steps.t: The risk management plan sets the acceptability criteria before assessment.
Every identified hazard is traced to a risk control that is verified.
Residual risk and overall benefit-risk are evaluated and documented.
The risk file is linked to design and post-market inputs.

tips: Risk is the file an auditor reads most carefully - and the one most often done as box-ticking. A risk file that genuinely drove design decisions looks completely different from one written to pass.
Set acceptability criteria BEFORE you analyse risks. Otherwise the temptation to declare whatever you found 'acceptable' is overwhelming - and obvious to an auditor.
Warnings and instructions are the WEAKEST control. If your mitigation for everything is 'we'll put it in the manual', expect a finding - design the risk out first.
Keep one integrated risk file (safety + security + AI), not three. Disconnected files miss the risks that only appear at the boundaries between them.
Risk management is lifelong, not a phase. Every complaint, change and field signal should be able to reopen the risk file - build that loop now.

lean.start: A one-page risk-management plan, your acceptability criteria written down, and a single risk table seeded with your first hazards. A spreadsheet is enough to begin.
lean.evolve: Deepen the method and connect the risk file to live data as the device and organisation mature.
startDetail: Risk-management plan (1 page): name the person responsible, the scope (this device and its intended use), and when risk activities happen in each design phase.
Acceptability criteria, written before you score anything: a simple severity × probability matrix (3×3 is fine) with a clear accept / reduce-further / unacceptable threshold.
One risk table (a spreadsheet tab): hazard → hazardous situation → harm, severity, probability, risk level, control measure, residual severity/probability, residual level.
Plain-language definitions of your severity and probability scales, so two people score the same risk the same way.
A seed list of 10–20 hazards brainstormed from the intended use, users and environment — energy, biological/chemical, usability, data/software, environmental.
A rule for risks you cannot reduce further: record the benefit-risk rationale rather than leaving the cell blank.
evolveDetail: Add dedicated risk branches for cybersecurity (AAMI TIR57) and for AI/ML behaviour where they apply.
Move from a single table to structured FMEA or fault-tree analysis for the highest-severity hazards.
Wire the risk file to production and post-market data so probabilities are revisited with real evidence, not estimates.
Formalise sign-off: a risk-management review and report gate before each design review and before release.

subs.why: Risk management is the backbone of medical-device safety and the one process auditors probe hardest. The plan sets the rules of the game - above all, what level of risk you will accept - before you start judging risks, so the decisions are principled rather than convenient.
subs.what: Adapt the risk management plan: scope, responsibilities, the risk-acceptability criteria and how risk feeds design and post-market.

how.t: Define the scope & the device it covers
how.d: Name the device/variant and the lifecycle phases the file covers (design through production, post-market and disposal). One plan per device family.
Decide, in writing and BEFORE any results, what residual risk is acceptable. Anchor it to the state of the art and to clinical benefit - not to what is convenient once you see the numbers.
Define the levels (e.g. a 5x5 matrix) and write what each means for THIS device, so two people estimating the same risk land in the same place.
How you will identify, estimate, evaluate, control and review risk (e.g. top-down hazard analysis plus FMEA for failure modes).
Who maintains the risk file, who performs the analysis, and who approves residual risk and signs the risk report.
State how risk controls become design inputs and how production and field feedback flow back to update the file.

subs.why: You can only control risks you have foreseen. Systematically listing hazards and the situations that turn them into harm is where most safety value is created - and where most teams stop too early.
subs.what: Identify foreseeable hazards and the sequences of events that lead to harm, across normal and fault conditions.

how.d: Intended use, intended users and environment, the device's energy/material/data characteristics, and known hazards for similar devices or predecessors.
Energy (electrical, thermal, mechanical, radiation), biological & chemical, usability, data & cybersecurity, environmental - systematically, category by category, not ad hoc.

how.t: Build the hazard -> situation -> harm chain
how.d: For each hazard, describe the sequence of events that exposes someone, and the actual harm and who it reaches (patient, user, bystander).
A hazardous situation can arise in normal use or when one thing fails - capture both.
The predictable wrong ways people will actually use it, not only the intended use.

how.t: Log every chain in the risk table
how.d: One row per hazard->situation->harm, ready to estimate - this becomes the risk analysis record.

subs.why: Estimating severity and probability, then comparing against your criteria, turns a list of worries into prioritised decisions about what must be controlled.
subs.what: Estimate the risk of each hazardous situation and evaluate it against the acceptability criteria.

how.d: The worst credible harm for each situation, on your defined scale.
Where you can, split it: P1 that the hazardous situation occurs x P2 that it then leads to harm. Otherwise a single, justified estimate.

how.t: Base it on evidence, not opinion
how.d: Use field data, literature, complaint history and similar-device data wherever possible; record the basis of each estimate.

how.t: Compare each risk to your criteria
how.d: Acceptable as-is, or does it require control? Apply the criteria you set in the plan.
Rank by risk so control effort goes where the potential harm is greatest.

subs.why: A control that is designed but not implemented and verified is just an intention. ISO 14971 also forces the right order: design it out first, then guard, then warn.
subs.what: Apply risk controls in the priority order (inherent safety, protective measures, information for safety) and verify each is effective.

how.t: Apply controls in the required order
how.d: 1) inherent safety by design, 2) protective measures in the device or manufacture, 3) information for safety. Work DOWN this order - never jump straight to a warning label.

how.t: Make each control a real design output
how.d: Turn it into a requirement/specification traced in the design file, not just a note in the risk table.
Confirm it is actually present in the built device.
Test that it reduces the risk as intended - do not assume.

how.t: Check for risks the control introduced
how.d: Every control can create new hazards or shift risk elsewhere; re-assess after adding it.
Keep the links so an auditor can follow each risk control to the evidence that it works.

subs.why: After controls, you must judge whether the remaining risk - individually and overall - is outweighed by the device's benefit. This is the conclusion the whole file builds toward, and it must integrate safety, security and AI risk.
subs.what: Evaluate residual risk and the overall benefit-risk, integrating security and AI risk, and conclude in the risk report.

how.d: After its control, re-score severity x probability against the acceptability criteria.
Step back from risk-by-risk: do all the residual risks together still leave the device acceptable? Individually-acceptable risks can sum to an unacceptable whole.
Does the clinical benefit, in light of the state of the art, outweigh the overall residual risk? Document the reasoning.
Fold cybersecurity and (if applicable) AI/model risk into one coherent benefit-risk picture, not separate silos.
Ensure the ones users must be aware of are communicated in the IFU / labelling.

how.t: Conclude in the risk management report
how.d: Sign off that risk management is complete and the device is acceptable to release; this report anchors the technical file.

activities.statement: Analyse & evaluate risks; implement controls in priority order.
activities.why: This is the analytical heart of risk management. ISO 14971 requires you to identify hazards and foreseeable sequences of events, estimate and evaluate the associated risks against your acceptability criteria, and implement risk controls in the priority order: inherent safety by design first, then protective measures, then information for safety. Use-related hazards from formative usability and security threats feed in here. The risk management table/file is the living record a Notified Body reads closely.
activities.what: Identify hazards and hazardous situations, estimate and evaluate each risk against the risk acceptability criteria, and define and implement risk controls in the ISO 14971 priority order, recording it all in the risk management file and table.
activities.how:
1. Complete the Risk Management Plan first (RSK-TPL-01): criteria & matrix.
2. Start the risk table (RSK-TPL-03) with hazards/threats/AI risks.
3. Define how security (TIR57) and AI (Art 9) risk branch into the same file.

steps.d: Hazards, foreseeable event sequences, hazardous situations - incl. use and security.
Against acceptability criteria in the risk table.
Design-out, then protective measures, then information for safety.

tips: Apply the control hierarchy - design-out first, information-for-safety last; labelling is the weakest control and reviewers know it.
Every use error and security threat is a hazard input - pull them in, do not run parallel risk registers.
Keep the risk table living and traceable from hazard to control to verification.
Do not set acceptability criteria just to rubber-stamp what you already built.

lean.start: A risk table covering the main hazards with evaluated risks and design-first controls.
lean.evolve: Full hazard coverage with use and security inputs, traceable to control verification.
startDetail: Hazards and hazardous situations identified from the intended use, users and environment.
Each risk estimated (severity × probability) and evaluated against your acceptability criteria.
Risk controls chosen in the ISO 14971 priority order: inherently safe design → protective measures → information for safety.
Controls implemented and recorded in the risk file, with residual risk re-evaluated.
Use-related and (where relevant) security-related hazards included, not just hardware failures.
Any new risk introduced by a control captured and assessed.
evolveDetail: Comprehensive hazard analysis with structured methods (FMEA/FTA) for the worst cases.
Each control traced to the verification that proves it works.
Use-error and cybersecurity risk analyses integrated into the one risk file.

subs.why: You can only control risks you have identified.
subs.what: Enumerate hazards and the sequences that lead to harm.

how.d: By function, use, foreseeable misuse, security.
Formative usability and threat findings.

subs.why: Evaluation against criteria is what decides where control is needed.
subs.what: Estimate probability and severity and compare to acceptability.

how.d: Probability x severity per the plan.
Which risks need control.

subs.why: The ISO 14971 hierarchy produces genuinely safer devices, not just documentation.
subs.what: Apply inherent-safety, protective and information controls in order.

how.d: Eliminate the hazard where possible.
Warnings/IFU only when design can't.

subs.why: The file is the traceable evidence the NB assesses.
subs.what: Capture the analysis, evaluation and controls in the RM file/table.

how.d: Each risk linked to its control.
Controls verified as effective.

activities.statement: Conclude overall residual risk & benefit-risk in the RM report.
activities.why: The risk management report concludes the process: it reviews that the risk management plan was executed, that residual risks are acceptable individually and overall, and that the overall benefit-risk is favourable (ISO 14971 section 8). It is signed by management and feeds the CER/PER and the technical file. A missing or weak overall residual-risk evaluation is a common conformity finding.
activities.what: Produce the risk management report: confirm the plan was implemented, evaluate the overall residual risk and the overall benefit-risk, and conclude acceptability - feeding the CER/PER and the technical file.
activities.how:
1. Complete the Risk Management Plan first (RSK-TPL-01): criteria & matrix.
2. Start the risk table (RSK-TPL-03) with hazards/threats/AI risks.
3. Define how security (TIR57) and AI (Art 9) risk branch into the same file.

steps.d: All planned risk activities completed and documented.
The aggregate, not just each risk in isolation.
Overall benefit-risk favourable; management sign-off.

refs: s8 Overall residual risk evaluation & report

tips: Evaluate OVERALL residual risk, not just each risk in isolation - section 8 requires the aggregate view.
The report must conclude an acceptable overall benefit-risk and be signed off by management.
Keep it in sync with the CER/PER benefit-risk - the two must agree, or a reviewer will notice.
It is a living conclusion - post-market data can re-open it via post-market risk updates.

lean.start: An RM report concluding acceptable overall residual risk and benefit-risk, signed by management.
lean.evolve: Kept current from post-market data, consistent with the CER/PER.
startDetail: Confirmation that the risk-management plan was implemented as written.
The overall residual risk evaluated across all individual risks together, not just item-by-item.
The overall benefit-risk judged acceptable, with the rationale recorded.
Consistency checks against the CER/PER and the intended-purpose claims.
Sign-off by the responsible person/management before release.
evolveDetail: Scheduled RM-report updates fed by PMS and complaint data.
Benefit-risk re-assessed when new hazards or field data emerge.
Version alignment maintained across the risk file, CER/PER and technical file.

subs.why: The report must attest the process was actually followed.
subs.what: Verify all planned risk activities were completed.

how.d: Each activity done and recorded.
Anything outstanding flagged.

subs.why: Section 8 requires the aggregate judgement, the crux of the report.
subs.what: Assess the overall residual risk and overall benefit-risk.

how.d: Consider interactions, not just individually.
Benefits outweigh the overall residual risk.

subs.why: Management ownership of the risk conclusion is required and audited.
subs.what: Conclude acceptability and obtain sign-off.

how.d: Acceptable, feeding CER/PER.
Documented ownership.

activities.why: IVDs carry a particular risk: a wrong result leads to a wrong clinical decision. IVD risk management (ISO 14971 applied to diagnostics) makes you think through false positives and negatives and the harm they cause downstream, not just device failure.
activities.what: Set up IVD-specific risk management to ISO 14971: identify diagnostic hazards — chiefly incorrect (false positive/negative) or delayed results and the clinical decisions they drive — assess the resulting clinical consequence and its probability, and control them through assay design, software checks and information for the user, recorded in the IVD risk file.

steps.d: Take RSK-SOP-02 and agree your acceptability criteria for false results.
Use RSK-TPL-06 covering analytical errors, user errors and result-interpretation harm; define the controls.
Capture residual risk and benefit-risk in the report (RSK-TPL-07), linked to your performance evidence.

tips: For IVDs the dominant hazard is usually a wrong or late result, not the box breaking - your hazard analysis must reflect that.
Tie risk controls to your analytical and clinical performance data - the numbers justify acceptability.
Keep IVD risk and the main risk file consistent; one feeds the other.

lean.start: One risk table focused on incorrect/delayed results and their clinical harm, with the obvious controls.
lean.evolve: Add quantitative risk estimation, interference depth and live links to post-market performance.
startDetail: Diagnostic hazards framed as wrong-result modes: false positive, false negative, delayed or uninterpretable result.
The clinical consequence of each wrong result traced to the decision it drives (missed diagnosis, wrong treatment).
Risk estimated from the severity of that clinical consequence and the likelihood of the error.
Controls chosen design-first: assay/cut-off design, internal QC and flags, then information for the user (IFU limitations).
Interference and specimen-quality failure modes included as hazard sources.
evolveDetail: Quantitative error-rate estimates tied to analytical/clinical performance data.
Deeper interference, cross-reactivity and specimen-integrity hazard coverage.
Live links from post-market performance (PMPF) back into the risk file.

subs.why: For IVDs the dominant hazard is a wrong or late result - set acceptability criteria for false results first.
subs.what: Adapt RSK-SOP-02 and agree the acceptability criteria for false results.

how.t: Adapt the SOP to diagnostic risk
how.d: The dominant IVD hazard is a wrong or late result.

how.t: Set acceptability criteria for false results
how.d: For false positives and false negatives separately.
What a wrong result does to patient management.
Sensitivity/specificity targets back the criteria.

subs.t: Build the IVD risk table & controls
subs.why: A risk table covering analytical, user and interpretation harm, with controls, is the heart of IVD safety.
subs.what: Build the IVD risk table covering analytical, user and result-interpretation harm and define controls.

how.d: RSK-TPL-06.
Analytical error, user error, and result-interpretation error.
Design controls and information-for-safety (e.g. result caveats).
Confirm each reduces the risk as intended.

subs.why: Evaluating residual and benefit-risk against performance evidence is the conclusion of the IVD risk file.
subs.what: Capture residual risk and benefit-risk in the report, linked to performance evidence.

how.d: After controls, against the false-result criteria.
Diagnostic benefit vs the residual risk of wrong results.
The report references the analytical/clinical performance.

how.t: Keep consistent with the main risk file
how.d: One coherent picture.

subs.why: Each manufactured lot of an IVD can drift - production-risk control keeps lot-to-lot performance trustworthy.
subs.what: Adapt RSK-SOP-03 to your manufacturing, or record a justified non-application.

how.t: Adapt the SOP - or record N/A
how.d: If you do not manufacture, record a justified non-application.
The performance each manufactured lot must meet.
How you watch performance across lots.
Production drift is a production/post-production risk.

subs.why: Calibrator/control traceability and lot-release criteria are what keep each lot's results valid.
subs.what: Define calibrator/control value-assignment with metrological traceability and monitor lot performance.

how.d: Document the value-assignment chain for calibrators/controls (IVDR expectation).
Quality control that gates results.
Only release lots meeting per-lot performance.
A slow lot-to-lot shift is an early warning.

subs.why: Lot-release and monitoring records, with drift fed to CAPA, keep the process under control over time.
subs.what: Keep lot-release and monitoring records and route drift into CAPA.

how.d: And the monitoring data.
To see drift before it becomes failure.
Investigate and correct systemic shifts.
Field action may be needed if released lots are impacted.

activities.statement: Feed post-market data back into the risk file & benefit-risk.
activities.why: Risk management does not stop at market. ISO 14971 section 10 requires production and post-production information to be collected and fed back into the risk file: new hazards, changed probabilities, real-world severity. PMS, complaints, vigilance and PMCF/PMPF data can shift the benefit-risk - and if it turns unfavourable you must act (field action, CER/PER update, design change). This closes the loop the whole system depends on.
activities.what: Continuously feed post-market data (PMS reports, complaints, vigilance, PMCF/PMPF) back into the risk management file: re-assess hazards and residual risk, re-confirm benefit-risk, and trigger CAPA / field action / CER updates where the picture changes.
activities.how: [TO AUTHOR in Phase 2]

steps.d: PMS, complaints, vigilance, PMCF/PMPF and literature.
New/changed hazards, probabilities and severity in the risk file.
CAPA, CER/PER update, design change or field action.

tips: Post-market risk is a duty, not optional - ISO 14971 section 10 is explicitly checked in audits.
A benefit-risk that turns unfavourable obliges action (Art 10) - do not sit on it.
Connect PMS, vigilance and risk in one loop, not three silos.
Feed material changes back into the CER/PER and the design, not just the risk table.

lean.start: A routine review of complaints/PMS against the risk file, updating residual risk and benefit-risk.
lean.evolve: A closed loop where PMS/vigilance/PMCF systematically drive risk, CER and design updates.
startDetail: A scheduled review pulling PMS reports, complaints, vigilance and PMCF/PMPF into the risk process.
Hazards and probabilities re-assessed against what the field data actually shows.
Residual risk and overall benefit-risk re-confirmed (or challenged) with real numbers.
New hazards or higher-than-expected occurrence rates captured as new risk-file entries.
Triggers into CAPA, field action and CER/PER updates when the picture changes.
The risk file versioned so the change history is auditable.
evolveDetail: Automated signal detection feeding the risk file.
Benefit-risk re-assessed on a defined cadence and on trigger events.
Design improvements driven by post-market risk trends.

subs.why: You cannot update risk on data you never gathered.
subs.what: Pull PMS, complaints, vigilance and PMCF/PMPF data.

how.d: One view across the post-market inputs.
Anything not in the risk file.

subs.why: Real-world data changes probabilities and severities the file assumed.
subs.what: Update the risk analysis and benefit-risk conclusion.

how.d: Reflect real-world evidence.
Still acceptable, or not.

subs.why: An unfavourable change without action is a regulatory and safety failure.
subs.what: Raise CAPA, CER/PER updates or field action as needed.

how.d: Where safety is affected.
Evidence and claims kept current.

activities.statement: Plan the software lifecycle & assign the 62304 safety class.
activities.why: IEC 62304 starts with planning and, critically, software safety classification (Class A/B/C) based on the possible harm from a software failure. The class sets how much rigour the whole lifecycle needs. The plan defines the lifecycle model, activities, configuration management and problem resolution. Getting the safety class and plan right up front avoids under- or over-engineering the entire software effort.
activities.what: Establish the software development plan and the configuration-management and problem-resolution processes, and assign the IEC 62304 software safety class (A/B/C) that scales the rigour of the whole software lifecycle.
activities.how:
1. Map the core design-build-release process.
2. Prepare the D&D plan (DEV-TPL-07) with phases and review gates.
3. For software, prepare the development plan and safety class (SWD-TPL-01).

steps.d: A/B/C from the harm a software failure could cause.
Lifecycle model, activities, deliverables.

steps.t: Set up config mgmt & problem resolution
steps.d: The supporting processes 62304 requires.

tips: Classify honestly - the safety class (A/B/C) drives how much 62304 rigour applies to everything downstream.
A whole-system Class C can sometimes be reduced by segregating safety-critical items - architecture matters.
Set up configuration management and problem resolution now, not later.
The plan is the map a reviewer follows to check you did what you said.

lean.start: A software development plan with the IEC 62304 safety class assigned, plus basic configuration management and problem resolution. The safety class drives everything else.
lean.evolve: Add architecture/segregation analysis and a security plan as the software grows.
startDetail: The IEC 62304 software safety class (A/B/C) assigned and justified from the worst-case harm of a software failure.
A software development plan (SWD-PLAN-01): lifecycle model, activities, deliverables and responsibilities scaled to the class.
A configuration-management approach: version control, baselines, and how releases are identified.
A problem-resolution process: how anomalies are logged, triaged and closed.
The list of SOUP / third-party components you expect to use, captured for later evaluation.
The interface to the risk file noted, so software-contributed risks flow into risk management.
evolveDetail: Software architecture and segregation analysis to isolate high-class items and reduce the rigour needed elsewhere.
A security plan and secure-development practices (threat modelling, SBOM) integrated with the lifecycle.
Automated CI pipelines with traceable builds, tests and release evidence.

subs.why: The class scales the rigour of the entire lifecycle.
subs.what: Determine Class A/B/C from potential harm.

how.d: What a software failure could cause.
Architecture can lower the class of items.

subs.why: The plan is the lifecycle contract a reviewer checks against.
subs.what: Define the lifecycle model, activities and deliverables.

how.d: Model and phases.
Per the safety class.

subs.t: Set up config mgmt & problem resolution
subs.why: 62304 mandates these supporting processes.
subs.what: Establish configuration management and problem resolution.

how.d: Config management plan.
Problem resolution workflow.

activities.why: For software - SaMD or embedded - IEC 62304 defines the lifecycle that makes software safe: requirements, architecture, detailed design, implementation, integration and system testing, plus maintenance, configuration management and problem resolution, all scaled to the software safety class. Skipping these steps leaves software that cannot be shown to be safe or maintainable, and the technical file cannot stand.
activities.what: Run the software development lifecycle per IEC 62304 at the determined safety class: software requirements, architectural and detailed design, implementation with unit verification, integration and integration testing, system testing, and the supporting processes - configuration management, problem resolution and software risk management - with the records each produces.
activities.how:
1. Write the SRS (SWD-TPL-03) and architecture (SWD-TPL-04); identify SOUP.
2. Verify (SWD-TPL-11/12) and validate (SWD-TPL-13/14).

steps.d: confirm the IEC 62304 software safety class set in P1.7 (A/B/C). It scales everything below - higher class, more rigour. Document the rationale.
derive software requirements from the system requirements and the risk controls, and check they're complete and testable (5.2).
define the software architecture and detailed design, and identify your SOUP (off-the-shelf / open-source components) with their versions and constraints (5.3-5.4).
keep a list of every third-party/open-source component, its version and its known issues - this is the seed of your SBOM and a frequent audit focus.
build the software and verify units against the detailed design with documented acceptance criteria (5.5).
integrate the parts, run integration and system testing against the requirements, and record results and every anomaly (5.6-5.7).
control versions of code and documents so you always know exactly what's in any release - version control with discipline, not just a repo.
log, evaluate and resolve software anomalies through a defined process, feeding safety-relevant ones back to risk.
maintain traceability from each requirement to its test, so you can show every requirement is verified.

steps.t: Software requirements trace to architecture and to tests.
The SOUP / third-party component list is maintained with versions.
Software is assigned a safety class with documentation scaled to it.

records: Software requirements, architecture and design documents
Unit, integration and system test records
tips: Your safety class (A/B/C) is the most consequential decision in software work - it sets how much rigour everything needs. Classify honestly; auditors challenge optimistic low classes.
SOUP/third-party components are where audits dig. Know every dependency, its version and its vulnerabilities - 'we use some open-source libraries' is not an acceptable answer.
IEC 62304 demands a controlled, documented process - it does NOT forbid agile. You can be agile AND compliant (see AAMI TIR45); you just have to evidence the activities.
Anomalies you found and fixed are evidence of a healthy process, not something to hide. An empty bug log looks more suspicious to an auditor than a managed one.

lean.start: A requirements → test trace, a one-page architecture note with your SOUP list, and a tracked issue log — scaled to your safety class.
lean.evolve: Add formal configuration management, automated CI/test pipelines and deeper documentation as the software grows.
startDetail: Software requirements written to be testable and traceable back to system/design inputs.
A software architecture note showing the main components and their interfaces.
A SOUP / third-party inventory with the version and intended function of each item.
Implementation with unit verification on the safety-relevant units.
Integration and system testing planned against the requirements.
Supporting processes running — configuration management, problem resolution and software risk management — each producing records.
evolveDetail: Formal configuration management with baselines and controlled releases.
Automated CI with traceable test execution and coverage.
Detailed design documentation and interface specs as complexity rises.

subs.why: Software requirements translate the device's needs into what the software must do - including the risk controls implemented in software. They are the spec your tests will check against.
subs.what: Define and review the software requirements, including functional, performance and risk-control requirements, traced to system level.

how.d: Including the risk controls that the software must implement.
What it must do, how well, and its safety-related behaviour.
So it can be verified objectively later.
Link each software requirement to its system requirement and forward to its test.

subs.why: The architecture shows how the software is structured and where the third-party (SOUP) components sit - which carry their own risks and must be tracked and assessed.
subs.what: Define the software architecture and identify and manage SOUP (software of unknown provenance) with versions and risk.

how.d: Components, their responsibilities and interfaces, scaled to the safety class.

how.t: Segregate by risk where it helps
how.d: Isolate safety-critical components so a failure elsewhere cannot defeat them (class C).
Every third-party / off-the-shelf component, with version and supplier.
What you require each SOUP to do, and the safety impact of its known anomalies.
How you will track new vulnerabilities/anomalies over the product's life.

subs.why: Disciplined implementation with unit verification catches defects at the cheapest possible point and builds the evidence trail IEC 62304 expects.
subs.what: Implement the software to the design and verify units against acceptance criteria, scaled to the safety class.

how.d: Follow the design and your coding standards.
Test units to a depth that scales with the safety class.
Peer or static review for safety-critical units.
Version control with traceable commits tied to requirements/anomalies.

subs.why: Integration and system testing prove the pieces work together and that the software meets its requirements - the evidence that the software is safe to release.
subs.what: Integrate the software and run integration and system tests against the requirements, recording results.

how.d: Confirm the pieces work together as the architecture intends.
Verify the software meets every software requirement.
Specifically test the software risk-control measures.
Every result traced back to the requirement it proves.

subs.why: Configuration management and a controlled release mean you always know exactly what you shipped, can reproduce it, and can manage the known anomalies you release with.

The evidence that the software was developed and verified per the plan.

activities.statement: Implement the software with reviews under the IEC 62304 lifecycle.
activities.why: Implementation is where the software architecture becomes code under the IEC 62304 lifecycle. For anything above Class A, 62304 requires defined implementation with unit verification, code and design reviews and configuration control, all traceable to the software requirements and architecture. Disciplined reviews here catch defects - and safety issues - far more cheaply than in verification or in the field.
activities.what: Implement the software to the architecture under configuration control, with code reviews and software design reviews traceable to requirements, capturing review records and routing every defect through problem resolution.
activities.how: [TO AUTHOR in Phase 2]

steps.d: Code under configuration control, traceable to requirements and design.
Code and design reviews scaled to Class B/C rigour.
No silent fixes; anomalies logged and assessed.

tips: Keep implementation traceable to requirements and architecture - 62304 is about the chain, not just the code.
Review to the safety class - Class C needs more rigour than Class B; do not one-size-fit-all.
Route every defect through problem resolution - silent fixes break traceability and audit trust.
Secure-coding practices (81001-5-1) belong here, not bolted on at release.

lean.start: Configuration-controlled code with reviews on the safety-relevant modules, traceable to requirements.
lean.evolve: Full review coverage, unit verification and integration testing across the software system.
startDetail: Code held under version control with identifiable baselines.
Code and software-design reviews on the safety-relevant modules, with review records.
Implementation traceable to the architecture and requirements.
Every defect routed through the problem-resolution process, not fixed silently.
Coding / secure-coding standards applied to the extent the safety class warrants.
evolveDetail: Review and unit-test coverage extended across the whole codebase.
Continuous integration running the test suite on every change.
Static analysis and security scanning wired into the pipeline.

subs.why: Uncontrolled code cannot be traced, verified or reproduced.
subs.what: Write the software to the architecture with version control.

how.d: Each unit maps to a requirement/design element.
Everything in version control.

subs.why: Reviews are the cheapest place to catch safety and quality defects.
subs.what: Conduct code and software design reviews to the safety class.

how.d: More review for higher 62304 class.
Checklists and findings captured.

subs.why: 62304 requires anomalies to be tracked and assessed, not quietly patched.
subs.what: Log, assess and resolve defects through the problem-resolution process.

how.d: Nothing fixed off the record.
Each defect evaluated for risk.

activities.statement: Verify & validate the software; compile the software file.
activities.why: Software verification (unit, integration and system testing) confirms the software meets its requirements; software validation confirms it meets user needs in the intended environment. IEC 62304 s5.5-s5.7 require planned testing traceable to requirements, with results and anomalies recorded. This is where defects are caught before release, and it is the evidence a reviewer traces requirement by requirement.
activities.what: Plan and execute software verification (unit/integration/system testing) and software validation against requirements and intended use, record the results and anomalies traceable to requirements, and compile the software file.
activities.how:
1. Write the SRS (SWD-TPL-03) and architecture (SWD-TPL-04); identify SOUP.
2. Verify (SWD-TPL-11/12) and validate (SWD-TPL-13/14).
activities.documents: SWD-TPL-05; SWD-TPL-11; SWD-TPL-12; SWD-TPL-13; SWD-TPL-14; SWD-LIS-01

steps.d: Test protocols traceable to requirements and intended use.
Unit, integration, system testing; validation against use.
Results, anomalies and traceability; compile the software file.

tips: Keep verification traceable to requirements - the traceability list is the evidence, not the test count.
Separate verification (meets requirements) from validation (meets user needs) - 62304 and 13485 both distinguish them.
Record anomalies and their disposition - an untracked bug is an audit finding.
Automate regression where you can - every update re-opens verification.

lean.start: System testing traceable to the key requirements plus validation against intended use, with anomalies logged.
lean.evolve: Full unit/integration/system verification traceability and automated regression.
startDetail: A verification plan spanning unit, integration and system testing, scaled to the safety class.
Tests executed and results recorded, each traceable to the requirement it checks.
Software validation against requirements and intended use (does it do the clinical job).
Anomalies logged, assessed for risk, and either fixed or justified.
The software file compiled: plan, requirements, architecture, tests, anomalies, release.
evolveDetail: Complete traceability from every requirement to its verifying test.
Automated regression suites so re-verification is fast and repeatable.
Coverage metrics tracked and gated at release.

subs.why: Untraceable testing is not evidence.
subs.what: Define test protocols mapped to requirements and use.

how.d: Each test to a requirement.
Validation scenarios.

subs.why: Layered testing catches defects at the right level.
subs.what: Execute the verification testing.

how.d: Unit, integration, system.
Pass/fail per test.

subs.why: Meeting requirements is not the same as meeting user needs.
subs.what: Validate the software in the intended environment.

how.d: Intended-use conditions.
Summative usability where relevant.

subs.why: The software file is the traceable evidence set.
subs.what: Disposition anomalies and assemble the software file.

how.d: Fixed or risk-assessed.

activities.statement: Release the software with anomalies assessed + cybersecurity docs.
activities.why: Software release under IEC 62304 5.8 requires that verification is complete, known anomalies are documented and their risk assessed, and the released version is under configuration control with an archived, reproducible build. Cybersecurity documentation (IEC 81001-5-1, MDCG 2019-16) - SBOM, threat model, security testing - is now expected in the technical file for connected and software devices. The software full report consolidates the lifecycle evidence.
activities.what: Release the software per 62304 5.8 (verification complete, residual anomalies assessed, version archived and controlled), and compile the cybersecurity documentation (SBOM, threat model, security testing) and the consolidated software report for the technical file.
activities.how: [TO AUTHOR in Phase 2]

steps.d: Verification done, known anomalies documented and risk-assessed.
Reproducible, configuration-controlled released version.
SBOM, threat model, security testing per MDCG 2019-16.

tips: Document known anomalies and their risk assessment at release - 62304 requires it, and hiding them fails audit.
Ship an SBOM - it is expected under MDCG 2019-16 and FDA, and you need it to respond to new vulnerabilities.
Archive the exact released build so it is reproducible - the release record must point to it.
Cybersecurity is a lifecycle, not a release-day checkbox - the threat model should have driven the design.

lean.start: A release record with verification complete and known anomalies assessed, plus a basic SBOM and threat model.
lean.evolve: Full cybersecurity documentation and a consolidated software report maintained across versions.
startDetail: A release record per IEC 62304 §5.8: verification complete, version archived and controlled.
Residual/known anomalies listed and each assessed for safety and security impact.
A software bill of materials (SBOM) listing components and versions.
A threat model and the security testing performed (at least the basics).
A consolidated software report pulled together for the technical file.
