A coordinated vulnerability-disclosure and patch process defined and run.
Release evidence and SBOM regenerated and archived per version.

subs.why: Releasing with unassessed anomalies is a safety and compliance risk.
subs.what: Confirm verification complete and residual anomalies documented + assessed.

how.d: All planned verification done.
Risk of each residual anomaly evaluated.

subs.why: You must be able to reproduce exactly what shipped.
subs.what: Archive the configuration-controlled release under version control.

how.d: Exact build identifier.
Build reproducible from the archive.

subs.why: Connected/software devices are expected to evidence security in the technical file.
subs.what: Produce SBOM, threat model and security testing evidence.

how.d: Components and versions listed.
Threats, controls and test results.

subs.why: The technical file needs a single coherent view of the software lifecycle.
subs.what: Compile the software full report from the lifecycle evidence.

how.d: Plan through release, traceable.
Verification, anomalies, security.

activities.statement: Describe the machine-learning model, data and controls.
activities.why: For ML-driven software, regulators and the EU AI Act expect a clear description of the machine-learning component: the model, its training/validation data and provenance, the learning approach, performance and limitations, and how it is controlled and monitored. It links the software lifecycle to the AI/ML lifecycle (AIM) and underpins analytical performance and transparency claims.
activities.what: Produce the machine-learning description: model architecture and intended function, training/validation/test data and provenance, performance and limitations, and the controls (versioning, monitoring, predetermined change control) that keep it safe.
activities.how: [TO AUTHOR in Phase 2]

steps.d: Architecture, intended function, datasets and provenance.
Metrics, operating envelope, known limitations and bias considerations.
Model/version control, monitoring, PCCP if the model updates.

refs: AI management system - link to AIM lifecycle

tips: Describe data provenance and representativeness - it is the first thing scrutinised for bias.
Separate the model version from the software version - both are controlled independently.
State limitations and the operating envelope honestly; an overclaimed model fails in the field and in review.
Link to the PCCP if the model will be updated - predetermined change control avoids re-certifying every retrain.

lean.start: A description of the model, its data and provenance, performance and limitations on a version-controlled model.
lean.evolve: Add monitoring, bias evaluation and a PCCP, and integrate with the AI/ML lifecycle governance.
startDetail: The model architecture and its intended function within the device.
Training / validation / test data described with provenance and representativeness.
Performance metrics and the conditions under which they hold — plus stated limitations.
Version control over the model artefact, data and code so a result is reproducible.
The controls that keep it safe: monitoring hooks and the change-control approach.
evolveDetail: Sub-group and bias evaluation with documented mitigations.
Production monitoring for drift with defined thresholds and responses.
A predetermined change-control plan (PCCP) governing retraining, tied to the AI/ML lifecycle.

subs.t: Describe the model and its data
subs.why: Without data provenance the model cannot be assessed for bias or validity.
subs.what: Document the architecture, intended function and datasets with provenance.

how.d: What the model outputs and how it is used.
Sources, representativeness, splits.

subs.why: Honest limits define the safe operating envelope.
subs.what: Report performance metrics and known limitations.

how.d: Against a defined reference.
Where the model is/ isn't valid.

subs.why: An uncontrolled model drifts and cannot be trusted over time.
subs.what: Establish versioning, monitoring and change control.

how.d: Independent of the software version.
Predetermined change control where used.

activities.statement: Meet the DiGA fast-track requirements (German market).
activities.why: DiGA (Digitale Gesundheitsanwendungen) is the German fast-track for reimbursable digital health applications, run by BfArM under the DVG and DiGAV. Beyond CE marking (class I / IIa), it demands specific requirements: data protection and security (including a security assessment), interoperability, usability, and evidence of a positive healthcare effect. It is a distinct, additional pathway for the German market, not a replacement for MDR conformity.
activities.what: Assess and document the DiGA requirements per DiGAV / BfArM: the CE baseline plus data protection & security, interoperability, usability, and the positive-healthcare-effect evidence needed for the DiGA directory listing.
activities.how: [TO AUTHOR in Phase 2]

steps.d: Data protection/security, interoperability, usability against the BfArM catalogue.
Study demonstrating the positive healthcare effect.
Provisional or full listing with the required evidence.

tips: DiGA is on top of CE marking, not instead of it - you still need MDR conformity first.
The positive-healthcare-effect study is the hard part - plan and resource it like a clinical study.
BfArM's security and interoperability requirements are specific and audited - map them explicitly, not generically.
Provisional listing (with a trial period) is an option if the positive-effect study isn't complete - use it strategically.

lean.start: A DiGAV requirements checklist mapping data-protection, security, interoperability and usability to your evidence.
lean.evolve: Complete the positive-effect study and move from provisional to permanent directory listing.
startDetail: A DiGAV / BfArM requirements checklist covering the CE baseline plus the DiGA-specific criteria.
Data-protection and security requirements mapped to your evidence (GDPR, BSI, C5 as applicable).
Interoperability requirements addressed (standard data formats / interfaces).
Usability evidence aligned to the DiGA expectations.
The positive-healthcare-effect study designed or scoped for the directory listing.
evolveDetail: A completed comparative study demonstrating the positive healthcare effect.
The move from provisional (fast-track) to permanent BfArM directory listing.
Ongoing security and data-protection re-assessment to keep the listing.

subs.why: DiGA has a specific, audited requirements catalogue beyond CE.
subs.what: Assess data protection/security, interoperability and usability against DiGAV.

how.d: Each requirement mapped to evidence.
Required for listing.

subs.why: A positive healthcare effect is the core of the DiGA case.
subs.what: Design the study demonstrating the positive healthcare effect.

how.d: Medical or patient-relevant structural/procedural.
Comparative, adequately powered.

subs.why: Listing in the DiGA directory is what unlocks reimbursement.
subs.what: Compile the application for provisional or full listing.

CE, security, effect evidence.

activities.why: Most device incidents are use errors, not component failures, so IEC 62366-1 requires a usability engineering process that designs the user interface around the users, use environment and tasks and proves, through formative and summative evaluation, that the device can be used safely. Use-related risk feeds the ISO 14971 risk file. Skipping usability engineering is a common Notified Body finding and a real patient-safety gap.
activities.what: Run the usability engineering process: a use specification (users, use environment, tasks), a user-interface specification, identification of use-related hazards, formative evaluation through development, and a summative (validation) evaluation demonstrating safe use - documented in a usability engineering file linked to risk management.
activities.how:
1. Write the use specification & identify use-related hazards (HUF-TPL-01).
2. Plan and run formative then summative evaluation (HUF-TPL-02/03/04).

steps.d: write down who really uses the device (clinicians? patients? in a panic? with gloves? poor eyesight?), where, and to do what. Real users, not idealised ones.
identify the tasks where a use error could cause harm (wrong dose, misread result). These are what you must prove can be done safely.
capture the users, use environments and tasks in a use specification - the foundation of the whole usability file.
define the UI (screens, alarms, labelling, physical controls) against those tasks and users.
run small, informal formative tests during development and fix the use problems you find - cheap now, expensive after launch.
every use-related hazard you discover goes into the ISO 14971 risk file with a control - usability and risk are joined at the hip.
with representative users, run the formal summative (validation) evaluation proving the safety-related tasks can be done safely. This is the evidence the Notified Body wants.
pull it together into the usability engineering file - the single place that proves your device is safe to use.

steps.t: A use specification identifies users, use environments and safety-related tasks.
A summative usability validation is planned or performed against the use spec.

tips: Most device harm comes from use error, not broken parts. Usability engineering is patient-safety work, not a UX nicety - and skipping it is a very common Notified Body finding.
Test with REAL representative users, not your own engineers. Your team knows the device too well to use it like a stressed first-timer would.
Formative testing during development is where you find the cheap fixes. Discovering a fatal use problem in the summative study (or in the field) is the expensive way to learn it.
Usability and risk share the same use-error hazards. If your usability file and risk file don't reference each other, an auditor will notice the disconnect.

lean.start: A short use specification, the safety-related tasks listed, and a few informal formative tests.
lean.evolve: Add a formal user-interface specification and a full summative validation study as the device nears market.
startDetail: A use specification: the intended users, the use environment(s), and the tasks they perform.
The safety-related (hazard-related) tasks identified — the ones where a use error could cause harm.
A first user-interface description (screens, controls, labels, alarms).
Use-related hazards captured and routed into the risk file.
A couple of informal formative tests with representative users to find early problems.
A usability engineering file started and linked to risk management.
evolveDetail: A formal UI specification under design control.
Structured formative rounds until the interface is ready for summative validation.
A summative validation study demonstrating safe use on the frozen design.

subs.why: Usability engineering exists because use error harms patients as surely as a component failure. The use specification - who uses it, for what, where - is the foundation everything else builds on.
subs.what: Define the use specification: intended users, uses, use environments and operating principle.

how.d: Professionals, patients, lay carers - their characteristics, training and limitations.
The tasks each user performs with the device.
Where it is used (clinic, home, ambulance) and the conditions there.
How the device is meant to be operated to achieve its purpose.

subs.why: Finding the tasks where a use error could cause harm focuses your design and testing on what actually matters for safety.
subs.what: Identify hazard-related use scenarios and the user tasks that are safety-critical.

how.d: Where a user could make a harmful mistake in each task.
The use sequences that could lead to harm.
The tasks that must not go wrong - your test priorities.
Feed these into risk management as hazardous situations.

subs.why: Formative testing during design finds and fixes usability problems early and cheaply, before they are baked into the product.
subs.what: Run formative usability evaluations during design and feed findings back into the design.

how.d: A few people matching each real user group - not colleagues.

how.t: Test realistic tasks on early prototypes
how.d: Click-throughs or early hardware; script the safety-related tasks.
Watch silently; note every hesitation, error and near-miss - the urge to help hides real problems.
For each problem, capture what happened and why (label, layout, workflow).
Formative testing is a loop - change the interface, then run it again.

subs.why: The summative study is the formal evidence that the finished design is safe to use - that representative users can perform the safety-critical tasks without harmful error.
subs.what: Run a summative usability validation on the final design with representative users, demonstrating safe use.

how.t: Use the final design & real users
how.d: Production-equivalent device, representative users, realistic environment.
Confirm users perform the critical tasks without harmful error.
Investigate root cause; confirm the residual use-risk is acceptable.
The formal evidence of safe use for the technical file.

activities.statement: Run iterative formative usability evaluations that feed risk & design.
activities.why: Formative evaluation is the iterative, in-design usability work: you test the evolving user interface with representative users to find and fix use problems before design freeze. Its purpose is improvement, not proof. Crucially, use errors and hazard-related use scenarios found here feed the risk analysis (the IEC 62366-1 / ISO 14971 interface) and drive design changes - doing this late means expensive rework or an unsafe device.
activities.what: Run iterative formative evaluations against the use specification, capture use problems and use-related hazards in the usability engineering file, route them to risk analysis and design, and iterate until the interface is ready for summative validation.
activities.how: [TO AUTHOR in Phase 2]

steps.d: Representative users, realistic tasks, on prototypes as the design matures.
Record use errors and hazard-related scenarios in the UEF.
Route findings to the risk file and design changes; repeat.

tips: Formative is for finding problems - recruit real representative users, not colleagues who already know the device.
Every use error is a risk input - route it to the risk file; formative usability and risk management are the same conversation.
Iterate early and often - fixing a use problem before freeze is far cheaper than after.
Formative is not summative - do not try to 'pass' it; its value is the problems it surfaces.

lean.start: A few formative sessions with representative users on the risky tasks, feeding fixes into design and risk.
lean.evolve: Structured, repeated formative rounds across the UI until it is ready for summative validation.
startDetail: Formative sessions run against the use specification with representative users.
Use problems and use-related hazards captured in the usability engineering file.
Findings routed to design (fix the UI) and to risk analysis (new/changed hazards).
The hazard-related tasks prioritised over cosmetic issues.
Iteration until the interface is stable enough for summative validation.
evolveDetail: Formative testing across all task flows, not just the riskiest.
A documented iteration history showing problems found and resolved.
Exit criteria defined for moving to summative validation.

subs.t: Plan formative rounds against the use spec
subs.why: Targeting the hazard-related tasks makes formative testing efficient and safety-relevant.
subs.what: Decide which tasks and users each formative round covers.

how.d: Tasks whose use errors could cause harm.
Match the intended user profiles.

subs.why: The findings are only useful if captured and traceable.
subs.what: Conduct the sessions and record use problems and hazard-related scenarios.

how.d: Realistic tasks, minimal prompting.
Each finding recorded and classified.

subs.why: Formative earns its cost by improving the design and the risk file before freeze.
subs.what: Route findings to risk analysis and design changes, then repeat.

how.d: Use errors become risk inputs/controls.
Mitigate by design first, labelling/training last.

activities.statement: Run summative usability validation on the frozen design.
activities.why: Summative (validation) evaluation is the final human-factors validation of the user interface against the use specification, on the (near-)final design, demonstrating that representative users can use the device safely in representative conditions. It is part of design validation and sits alongside the clinical study on the frozen design. For a user interface of unknown provenance (UOUP), IEC 62366-1 provides a specific evaluation route.
activities.what: Plan and run the summative usability validation on the frozen design with representative users and realistic use scenarios covering the hazard-related tasks; conclude whether safe use is demonstrated; and where a UI of unknown origin is involved, document the UOUP evaluation.
activities.how: [TO AUTHOR in Phase 2]

steps.d: Hazard-related tasks from risk, representative users, realistic conditions, pass criteria.
Run the study; observe safe/effective use of critical tasks.
Usability Evaluation Summary Report; UOUP evaluation if applicable.
No UI changes after summative starts.
From the risk analysis, not a generic walkthrough.

records: UEF for UI of Unknown Origin (UOUP) - if applicable
tips: Summative runs on the frozen design - a UI change afterwards can force a full re-run, so freeze first.
Cover the hazard-related use scenarios from your risk analysis, not a generic feature walkthrough.
A failed critical task is a design problem, not a training fix - resist the urge to 'solve' it in the IFU.
Time summative usability with the clinical study - both validate the finished device on the frozen design.

lean.start: A summative study on the frozen design covering the critical, hazard-related tasks with representative users.
lean.evolve: Broaden scenarios/users and integrate the residual use-risk conclusion into the risk file and CER/PER.
startDetail: A summative validation plan targeting the hazard-related tasks and realistic use scenarios.
Representative users from each distinct user group, on the frozen design.
Test conditions that reflect the real use environment.
Use errors and difficulties recorded and analysed for residual risk.
A clear conclusion on whether safe use is demonstrated.
A UOUP evaluation where a user interface of unknown provenance is involved.
evolveDetail: Wider scenario and user-group coverage.
Residual use-risk conclusions folded into the risk file and CER/PER.
Post-market usability signals fed back into the usability file.

subs.why: A summative study is only valid if it targets the hazard-related tasks with the right users under realistic conditions.
subs.what: Define the critical tasks, users, conditions and pass criteria.

how.d: Hazard-related use scenarios drive the task list.
What safe use looks like, pre-specified.

subs.why: Only the marketed design's usability can be claimed.
subs.what: Run the study with representative users on the final UI.

how.d: No changes during the study.
Safe and effective completion.

subs.why: The conclusion is the validation evidence; legacy UI needs the specific UOUP route.
subs.what: Write the summary report and, where relevant, the UOUP evaluation.

how.d: Against pass criteria, with residual use-risk stated.
Follow the IEC 62366-1 UOUP path.

activities.why: An AI/ML component adds obligations on top of the device rules: under the EU AI Act a medical-device AI is typically high-risk, requiring a risk-management system, data and data governance, technical documentation, transparency, human oversight and demonstrated accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity. ISO/IEC 42001 provides the AI management system to govern it. Treating AI like ordinary software misses these duties and the model-specific risks of drift, bias and opacity.
activities.what: Run the AI/ML lifecycle: data governance (relevant, representative, quality-checked data), model development and validation, performance and robustness evaluation, logging and record-keeping, transparency and human-oversight measures, and post-market model monitoring for drift - integrated with the risk file and technical documentation.
activities.how:
1. Govern training/validation/test data and examine bias (AIM-TPL-02).
2. Validate model performance and subgroups (AIM-TPL-03).

steps.d: a medical-device AI is almost always 'high-risk' under the EU AI Act - confirm it, because that triggers the full set of obligations below.
make sure training, validation and test data is relevant, representative of the intended population and quality-checked. Document where it came from and its limitations (Art 10).
actively check for bias across the populations the device will serve, and document what you found and did. Unexamined bias is an ethical AND regulatory failure.
develop the model and validate its performance against defined metrics, including how it holds up across sub-groups (robustness, accuracy), not just on average (Art 15).
design how a human stays meaningfully in control - can review, override or stop the AI (Art 14). 'The AI decides' is not acceptable for high-risk medical AI.
provide the information and instructions that let users understand what the AI does, its limits, and how to use it correctly (Art 13).
build automatic logging/record-keeping so the AI's operation is traceable (Art 12) - essential for investigation and post-market monitoring.
set up post-market model monitoring and a Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP) for how you'll update the model in a controlled way.

steps.t: Fold AI risk into the main file
steps.d: add AI-specific risks (drift, bias, opacity, automation bias) into the integrated risk file (P2.1) and the technical documentation.

steps.t: Training and validation data governance is documented.
Model performance is reported across the relevant sub-groups.
Human oversight and an override mechanism are designed in.

records: Model validation and performance / robustness reports
tips: Treating medical AI as 'just software' misses a whole regulation. Under the EU AI Act it's high-risk, with duties on data, transparency, oversight and robustness ordinary software doesn't have.
Bias isn't optional to check. If your model performs worse for some patient groups and you didn't look, that's an ethical and regulatory failure - and increasingly, a headline.
Plan how you'll update the model BEFORE you launch. A Predetermined Change Control Plan lets you improve the model without a new submission each time - design it in.
'The AI recommended it' is not a defence. Build genuine human oversight - a person who can understand, question and override the AI - and prove it.

lean.start: Documented data governance, a validation report with sub-group performance, and a clear human-oversight design — the AI Act high-risk essentials, done at pilot scale.
lean.evolve: Add an AI management system (ISO/IEC 42001), drift monitoring and a predetermined change-control plan as the model evolves.
startDetail: A data-governance record: data sources, relevance/representativeness, quality checks, and how bias was examined.
A model card / validation report stating the metrics, the test data, and performance broken down by relevant sub-groups.
A human-oversight design: where a person can review, override or stop the model, and what they see to do so.
The model's intended use and known limitations written down (inputs, operating range, failure modes).
Logging designed in: what the system records at inference so behaviour can be audited later.
The interface to the device risk file and technical documentation, treating model risks (drift, bias, opacity) explicitly.
evolveDetail: An ISO/IEC 42001 AI management system governing the model lifecycle across products.
Live drift and performance monitoring in production with alert thresholds.
A predetermined change-control plan (PCCP) so retraining stays inside an agreed envelope.

subs.why: An AI medical device is only as trustworthy as the data behind it. Documented data governance - provenance, quality, representativeness, bias - is the foundation of a defensible model.
subs.what: Document the governance of your training, validation and test data: provenance, quality, representativeness and bias management.

how.d: Where each dataset came from, and your right to use it.
Completeness, accuracy and labelling quality of the training/validation/test sets.
Does the data cover your intended patient population and use conditions?
Find dataset bias (demographic, acquisition) and reduce it.
Keep the test set genuinely held out to avoid leakage.

subs.why: The model's performance must be proven - and proven across the sub-groups it will serve, not just on average. Hidden sub-group failure is the classic AI safety trap.
subs.what: Train the model and validate its performance, including across relevant sub-groups, against pre-set acceptance criteria.

how.d: Acceptance criteria before you measure - tied to the clinical need.

how.t: Validate on the held-out test set

activities.why: For an AI medical device, the EU AI Act adds obligations that culminate near market entry: record-keeping and logging (Art 12), transparency and instructions for use (Art 13), human-oversight design (Art 14), and registration plus the Annex IV technical documentation. activities.why: Getting software safely from 'done' to 'in the field' - and keeping it safe through updates - is its own controlled process. IEC 62304 covers release, deployment and maintenance so that what users run is the version you verified, and updates do not introduce new risk.

activities.statement: Clinical evaluation plan + state of the art.
activities.why: A device may only be placed on the market if its clinical (MDR) or performance (IVDR) evidence shows it meets the safety and performance requirements for its intended purpose. MDR Art 61 and Annex XIV require a clinical evaluation; IVDR Annex XIII requires a performance evaluation (scientific validity, analytical and clinical performance). This evidence is the heart of the technical file and the most scrutinised part of conformity assessment.
activities.what: Finalise the clinical evaluation (MDR) or performance evaluation (IVDR): the evaluation plan, the appraisal of clinical / scientific literature and any investigations or performance studies, the demonstration of safety and performance and an acceptable benefit-risk, and the evaluation report that feeds the technical file and PMS/PMCF.
activities.how:
1. Complete the CER (CLE) or PER (PEV) with conclusions traceable to data.
2. Produce the SSCP/SSP.

steps.d: write the clinical (MDR) or performance (IVDR) evaluation plan: your intended purpose, the safety and performance claims you must back up, and HOW you'll get evidence.
decide whether you'll rely on published literature, equivalence to an existing device, your own clinical/performance data, or a mix - and be honest about what's actually available.
do a documented, reproducible literature search (a method like PICO) - not a quick Google. The Notified Body checks your search was thorough and unbiased.
critically appraise each source for relevance and quality against your claims. Cherry-picking favourable papers is exactly what reviewers look for.

steps.t: Run a study if there is a gap
steps.d: if literature and equivalence don't cover your claims, plan and run a clinical investigation (ISO 14155) or performance study to fill the gap.
pull the evidence together to show the device meets the safety and performance requirements and that its benefits outweigh its residual risks for the intended purpose.
compile the Clinical (or Performance) Evaluation Report - the centrepiece of your technical file and the most scrutinised document you'll write.
connect the evaluation to post-market surveillance and PMCF/PMPF so it's kept alive with real-world data, not frozen at submission.

steps.t: A clinical/performance evaluation plan defines the route and acceptance criteria.
A documented, reproducible literature search and appraisal exists.
Clinical evidence supports each claim and the intended purpose.

tips: The clinical/performance evaluation is the single most scrutinised part of MDR/IVDR conformity assessment. Thin or biased evidence here is the most common reason devices fail review.
Your literature search must be systematic and reproducible. A reviewer who can't follow how you searched - or spots you skipped unfavourable results - will reject the whole evaluation.
Equivalence is much harder under MDR than it used to be. Claiming equivalence to another company's device, without access to its technical data, rarely survives - plan for your own evidence.
The CER is never 'finished' - it's a living document fed by PMS and PMCF. Build the update loop now, because the Notified Body checks it's maintained.

activities.statement: Build the clinical evidence base: systematic literature & equivalence.
activities.why: Under MDR most of the clinical evidence base is built from a systematic literature review and, where claimed, a demonstration of equivalence. The search must be reproducible and unbiased; equivalence under MDR (technical, biological and clinical characteristics) is much harder than before and rarely survives without access to the comparator's technical documentation (MDCG 2020-5). Reviewer independence must be documented.
activities.what: Conduct and document the systematic literature search and appraisal, and where equivalence is claimed demonstrate it across the technical, biological and clinical characteristics with justification; record the appraisers' declarations of interest.

activities.statement: Conduct a clinical investigation to ISO 14155 (where required).
activities.why: Where literature and equivalence cannot demonstrate conformity - typically for implantable and class III devices, or novel technology - you conduct a clinical investigation to ISO 14155: the pivotal validation of clinical safety and performance in humans. It requires competent-authority and ethics approval, a clinical investigation plan, an investigator's brochure, informed consent, GCP-compliant conduct and a report. It is the most regulated and resource-heavy evidence activity, and it is conditional - a feasibility/pilot investigation usually precedes the pivotal one.
activities.what: Where required, design and conduct a clinical investigation to ISO 14155 and MDR Annex XV: the CIP, investigator's brochure and informed consent; obtain approvals; conduct to GCP; and produce the clinical investigation report that feeds the CER.

tips: This is conditional and staged - a feasibility/pilot investigation normally precedes the pivotal study; do not jump straight to pivotal.

Design endpoints to your intended-purpose claims, not to what is easy to measure - the CIR must support the CER's conclusions.
The clinical investigation report must be signed off and feed the CER - plan the whole timeline back from your submission date.

activities.statement: Compile the Clinical Evaluation Report and, where required, the SSCP.
activities.why: Under MDR Art 61 and Annex XIV Part A the Clinical Evaluation Report (CER) draws the whole clinical evaluation into a single, reasoned demonstration that the device meets its safety and performance requirements with an acceptable benefit-risk profile for its intended purpose. It is the most heavily scrutinised document in conformity assessment. For implantable and class III devices, Art 32 additionally requires a Summary of Safety and Clinical Performance (SSCP) - validated by the Notified Body and published on EUDAMED. Neither is ever 'finished': both are updated from PMS and PMCF for the life of the device.
activities.what: Consolidate the clinical evaluation plan, the literature appraisal, any clinical investigations and equivalence data into the CER, with a documented benefit-risk conclusion against each intended-purpose claim; produce the SSCP where the device is implantable or class III; and establish the update cycle that refreshes both from PMS/PMCF.

The SSCP is public. Once EUDAMED-published it is read by competitors, clinicians and patients - keep it accurate, consistent with the IFU, and never overclaim.

activities.why: An IVD's CE evidence is its performance evaluation - the proof of scientific validity, analytical performance and clinical performance for the intended purpose. It is the IVDR equivalent of clinical evaluation, and it is what a notified body scrutinises hardest.
activities.what: Compile the IVD performance evaluation: the performance evaluation plan (PEP), the three pillars of evidence — scientific validity, analytical performance and clinical performance — and the performance evaluation report (PER) that feeds the technical file, kept live through post-market performance follow-up (PMPF).

Compile the performance evaluation report against the intended purpose and claims.
Define the post-market performance follow-up that keeps it updated after launch.

steps.t: Scientific validity, analytical and clinical performance are all evidenced.
The performance claims match the evidence.

tips: Three pillars - scientific validity, analytical and clinical performance - must all be present; a gap in one fails the evaluation.
Your claims and your evidence must match exactly; over-claiming is the classic IVDR finding.
Plan PMPF now - the evaluation is a living document, not a one-off.

activities.statement: Establish scientific validity - the analyte-condition association.
activities.why: Scientific validity is the first tier of the IVDR evidence pyramid: the association of the analyte with the clinical condition or physiological state. It is established from the scientific literature, expert consensus or prior data - not usually from your own study - and it underpins everything above it: without a sound analyte-condition link, analytical and clinical performance are meaningless. It is established early, from the research that the device came out of.
activities.what: Demonstrate the scientific validity of the analyte-condition association through a systematic literature review and, where relevant, prior data, documented in the scientific validity report that anchors the performance evaluation.

steps.d: The association you rely on for the intended purpose.
Systematic literature, consensus, prior proof-of-concept.
The scientific-validity conclusion anchoring the PER.

refs: Literature, consensus, prior data - feeds AP & CP

tips: Scientific validity is usually established from literature and consensus, not a new study - do not over-engineer it.
For a novel biomarker it is the hard part and may need generating - budget for it.
A reproducible literature search applies here too - document databases and terms.
SV is the foundation; if it is weak, no amount of analytical or clinical performance rescues the claim.

lean.start: A documented analyte-condition association from literature or consensus, captured in a scientific-validity report.
lean.evolve: Strengthen with additional evidence as it accumulates and keep current with new literature.
startDetail: A defined review question: which analyte, which clinical condition or physiological state, in which population.
A reproducible literature-search protocol (databases, terms, inclusion/exclusion) with a dated results log.
An appraisal of the retrieved evidence for quality and relevance — not just a citation list.
A scientific-validity report concluding the strength of the analyte-condition association.
Any prior in-house or predicate data referenced where it supports the association.
evolveDetail: Scheduled literature re-runs so the association stays current.
Incorporation of your own accumulating clinical data.
Alignment with any common specifications or reference methods that emerge.

activities.statement: Run a feasibility / proof-of-concept study to prove preliminary analytical validity.
activities.why: Most IVDs emerge from years of research; before committing to the full analytical and clinical performance studies you run a feasibility / proof-of-concept study to confirm the assay actually works, generate preliminary analytical data (LoD, precision, dynamic range) and de-risk the design. IVDR expects the performance evaluation to be planned and staged - feasibility findings feed the design inputs, sharpen the intended purpose, inform the risk analysis and set realistic acceptance criteria for the pivotal studies that follow.
activities.what: Run an early proof-of-concept study on a representative (not necessarily final) design to confirm technical viability and produce preliminary analytical performance, then use the results to refine design inputs, intended purpose and risk, and to scope the pivotal analytical and clinical performance studies.
activities.how: [TO AUTHOR in Phase 2]

steps.d: What must be true for the assay to be viable - target LoD, precision, dynamic range, specimen types.
Generate preliminary analytical data on a representative design; keep it exploratory, not pivotal.
Update design inputs, intended purpose, risk analysis and the performance evaluation plan's acceptance criteria.

tips: Feasibility data is not pivotal data - regulators will not accept a feasibility study as the analytical or clinical performance evidence. Its job is to de-risk, not to prove.
Freeze enough of the design before the pivotal analytical study, or you will simply repeat it on the changed assay.
Feed feasibility failures into the risk file and the design, not the bin - they are your cheapest lessons.

lean.start: A short proof-of-concept confirming the assay measures the analyte, with preliminary LoD/precision to set the pivotal acceptance criteria.
lean.evolve: Formalise into a staged performance-evaluation plan where feasibility gates the pivotal analytical then clinical studies.
startDetail: A proof-of-concept study on a representative (not necessarily final) design.
Confirmation that the assay actually measures the intended analyte (technical viability).
Preliminary analytical performance — e.g. LoD and precision — to size the pivotal studies.
Results fed back to refine design inputs, intended purpose and risk.
The pivotal analytical and clinical performance studies scoped from what feasibility showed.
evolveDetail: A staged PEP where feasibility gates the pivotal analytical study.
Pre-set acceptance criteria carried from feasibility into the pivotal design.
Design changes from feasibility folded back through design control and risk.

subs.why: Without pre-set targets a feasibility study drifts and proves nothing useful for the pivotal plan.
subs.what: Define the preliminary performance targets the assay must hit to be worth pursuing.

how.d: LoD, precision, dynamic range, specimen types, obvious interferents.
Objectives reflect the claims you will eventually need to support.

subs.why: Early real data is the only honest way to know whether the concept is viable before big spend.

how.d: Close enough to the intended design that the data is meaningful.

subs.t: Feed results into design, risk & the PE plan
subs.why: Feasibility earns its cost only if it improves the design and the plan that follow.

activities.statement: Establish analytical performance (analytical validity) - the IVD's verification.
activities.why: Analytical performance is the verification evidence for an IVD: it demonstrates the assay measures the analyte correctly. IVDR Annex XIII 1.2.2 and Annex I(9) require analytical sensitivity and specificity, trueness, precision (repeatability and reproducibility), limit of blank / detection / quantitation, measuring range and linearity, interference and cross-reactivity, specimen types and stability. This is the 'analytical validity' that must be solid before you spend on clinical performance - a weak AP dossier sinks the whole evaluation.
activities.what: Plan and execute the analytical performance studies to recognised protocols (CLSI/ISO) on the near-final design, covering the full IVDR analytical parameter set across the real specimen matrix and stability conditions, and document the outcome against pre-set acceptance criteria in the analytical performance report.
activities.how: [TO AUTHOR in Phase 2]

steps.d: Parameters, protocols (CLSI EP), specimen types, interferents, acceptance criteria.
Precision, trueness, LoB/LoD/LoQ, linearity/measuring range, analytical specificity, carryover, stability.
Document results, deviations and conclusions in the analytical performance report.
CLSI/ISO protocols referenced by number; sample sizes justified.
The matrix and interferents that occur in intended use, not only easy ones.

tips: Reference the CLSI EP protocols by name - reviewers expect EP05/EP06/EP07/EP17 and read your study designs against them.
Test the specimen types and interferents that occur in real use (haemolysis, lipaemia, common drugs), not just the easy matrix.
Stability (real-time + accelerated) is where dossiers stall because real-time takes calendar time - start it early, in parallel.
Analytical performance is verification, not validation - keep the language precise; clinical performance is the validation step.

lean.start: An analytical performance plan and the core studies (precision, trueness, LoD, key interferents) against pre-set acceptance criteria.
lean.evolve: Complete the full parameter set and real-time stability, and fold results into the Performance Evaluation Report.
startDetail: An analytical performance plan referencing recognised protocols (CLSI / ISO) per parameter.
The core studies executed on the near-final design: precision, trueness/bias, LoD/LoQ, linearity.
Key interferences and cross-reactants tested across the real specimen matrix.
Pre-set acceptance criteria stated before testing, and results judged against them.
Specimen types and stability conditions covered as the intended purpose requires.
An analytical performance report documenting outcome vs criteria.
evolveDetail: The full IVDR analytical parameter set completed.
Real-time (not just accelerated) stability data accumulated.
Results folded into the Performance Evaluation Report.

activities.statement: Establish analytical performance of the software / algorithm.
activities.why: For software- and algorithm-driven IVDs (including SaMD and AI-based devices) analytical performance also covers the computational output - the algorithm's measurement or classification performance against a reference, its precision/reproducibility and its behaviour across the input space. It complements the wet-lab analytical performance and is scrutinised for reproducibility, dataset provenance and version control.
activities.what: Plan and run analytical performance for the software/algorithm component: define the reference, datasets and metrics, evaluate precision/reproducibility and edge cases, and report against acceptance criteria with the exact software version pinned.
activities.how: [TO AUTHOR in Phase 2]

Precision/reproducibility, input-space coverage, failure modes.
AP is valid only for the software version tested.

tips: Pin the exact software version - analytical performance is only valid for the version tested; a later build needs re-assessment.
Keep training, validation and test data strictly separated for algorithms - leakage inflates performance and gets caught.
Document dataset provenance and representativeness; a reviewer will ask where the data came from and whether it matches the intended population.

lean.evolve: Add input-space/edge-case coverage and bias evaluation, and link to the AI lifecycle where applicable.

Edge cases and input-range behaviour tested, not just the happy path.
Results reported against pre-set acceptance criteria.

evolveDetail: Broader input-space and edge-case coverage.
Sub-group bias evaluation where the algorithm affects clinical decisions.
Linkage to the AI/ML lifecycle (PCCP, monitoring) where applicable.

subs.why: Performance claims mean nothing without a defined ground truth and representative data.
subs.what: Specify the reference standard, datasets (with provenance) and the metrics.

how.d: How each case's true value/label is determined.
Training/validation/test kept independent.

subs.what: Assess precision/reproducibility and behaviour on edge and failure cases.

activities.statement: Establish clinical performance (clinical validity) - the IVD's validation.
activities.why: Clinical performance is the validation evidence: the device yields results correlated with the clinical condition in the intended population - diagnostic sensitivity/specificity, PPV/NPV, likelihood ratios and expected values. IVDR Annex XIII 1.2.3 requires it; where it cannot be shown from existing data or literature it comes from a clinical performance study to ISO 20916, on the frozen device, in the intended-use setting. It is CONDITIONAL: for well-established analytes, scientific validity + analytical performance + existing clinical data may suffice - the route must be justified in the plan.
activities.what: Establish clinical performance: decide the route (existing data/literature vs a clinical performance study) and justify it; where a study is needed, plan and run it to ISO 20916 on the final device in the intended population, reporting diagnostic performance against pre-set acceptance criteria.
activities.how: [TO AUTHOR in Phase 2]

Companion diagnostics and novel analytes attract the most scrutiny - plan a formal study and expect competent-authority involvement.
Clinical performance and summative usability both validate the finished device - schedule them together on the frozen design.

activities.statement: Compile the Performance Evaluation Report + SSP + PMPF.
activities.why: The Performance Evaluation Report is the IVDR analogue of the CER: it consolidates scientific validity, analytical performance and clinical performance into a demonstration that the device meets its intended purpose, with the benefit-risk. For class C/D it drives the Summary of Safety and Performance (SSP), and it is maintained through post-market performance follow-up (PMPF). It is the most scrutinised part of the IVDR technical file.
activities.what: Compile the Performance Evaluation Report from the SV, AP and CP evidence with the benefit-risk conclusion; produce the Summary of Safety and Performance (SSP) for class C/D; and establish the PMPF plan/report loop that keeps the PER current.

The SSP (class C/D) is public via EUDAMED - keep it accurate and consistent with the PER.
Wire the PMPF loop now - the PER is living, and the NB checks it is maintained.
The three evidence tiers must cohere in the PER, not sit as three disconnected reports.

activities.statement: Classify the device under MDR - the gate that sets your route.
activities.why: Classification is the Phase-1 gate that sets your entire conformity route. MDR Annex VIII's 22 rules assign the device to class I, IIa, IIb or III based on intended purpose, duration and invasiveness. The class determines the conformity-assessment procedure (Art 52), whether a Notified Body is involved, and the depth of clinical evidence - get it wrong and the whole plan and cost are wrong. Class I is self-declared (except sterile Is, measuring Im, reusable-surgical Ir); IIa and above need a Notified Body.
activities.what: Determine the MDR risk class from the intended purpose against Annex VIII, document the rule-by-rule rationale, and confirm the resulting conformity-assessment route (self-declaration vs Notified Body) that the rest of the project follows.
activities.how: [TO AUTHOR in Phase 2]

steps.d: Work the 22 rules against the intended purpose, duration and invasiveness.
Where several rules apply, the highest class wins.
Self-declaration (class I) vs Notified Body (IIa+); record the decision.

records: MDR classification record with rule rationale
tips: Classify from the intended purpose, not the technology - the same tech can be a different class for a different claim.
Document the rule-by-rule reasoning; a reviewer checks you applied Annex VIII, not just the answer.
Borderline and up-classification are common under MDR - if unsure, check MDCG 2021-24 or seek a competent-authority view.
The class drives NB involvement, clinical depth and timeline - settle it before you plan anything else.

lean.start: A documented classification with the governing Annex VIII rule and the resulting route (self-cert vs Notified Body).
lean.evolve: Re-confirm on any intended-purpose change; classification is revisited whenever claims change.
startDetail: The intended purpose, duration of use (transient/short/long-term) and degree of invasiveness written down — the inputs to the rules.
A rule-by-rule walk through MDR Annex VIII's 22 rules, recording why each does or doesn't apply.
The resulting class (I / IIa / IIb / III) with the single governing rule that drives it.
The conformity-assessment route that follows: self-declaration (class I) vs Notified Body (IIa+), noting the Is/Im/Ir special cases.
A note of the implications for clinical-evidence depth and technical-file scope at that class.
evolveDetail: A change-control trigger so any claim change re-opens classification.
Borderline/qualification questions logged with rationale (MDCG guidance, device vs medicinal).
Alignment with the Notified Body's view early to avoid a late reclassification.

subs.what: Work the 22 rules against the device's intended purpose and characteristics.

how.d: The parameters the rules turn on.

subs.why: Reviewers assess the reasoning, not just the class.
subs.what: Record which rule governs and why.

subs.why: The route sets NB involvement, evidence depth and timeline for everything downstream.
subs.what: Decide self-declaration vs Notified Body and record it.

activities.statement: Classify the IVD under IVDR - rule-based A/B/C/D gate.
activities.why: IVDR replaced the old list-based IVDD approach with a rule-based, risk classification: classes A, B, C and D via Annex VIII's 7 rules, on the risk to the individual and to public health. The class sets the conformity route - class A is self-declared (except A-sterile), B/C/D require a Notified Body, and class D additionally requires EU reference laboratory involvement and batch verification. Because IVDR up-classified most assays versus the IVDD, the vast majority now need an NB, so classification determines everything downstream.
activities.what: Determine the IVDR class (A/B/C/D) from the intended purpose against Annex VIII's seven rules, document the rule rationale, and confirm the conformity route (self-cert vs Notified Body, plus EU reference laboratory and batch verification for class D).
activities.how: [TO AUTHOR in Phase 2]

steps.d: In order, on risk to the individual and to public health.
A (lowest) to D (highest).
Self-cert (A) vs NB (B/C/D); class D adds EU ref-lab + batch verification.

records: IVDR classification record with rule rationale
tips: IVDR classification is rule-based, not a list - apply Annex VIII rules 1-7 and take the highest applicable class.
Most assays up-classified versus the IVDD; assume you need a Notified Body unless the device is clearly class A.
Class D triggers EU reference-laboratory review and batch verification - a major timeline impact, so plan for it early.
Companion diagnostics are class C by rule; near-patient and self-testing raise the class - check the intended user.

lean.start: A documented A/B/C/D classification with the governing Annex VIII rule and the route it implies.
lean.evolve: Re-confirm on intended-purpose changes and factor class D reference-lab timelines into the plan.
startDetail: The intended purpose framed for the rules: what is detected/measured, for what clinical decision, and the risk to individual and public health.

The class (A / B / C / D) with the governing rule and any higher-rule override.
The route it implies: self-declaration (class A, non-sterile) vs Notified Body (B/C/D).
For class D: the EU reference-laboratory involvement and batch-verification timelines flagged into the plan.

activities.why: Selling in the USA means FDA, not CE. You have to determine the right pathway (510(k), De Novo, PMA or exempt), make the submission, and only distribute once cleared or approved - with US-specific QMSR and UDI obligations on top.
activities.what: Complete the MDR Annex I GSPR (General Safety and Performance Requirements) conformity checklist: go through each requirement, mark it applicable or not with justification, and map every applicable requirement to the specific evidence, standard or document that satisfies it — the backbone index of the technical file.

steps.d: Take TEF-SOP-01 and determine classification and pathway (TEF-TPL-01).

Build the submission (substantial-equivalence for a 510(k), or clinical/technical evidence for a PMA, plus FDA cybersecurity docs for software); submit and respond to FDA questions; record on TEF-FOR-01/02.
Confirm clearance/approval before US distribution; complete establishment registration and device listing; log status in TEF-LIS-01 and reassess on significant change.

steps.t: The FDA pathway is determined and documented.
No US distribution occurs before clearance or approval.

correct and provide CAPA evidence for any Stage 2 nonconformities within the timeline the Notified Body sets - this is the last gate before the certificate.

activities.why: Certification is not the end - manufacturers must proactively collect and act on real-world data throughout the device's life. MDR Art 83-92 (IVDR Art 78-81) require a PMS system, a PMS plan, periodic safety / PMS reports (PSUR / PMS report), and post-market clinical / performance follow-up (PMCF / PMPF), feeding vigilance, risk and the clinical evaluation. A live PMS system is mandatory and a key audit focus.
activities.what: Operate the post-market surveillance system: a PMS plan, proactive and reactive data collection, trend analysis, the PSUR / PMS report, and PMCF / PMPF studies - with outputs flowing into vigilance, the risk file, the clinical / performance evaluation and CAPA.
activities.how:
1. Run PMS and produce PMS report/PSUR (PMS-SOP-01).
2. Operate vigilance reporting (VIG-SOP-02) and PMCF/PMPF (PCF/PEV).

steps.d: write the PMS plan: what data you'll collect, from where, how often, and what triggers action. PMS is proactive - you go looking for signals, not just wait for complaints.
gather post-market data reactively (complaints, returns, incidents) AND proactively (literature, registries, user surveys, similar-device data).
analyse the data for trends and signals - a slow rise in a particular issue is exactly what PMS exists to catch early.
write the periodic safety/PMS report at the frequency your device class requires, summarising findings and actions.
plan and run post-market clinical (or performance) follow-up to keep your evidence current after launch - regulators expect ongoing evidence, not a one-off.
feed PMS findings back into vigilance (reportable events), the risk file, the clinical evaluation and CAPA. PMS that changes nothing is just data collection.

steps.t: A PMS plan defines data sources, indicators and review frequency.
Field data, complaints and literature are collected and analysed on schedule.
PMS findings feed risk, clinical evaluation and labelling.
Periodic safety / PMS reports are produced as required.

records: PMCF / PMPF plan and evaluation report
tips: PMS is proactive, not 'wait for complaints'. Under MDR/IVDR you must actively go looking for safety signals - a reactive-only system is a guaranteed finding.
The PSUR is on a clock tied to your device class. Missing the cadence is a straightforward, avoidable nonconformity - put it in the calendar.
PMS exists to CHANGE things. If your PMS reports never trigger a risk update, a CER update or a CAPA, an auditor will ask what the point of them is.
PMCF/PMPF is where post-market reality meets your pre-market claims. Plan it before launch - retrofitting follow-up evidence under a deadline is brutal.

activities.statement: Operate PMS - proactive surveillance, PMS report & PSUR.
activities.why: PMS is the active, systematic collection and analysis of real-world data on your device once it is on the market. MDR Art 83 requires a PMS system run to the PMS plan; the outputs are the PMS report (class I) or the Periodic Safety Update Report (PSUR, class IIa and above), summarising findings, conclusions and any preventive/corrective actions, and feeding the CER, risk file and design. This is proactive surveillance, distinct from reactive vigilance reporting.
activities.what: Operate the PMS system per the plan: gather and analyse complaints, vigilance, literature and market data; draw conclusions on safety and performance; and produce the PMS report or PSUR (at the class-driven frequency) that feeds the CER/PER, risk file and CAPA.

activities.why: Once your IVD is in use, real-world performance can differ from the lab. Post-market surveillance and performance follow-up (PMPF) catch drift, misuse and rare failures and feed them back into your evidence and risk file - an IVDR legal duty.
activities.what: Stand up IVD post-market surveillance and post-market performance follow-up (PMPF): collect real-world performance data (field results, complaints, external QA / proficiency data, literature) to a plan, analyse it for performance drift or new failure modes, and feed the findings back into the performance evaluation, the risk file and CAPA.

tips: PMPF is proactive - waiting for complaints is not performance follow-up.

activities.statement: Write the PMCF plan (proactive clinical follow-up).
activities.why: The PMCF plan defines how you will proactively collect clinical data once the device is on the market - the activities (registries, surveys, studies, literature), their objectives and their schedule. MDR Annex XIV Part B requires it, and MDCG 2020-7 provides the template. A concrete PMCF plan is what turns 'we'll monitor' into an actual evidence-generation programme that keeps the CER current.
activities.what: Write the PMCF plan per MDCG 2020-7: the proactive activities, their objectives tied to residual uncertainties and claims, methods and schedule, feeding the PMCF report, CER and PSUR.
activities.how:
1. Run PMS and produce PMS report/PSUR (PMS-SOP-01).
2. Operate vigilance reporting (VIG-SOP-02) and PMCF/PMPF (PCF/PEV).

activities.statement: Execute PMCF and write the PMCF evaluation report.
activities.why: Post-Market Clinical Follow-up (PMCF) is the proactive, continuous collection of clinical data once the device is marketed, to confirm safety and performance over its lifetime and refine the benefit-risk. MDR Annex XIV Part B requires a PMCF plan and, following it, a PMCF evaluation report that feeds the CER and the PSUR. For IVDs the equivalent is PMPF (post-market performance follow-up). Where PMCF is not conducted, the absence must be justified.
activities.what: Execute the PMCF plan and produce the PMCF evaluation report: analyse the clinical data collected post-market (registries, surveys, studies, literature) against the plan's objectives, update the benefit-risk and clinical claims, and feed the CER and PSUR.

activities.statement: Set up EU vigilance & incident reporting.
activities.why: Vigilance is the reactive duty to report serious incidents and field safety corrective actions to competent authorities within strict deadlines. MDR Art 87-92 (IVDR Art 82-87) set what and when to report - a serious incident is generally reportable within 15 days (2 days for serious public-health threat, 10 for death/serious deterioration). This is distinct from proactive PMS; missing a vigilance deadline is a serious regulatory failure.
activities.what: Establish the EU vigilance process: how serious incidents are captured, evaluated for reportability, and reported to authorities within the deadlines (via EUDAMED where available), including field safety corrective actions and FSNs.
activities.how:
1. Run PMS and produce PMS report/PSUR (PMS-SOP-01).
2. Operate vigilance reporting (VIG-SOP-02) and PMCF/PMPF (PCF/PEV).

steps.d: Reportability against the serious-incident definition.
Authorities via EUDAMED; 15/10/2-day clocks.
Field safety corrective actions and notices where needed.

tips: Vigilance is reactive reporting; PMS is proactive surveillance - do not merge the two processes.
Know the clocks: generally 15 days, 10 for death/serious deterioration, 2 for a serious public-health threat.
Evaluate reportability quickly and document the decision - even 'not reportable' needs a rationale.
A field safety corrective action needs an FSN to users - plan the communication path.

activities.why: From the moment anything is made or shipped, problems can occur - complaints from the field and nonconforming product internally - and you must be able to capture, evaluate and contain them, and decide whether they are reportable. ISO 13485 requires complaint handling (8.2.2), notification to authorities where applicable (8.2.3) and control of nonconforming product (8.3); the regulations add mandatory vigilance reporting. Standing this up early means no event is lost before the full CAPA and vigilance systems come online in Phase 2.
activities.what: Establish the intake and handling of complaints and the control of nonconforming product: how each is captured, evaluated, contained, dispositioned and escalated, including the trigger for regulatory reporting. The output is working complaint-capture and nonconforming-product controls that feed CAPA and vigilance.
activities.how:
1. Open the complaint log (CMP-LIS-01) and define intake.
2. Define how nonconforming product/output is identified and segregated (NCP-SOP-01).

steps.d: decide how complaints reach you (email, phone, form) and make sure they ALL land in one place that gets logged. Lost complaints are both a safety and a legal problem.
record every complaint with date, source, device and description, and acknowledge it. The reporting clock can start the moment you become aware.
assess whether it needs investigation and - crucially - whether it might be reportable to a regulator (a potential serious incident).
write down the criteria and timeline for notifying authorities, even before the full vigilance system (P2.10). You must not miss the clock from day one.
define how product that doesn't meet spec is spotted and immediately marked and segregated, so it can't be used or shipped by mistake.
define who decides what happens to nonconforming product (rework, accept-with-concession, scrap) and record the decision and the authority for it.
link complaints and nonconformities to the CAPA process so recurring problems get root-caused, not just patched.

steps.t: A single complaints log captures every complaint with the date received.
Nonconforming product is identified and segregated in a defined quarantine.
Reportability of each complaint is assessed and recorded.
Complaint and nonconformity trends are reviewed.

records: Complaint records with evaluation and reportability decision
Nonconforming-product records with disposition and authority
tips: Never let a complaint sit in someone's inbox. The reporting clock and patient safety both depend on complaints being captured the moment they arrive.
'Complaint' is broader than you think - any communication alleging a deficiency in a marketed device counts, including via distributors and social media. Capture them all.
Segregate nonconforming product physically, not just on paper. The classic disaster is rejected stock quietly finding its way back into shippable inventory.
Even pre-market, set the reporting criteria now. Manufacturers get into serious trouble for LATE vigilance reports far more often than for the underlying issue.

Trend analysis across complaints to surface systemic issues.
Reportability decision trees integrated with the vigilance system.

subs.t: Set up the complaint intake channel & log
subs.why: You must hear about problems in the field to act on them. A single, known intake channel and a complaint log are the minimum - and a legal expectation.
subs.what: Create one clear channel for complaints to arrive and a single log to record every one with the date received.

how.d: A single route (email/form/phone) staff and customers use - so nothing is missed.
One row per complaint, capturing the date received first (the reporting clock often starts here).
Device, lot/serial, what happened, the reporter, and any patient harm.
Someone responsible for assessing each complaint promptly.

subs.why: Complaints have to be assessed consistently - including the critical question of whether they are reportable to a regulator on a clock. A procedure makes that reliable instead of ad hoc.
subs.what: Adapt the complaint-handling SOP: how each complaint is assessed, investigated and assessed for reportability.

how.d: How each complaint is assessed and investigated, proportionate to risk.
Decide if and when it is a reportable event (vigilance/MDR), against the regulatory timelines.
Whether to request the device back and how the returned unit is examined.
Recurring complaints feed corrective action, not just one-off fixes.

how.t: Close the loop with the complainant
how.d: Where appropriate, respond and record the outcome.

subs.why: Every nonconformity needs a decision (rework, scrap, concession) and the systemic ones need fixing at the root - otherwise the same problem returns.
subs.what: Define how disposition decisions are made and how recurring issues feed CAPA.

how.d: Rework, repair, scrap, or use-as-is (concession) with justification.
Who can sign off each disposition, especially use-as-is.
Confirm reworked items meet requirements before release.
Route recurring root causes into corrective action so the problem does not return.

activities.why: CAPA is the engine of improvement and the place auditors look hardest: ISO 13485 (8.5) requires corrective action to eliminate the causes of nonconformities and preventive action to address potential ones, with verification of effectiveness. Connected products add security-incident management (ISO 27001 A.5.24+) and GDPR breach notification (Art 33/34) on tight timelines. Making CAPA and incident response operational ties complaints, nonconformities, audit findings, security incidents and breaches into one closed-loop system.
activities.what: Make CAPA and incident response operational: a CAPA process (intake, investigation, root-cause analysis, action, effectiveness check) fed by complaints, nonconformities, audits and trends; plus security-incident management and a GDPR breach-notification process with the 72-hour clock and decision criteria.
activities.how:
1. Operate CAPA (CAP-SOP-01) with root-cause and effectiveness checks.
2. Stand up incident & breach response (IRP-SOP-01) incl. the 72h breach path.

steps.d: decide which problems become a CAPA (recurring nonconformities, complaints, audit findings, adverse trends) vs a quick fix. Not everything is a CAPA - but the systemic stuff must be.
record what happened, where and how big - a vague problem statement leads to a useless investigation.
take immediate correction to stop the bleeding (quarantine product, pause a process) before you investigate the root cause.
dig past the symptom to the cause (5-whys, fishbone). 'Operator error' is almost never the root cause - ask why the process allowed it.
implement corrective action to remove the cause, and preventive action where the same cause could bite elsewhere.
check the action was effective before you close the CAPA - did the problem stop recurring? Closing without this is the #1 CAPA finding.
route complaints, nonconformities, audit findings and trends into one CAPA system, and analyse the data for patterns you'd miss case by case.
detect, classify, contain, eradicate and learn from information-security incidents (ISO 27001 A.5.24-A.5.28) - and feed the lessons into CAPA.

steps.t: Handle personal-data breaches on the clock
steps.d: assess any personal-data breach and notify the supervisory authority within 72 hours, and data subjects where required (GDPR Art 33/34). Pre-decide who makes that call.

steps.t: The CAPA log records root-cause analysis and an effectiveness check.
CAPA is reserved for systemic issues and effectiveness is verified before closure.

records: CAPA records with root-cause, actions and effectiveness checks
tips: CAPA is where auditors spend the most time, because it shows whether your system actually learns. A pile of open, overdue CAPAs says more than any policy document.
Effectiveness check is the step everyone skips and every auditor checks. A CAPA closed without proving the fix worked is just paperwork - and a guaranteed finding.
'Operator error' or 'human error' is almost never the true root cause. Ask why the process let the error happen and reach the cause you can actually fix.
Don't open a CAPA for everything - you'll drown. Correct small things on the spot; reserve CAPA for systemic or recurring problems. A CAPA system you can't keep up with is worse than none.

lean.start: One CAPA log with root-cause and an effectiveness check. Reserve CAPA for systemic issues; fix small things on the spot.
lean.evolve: Add trend analysis, formal root-cause methods and integrated security-incident/breach workflows as volume grows.
startDetail: A single CAPA log with intake from complaints, nonconformities, audits and trends.
An investigation and root-cause step — not jumping straight to a fix.
Corrective and, where warranted, preventive actions with owners and due dates.
An effectiveness check that confirms the action actually worked before closing.
A threshold so CAPA is reserved for systemic issues, with on-the-spot correction for one-offs.
Basic security-incident and GDPR-breach handling (72-hour clock) wired in.
evolveDetail: Trend analysis feeding preventive actions proactively.
Formal root-cause methods (5-whys, fishbone, fault tree) applied consistently.
Integrated security-incident and breach workflows with defined escalation.

subs.why: CAPA is how your QMS learns. A clear procedure and a single log keep it focused on systemic problems rather than becoming a bureaucratic dumping ground for every minor issue.
subs.what: Adapt the CAPA procedure and open a single CAPA log, reserving CAPA for systemic issues.

how.d: Systemic or high-risk issues - fix small one-offs on the spot, don't drown CAPA.
One place to track every CAPA to closure.
Identify, investigate root cause, act, verify effectiveness, close.

how.t: Set the sources that feed it
how.d: Complaints, audits, nonconformities, trends, incidents.

subs.why: Fixing symptoms guarantees recurrence. Real root-cause analysis is what makes a corrective action actually corrective.
subs.what: Investigate each CAPA to its root cause using a structured method.

how.d: Clearly state what is wrong, where and how often.
Immediate correction to stop ongoing harm while you investigate.
A structured method (5 Whys, fishbone) - not the first symptom.
Address the root cause so it cannot recur.

subs.why: An action that is not checked may not have worked. Effectiveness verification before closure is the step that separates a real CAPA system from a paper one.
subs.what: Implement the corrective action and verify it was effective before closing.

how.d: Make the change everywhere it applies.
Apply the fix to other products/processes with the same cause.
Confirm with evidence that the problem stopped recurring.
Do not close on 'action done' - close on 'action worked'.

subs.why: For connected devices, a security incident or data breach is a nonconformity with a regulatory clock. Wiring these into CAPA keeps response fast and compliant.
subs.what: Integrate security-incident and data-breach handling into the CAPA/incident workflow with their reporting clocks.

how.d: How security incidents and data breaches enter the system.
Breach notification (72h) and device vigilance timeframes.
The incident-response loop, recorded.
So the same incident cannot recur.

activities.statement: Operate & govern CAPA - run the loop, not just define it.
activities.why: Establishing the CAPA process is not the same as running it. This is the ongoing governance: operating the CAPA system so problems from complaints, nonconformities, audits and PMS are investigated to root cause, effective actions are taken and verified, and management reviews CAPA health. A well-run CAPA loop is the single strongest signal to an auditor that the QMS is alive; a backlog of open, ineffective CAPAs is the strongest signal it is not.
activities.what: Operate and govern the CAPA system on an ongoing basis: triage inputs, drive root-cause investigation, take and verify the effectiveness of corrective and preventive actions, and report CAPA metrics into management review - maturing from reactive fixes to trend-driven prevention.
activities.how: [TO AUTHOR in Phase 2]
activities.depends: RSK.postmarket, PMS.operate, VIG.eu, VIG.us, CMP.setup, CAP.setup, NCP.setup, MRV.establish, AUD.establish, IRP.establish

steps.d: Inputs from complaints, NCs, audits, PMS; 5-whys / fishbone.
Corrective/preventive actions taken and their effectiveness checked.
CAPA metrics and ageing into management review; act on trends.

tips: Verify effectiveness, don't just close - an action that didn't work is a repeat finding waiting to happen.
Watch the CAPA backlog and ageing - auditors read it directly as QMS health.
Get to real root cause (5-whys / fishbone), not the symptom - or the problem returns.
Preventive action means acting on trends before they become complaints - that is the mature end of the loop.

lean.start: A running CAPA log with root-cause investigation and effectiveness checks on the significant issues.
lean.evolve: Trend-driven preventive action and CAPA metrics governed through management review.
startDetail: CAPA inputs triaged as they arrive (complaints, nonconformities, audit findings, trends).
Root-cause investigation driven on the significant issues, not just symptom fixes.
Corrective and preventive actions taken, with owners and due dates tracked to closure.
Effectiveness checks confirming each action worked before the CAPA is closed.
CAPA metrics (open/overdue, recurrence, time-to-close) reported into management review.
A shift over time from reactive fixes toward trend-driven prevention.
evolveDetail: Proactive preventive actions triggered by trends before failures occur.
CAPA KPIs trended and governed at management review.
Cross-linking of CAPA with risk, PMS and supplier data.

subs.why: CAPA only works if the right problems get real investigation.
subs.what: Triage inputs and drive root-cause analysis.

how.d: Complaints, NCs, audit findings, PMS trends.
5-whys / fishbone, not the symptom.

subs.why: An unverified action is not a closed problem.
subs.what: Take corrective/preventive actions and check they worked.

how.d: Owner and due date.
Evidence the problem is gone.

subs.why: Ageing, open CAPAs are the clearest sign of a failing system.
subs.what: Monitor CAPA ageing and closure.

how.d: Open items by age.
Overdue CAPAs raised.

subs.why: CAPA health is a required management-review input and drives prevention.
subs.what: Report CAPA metrics and trends to management review.

how.d: Open/closed, ageing, effectiveness.
Act on trends before complaints.

activities.why: Devices and their QMS change constantly, and uncontrolled change is how validated states are lost and unintended consequences slip in. ISO 13485 requires design and development changes to be identified, reviewed, verified / validated and approved before implementation (7.3.9), and document changes to be controlled (4.2.4). The regulations add the notion of significant changes that can affect certification (MDCG 2020-3). A single change-control process keeps design, document and process changes coordinated and evidenced.
activities.what: Establish change management: a process to request, assess (impact on safety, performance, regulatory status, validation), review, approve, implement and verify changes to design, documents, processes and software - including whether a change is significant for the regulatory route - with configuration management to keep versions coherent.
activities.how:
1. Operate change control (CHG-SOP-01) with impact & significance assessment.
2. Link AI changes to the PCCP envelope.

steps.t: Make changes go through one door
steps.d: set up a single change-request route so nothing significant changes informally. Uncontrolled change is how validated states quietly break.
record what's changing, why, and who asked - the start of the trail an auditor will follow.

steps.t: Assess the impact before any work
steps.d: before doing anything, assess the change's effect on safety, performance, risk, the validation status and the regulatory classification. Most damage comes from changes made without this step.

steps.t: Decide if it is a significant change
steps.d: determine whether it's a 'significant change' that affects your CE certificate (MDCG 2020-3) or needs a new FDA submission. Get this wrong and you can invalidate your market authorisation.
review the change with the right disciplines and approve it BEFORE implementation - not after it's already in production.
make the change, then re-do whatever verification/validation it affected. A change to a verified design un-verifies the parts it touches.
update all affected documents, records, risk file and the technical file, so nothing is left describing the old state.
maintain configuration/version control so the released state of design, documents and software is always known - you can always say exactly what's in the current device.

steps.t: Changes are logged with an impact assessment before implementation.
Significant changes are assessed for regulatory notification.

records: Change review / approval records and significant-change determinations
tips: The 'significant change' determination is where small companies get hurt. A change you thought was minor can require Notified Body approval or a new 510(k) - assess it before you ship, not after.
A change to a verified design un-verifies the bits it touches. Re-running the affected V&V is not optional - skipping it is how regressions reach patients.
Update the technical file and risk file with EVERY change. The classic finding is a device that's been improved three times while its documentation still describes version one.
Resist informal changes 'just this once'. The whole point of change control is that there is no back door - one uncontrolled change can unravel a validated state.

lean.start: A single change-request log with an impact check before each change.
lean.evolve: Add formal change boards, significant-change assessment workflows and configuration baselines as the device and team grow.
startDetail: A change-request log capturing what is proposed and why.
An impact assessment before approval: effect on safety, performance, regulatory status and validation.
A significant-change assessment — does this change the regulatory route or need NB notification?
Review and approval by the right roles before implementation.
Implementation with verification that the change did what was intended.
Configuration management so document/software versions stay coherent.
evolveDetail: A change control board with defined membership for higher-impact changes.
Structured significant-change / substantial-modification workflows.
Configuration baselines and release management across products.
