subs.why: Uncontrolled change is how validated systems and safe designs quietly break. A single change-request log makes every change visible and decided, not accidental.
subs.what: Establish a single change-request log capturing every proposed change.

how.d: One place for every proposed change - no side-channel changes.
What, why, who requested it, and what it affects.
Route low-impact vs significant changes appropriately.

how.t: Require a decision before any change
how.d: Nothing changes without documented approval.

subs.why: The point of change control is to think before you act: what does this change touch - risk, validation, regulatory status - so nothing is broken downstream.
subs.what: Assess each change's impact on risk, design, validation and documentation before implementing it.

how.d: Risk file, design, validation, labelling, documents and regulatory status.
What must be re-verified, re-validated or re-approved.
Sequence, the evidence needed, and a rollback if it fails.
Decide with the impact assessment in front of you.

subs.why: Some changes are 'significant' and trigger regulatory notification or a new conformity step. Missing that turns a routine update into a compliance breach.
subs.what: Assess whether a change is significant enough to require regulatory notification or re-assessment.

how.d: Against the MDR/IVDR (and FDA) significant-change criteria.
Notified Body or competent authority, before or as required.
Some changes need a new conformity step before release.
Record why a change was or was not notifiable - auditors check this.

subs.why: A controlled implementation - with the re-verification the impact assessment called for - is what keeps the change from introducing new problems.
subs.what: Implement the change under control, complete the required re-verification/validation, and update the documents.

how.d: Following the controlled change plan, not informally.
Re-verify / re-validate exactly what the impact assessment called for.
Specs, traceability, risk file, labelling and version history.
Verify the change achieved its purpose without new problems.

activities.why: From the moment anything is made or shipped, problems can occur - complaints from the field and nonconforming product internally - and you must be able to capture, evaluate and contain them, and decide whether they are reportable. ISO 13485 requires complaint handling (8.2.2), notification to authorities where applicable (8.2.3) and control of nonconforming product (8.3); the regulations add mandatory vigilance reporting. Standing this up early means no event is lost before the full CAPA and vigilance systems come online in Phase 2.
activities.what: Establish the intake and handling of complaints and the control of nonconforming product: how each is captured, evaluated, contained, dispositioned and escalated, including the trigger for regulatory reporting. The output is working complaint-capture and nonconforming-product controls that feed CAPA and vigilance.
activities.how:
1. Open the complaint log (CMP-LIS-01) and define intake.
2. Define how nonconforming product/output is identified and segregated (NCP-SOP-01).

steps.d: decide how complaints reach you (email, phone, form) and make sure they ALL land in one place that gets logged. Lost complaints are both a safety and a legal problem.
record every complaint with date, source, device and description, and acknowledge it. The reporting clock can start the moment you become aware.
assess whether it needs investigation and - crucially - whether it might be reportable to a regulator (a potential serious incident).
write down the criteria and timeline for notifying authorities, even before the full vigilance system (P2.10). You must not miss the clock from day one.
define how product that doesn't meet spec is spotted and immediately marked and segregated, so it can't be used or shipped by mistake.
define who decides what happens to nonconforming product (rework, accept-with-concession, scrap) and record the decision and the authority for it.
link complaints and nonconformities to the CAPA process so recurring problems get root-caused, not just patched.

steps.t: A single complaints log captures every complaint with the date received.
Nonconforming product is identified and segregated in a defined quarantine.
Reportability of each complaint is assessed and recorded.
Complaint and nonconformity trends are reviewed.

records: Complaint records with evaluation and reportability decision
Nonconforming-product records with disposition and authority
tips: Never let a complaint sit in someone's inbox. The reporting clock and patient safety both depend on complaints being captured the moment they arrive.
'Complaint' is broader than you think - any communication alleging a deficiency in a marketed device counts, including via distributors and social media. Capture them all.
Segregate nonconforming product physically, not just on paper. The classic disaster is rejected stock quietly finding its way back into shippable inventory.
Even pre-market, set the reporting criteria now. Manufacturers get into serious trouble for LATE vigilance reports far more often than for the underlying issue.

lean.start: A quarantine area and a nonconforming-product form, so suspect product can't be used by mistake.
lean.evolve: Add trend analysis, formal disposition boards and links to supplier and production controls as volume grows.
startDetail: A clear definition of nonconformity: product or material that fails to meet requirements.
A physical or system quarantine so nonconforming product is segregated and can't be used.
A nonconformity record capturing what, where and how much.
A disposition decision: rework, repair, use-as-is (justified), scrap or return.
Escalation into CAPA when the nonconformity points to a systemic cause.
A concession/authorisation step, with sign-off, where use-as-is is proposed.
evolveDetail: Trend analysis across nonconformities to find recurring causes.
A material-review board for disposition decisions.
Links from nonconformities back into supplier control and production.

subs.why: When something is wrong with product, it must be stopped from being used by mistake. Identification and segregation is how you prevent a known-bad unit reaching a patient.
subs.what: Establish how nonconforming product is identified, segregated and quarantined.

how.d: Label nonconforming product so it cannot be confused with good stock.
A defined, controlled area so it cannot be used or shipped by accident.
Log what, where, how much and why.
Apply this to incoming, in-process and finished product alike.

subs.why: IEC 62304 requires every software defect to be recorded, risk-assessed and fixed traceably - especially the safety-relevant ones.
subs.what: Adapt DEV-SOP-01 and open the problem/anomaly log.

how.t: Adapt the SOP to your software
how.d: DEV-SOP-01.
DEV-LIS-01 - every defect recorded.
Record, assess safety, resolve, verify, close.
From testing, the field, and SOUP advisories.

subs.why: Assessing safety relevance and fixing under change control is what separates a real process from a bug list.
subs.what: Record each problem, assess its safety/risk, and resolve it under change control with a verified fix.

how.d: DEV-FOR-01.
Link to the risk file - does it affect a risk control?
Fix it as a controlled change, not a quiet patch.
Confirm it works and introduced nothing new.

subs.why: Maintaining the log and trending recurring issues turns individual fixes into systemic improvement.
subs.what: Keep the anomaly log current, disclose known anomalies, and trend recurring issues into CAPA.

how.d: Including known anomalies shipped with a release.
Where required - hiding them is an audit finding.
Spot patterns across problems.
Turn recurring fixes into root-cause correction.

activities.why: A management system only works if Top Management owns it - ISO 13485 makes management commitment, the quality policy, measurable objectives and a clear assignment of responsibility and authority explicit requirements of clause 5, not optional good practice. Without a committed leadership that provides resources, appoints a management representative and sets the direction, the QMS decays into a paperwork exercise nobody follows. Reading the Implementation Handbook up front gives everyone the same mental model of the four-phase roadmap and how the documents fit together, so the build proceeds in a planned sequence rather than ad hoc.
activities.what: Establish leadership ownership of the QMS: Top Management formally commits resources, appoints a Management Representative (QMR) with defined authority, and - with the team - reads the Implementation Handbook (QMN-MAN-02) and agrees the implementation timeline in the tracker (QMN-LIS-01). The output is documented management commitment, a named QMR with a mandate, and a shared, scheduled implementation plan.
activities.how:
1. Top Management commits resources and a QMR.
2. Read the Implementation Handbook (QMN-MAN-02) and this guide.
3. Agree the implementation timeline using the tracker (QMN-LIS-01).
activities.documents: QMN-MAN-01; QMN-POL-01; QMN-LIS-01; QMN-MAN-02; QMN-MAN-03; QMN-LIS-02

steps.d: make an explicit decision to build the QMS and pursue the device, and say so to the team. Half-hearted starts are where QMS projects quietly die.
allocate the people, time and budget - and be realistic, this is months of work, not a weekend. Record the commitment in kick-off or management-review minutes.
name one person as Management Representative with clear authority to build, run and report on the QMS. A founder wearing two hats is fine early on, but the role must be explicit.
if you will sell in the EU, identify the Person Responsible for Regulatory Compliance (Art 15). Small manufacturers can use a qualified external PRRC.
state plainly that when safety and speed/cost conflict, safety wins. Your team calibrates to whatever leadership actually rewards.
work through the Implementation Handbook (QMN-MAN-02) and this guide so everyone shares the same four-phase mental model - an hour now saves weeks of confusion.
assign an owner to each phase and activity. The QMR coordinates but cannot do it all alone - shared ownership is what keeps momentum.
use the tracker (QMN-LIS-01) to schedule activities by phase and wave with target dates - and pad it, because first-timers always underestimate.
put a short weekly or fortnightly QMS stand-up in the calendar so the build does not stall the moment day-to-day work gets busy.

steps.t: Top management's commitment to the QMS is documented and dated.
A Quality Management Representative is appointed in writing.
Quality objectives have been communicated to staff.
Resources for the QMS are identified and allocated.

records: Written QMR appointment with defined authority
Implementation plan in QMN-LIS-01 with owners and dates
Record that the team read the Handbook
tips: A QMR who is also the busiest founder is the most common point of failure. Protect dedicated time for the QMS, or progress quietly stops.
You don't need a big quality team to start. One committed owner plus this platform gets a lean device a long way - bring in specialists for the hard parts (clinical, software, audit).
Management commitment is auditable: an auditor looks for minutes, the QMR appointment, and evidence that leadership actually reviews the QMS. 'We care about quality' is not evidence.
Resist 'certify first, build later'. The certificate is the by-product of a system that genuinely runs - fake it and the Stage 2 audit will find out.

lean.start: A short kick-off note recording leadership's commitment, a named QMR, a one-line quality policy and a simple timeline. Commitment can be one page.
lean.evolve: Formalise management responsibilities, add a resource plan and a regular leadership-review cadence as the organisation grows.
startDetail: A one-page management-commitment note signed by top management, committing resources to the QMS.
A named Management Representative (QMR) with written authority over the QMS.
A short quality policy (a few sentences) and 3–5 measurable quality objectives.
A quality-manual stub: the QMS scope, any excluded/non-applicable clauses, and the process-map reference.
A shared implementation timeline agreed with the team (the four-phase roadmap in a tracker).
The team having read the implementation handbook so everyone shares the same mental model.
evolveDetail: A responsibility/authority matrix across all QMS roles.
A resource plan tied to the objectives and a scheduled management-review cadence.
Objectives tracked with KPIs and reviewed at each management review.

subs.t: Secure top-management commitment to the QMS
subs.why: A quality management system only works when the people who control budget, priorities and hiring genuinely back it. If leadership treats it as a box-ticking exercise, so will everyone else - and the system quietly dies. Commitment is the foundation every later activity rests on.
subs.what: Get a documented, resourced decision from top management to build the QMS and pursue your regulatory goal. Be honest about both the benefits and the costs so there are no surprises later.

how.d: Market access, fewer costly mistakes, investor and customer confidence, a safer product.
Workload and discipline will increase during implementation - hiding this breeds resistance.
A short, dated commitment statement signed by top management - one page is enough.
Name the budget, the people and the time; a commitment with no resources is a slogan.
Tell all staff the organisation is going for this and why.

subs.why: Someone needs clear, delegated authority to own the QMS day to day and to speak for it to management - otherwise responsibility is everywhere and nowhere. ISO 13485 and MDR both require this role.
subs.what: Appoint a management representative in writing, with authority to establish, implement and maintain the QMS and to report on its performance to top management.

how.d: Someone with enough standing and access to management to actually drive change.
Maintaining the QMS, raising quality issues, reporting performance.
They can escalate quality concerns to top management without a filter.
Tell staff who the QMR is and what they own.

subs.t: Form the quality & regulatory project team
subs.why: Implementing a QMS touches design, production, suppliers, software and clinical work - far too much for one person. A small cross-functional team spreads the load and keeps the system grounded in how the company actually works.
subs.what: Assemble a small team covering the key functions, with a chair acting as the quality focal point.

how.d: Cover design, operations, software, clinical - so decisions are realistic.

how.t: Appoint a chair / quality focal point
how.d: One person coordinates, tracks progress and answers quality questions.
Give every member a clear slice so nothing falls between chairs.
Cadence and format for reporting to the QMR and management.

subs.why: You cannot plan what you have not measured. A baseline tells you honestly where you stand against ISO 13485 and the MDR/IVDR today, so the plan targets real gaps - and gives you a 'before' picture to show progress against.
subs.what: Assess current practice against the standard(s) and regulation, clause by clause and process by process, and record the gaps with a sense of their size.

how.d: The applicable standard(s) and regulation as your yardstick.
Rate where you are today - none, partial, or in place.
Capture each gap concretely, not just a score.
Rank by risk and effort so the plan tackles the important things first.

subs.why: A list of gaps is not a plan. Turning them into specific, time-bound actions with named owners is what actually moves the QMS forward and lets management see whether you are on track.
subs.what: Convert the baseline gaps into SMART actions, each with an owner and a target date, tracked to closure.

how.d: Each gap becomes a concrete action with a clear 'done' condition.
One accountable owner and a realistic target date each.
Prerequisites first (document control before all the SOPs).
Hold the plan in the tracker and keep it live.

subs.why: Momentum dies without rhythm. A short, regular meeting keeps implementation alive, surfaces blockers early, and builds the habit of talking about quality - which later becomes your management review.
subs.what: Set up recurring (e.g. weekly) team meetings with a standing agenda and a reporting line to management, and minute the decisions and actions.

how.d: Weekly beats a quarterly marathon.
Open actions, blockers and decisions every time.
Who agreed to do what by when - your evidence and memory.
Roll highlights up on a cadence; this seeds your management review.

subs.t: Define and document the QMS scope
subs.why: Scope is the boundary of your whole system: which products, processes and sites it covers, and which standard requirements genuinely do not apply to you. Get it wrong and you either over-build or leave a hole an auditor will find.
subs.what: Define the QMS scope - products, processes, sites and regulatory frameworks - and justify any non-applicable requirements.

how.d: Products and variants, processes, sites, and the regulatory frameworks (MDR/IVDR, FDA, UKCA...) the QMS covers.
Manufacturer, importer or distributor - and your device class, which sets how much of the standard applies.
Any clause you exclude (e.g. sterilization, installation, servicing) needs a documented rationale; exclusions are only allowed where the requirement genuinely cannot apply.
Anything you outsource (e.g. manufacturing, sterilization) stays in scope and must be controlled.
Scope what you actually do today, not your five-year ambition - you can widen it later.

subs.why: The Quality Policy is leadership's public promise about quality. It sets the tone and is what objectives and behaviours are measured against.
subs.what: Draft a short Quality Policy appropriate to your organisation, approved by top management.

how.d: A few sentences staff can actually remember and mean - not a wall of text.
Meeting requirements, maintaining QMS effectiveness, and continually improving - the commitments the standard expects.

how.t: Make it a framework for objectives
how.d: The policy should be the umbrella your measurable quality objectives hang from.
Top management signs it; then make sure staff understand it, not just that it is posted.

subs.why: Objectives turn the policy into something measurable. Without them, 'quality' is just a word and you have nothing to review progress against.
subs.what: Define a small set of measurable, relevant quality objectives.

how.d: Objectives should make the quality policy concrete and measurable.
Three solid objectives beat ten vanity metrics; tie them to product quality and the QMS.
A number and a deadline, set at relevant functions and levels.
One owner per objective, reviewed at the quality meeting and in management review.

subs.t: Draft the lean QMS / Quality Manual
subs.why: The manual ties everything together - but it should be a lean map of your processes pointing to your SOPs, not a 200-page essay nobody reads.
subs.what: Draft a lean manual: a process map and the links between processes, pointing to the governing procedures.

how.d: Show the QMS processes and how they feed each other - the core of a process-based manual.

how.t: Point to procedures, don't repeat them
how.d: The manual references SOPs rather than duplicating their content - keep it lean.
Include the scope statement and the exclusion rationale here.
The hierarchy: manual -> SOPs -> work instructions -> records.
Update it as processes change, so it stays the true picture of how you work.

subs.why: An unapproved, uncommunicated manual and policy are just drafts. Formal approval and communication make them the real basis of the system.
subs.what: Approve the manual, policy and objectives, and communicate them to staff.

how.d: Top management signs and dates the manual, policy and objectives as released documents.
Brief people on the policy and what it means for their work - awareness, not just availability.
Hold them in the Released zone where everyone can reach the current version.
Keep the signatures and briefing attendance as records.

activities.why: Management review is where Top Management judges whether the QMS is suitable, adequate and effective, using analysed data - audit results, complaints, CAPA, supplier and process performance, post-market and regulatory inputs - and decides on resources, changes and improvements (ISO 13485 5.6, 8.4). It is the formal closing of the loop and a mandatory record auditors always check.
activities.what: Run management review and the analysis of data that feeds it: define the inputs, collect and analyse data on QMS and product performance, hold the review at planned intervals, and record the outputs - decisions on suitability and effectiveness, improvements, resource and change actions.
activities.how:
1. Analyse quality/security/AI data and trends (MRV-FOR-01).
2. Hold the management review and decide actions (MRV-TPL-01).

steps.d: pull together the numbers: audit results, complaints, CAPA status, nonconformities, supplier and process performance, post-market data, regulatory changes (8.4).

steps.t: Look for trends, not just totals
steps.d: analyse the data for patterns - is something getting worse? A rising complaint rate matters far more than this month's count.
compile the inputs against a standard agenda, including progress on the quality objectives and follow-up from the last review.
sit down at planned intervals and genuinely assess: is the QMS suitable, adequate and effective? This is leadership's job, not something the QMR does alone (5.6).
decide on improvements, changes and the resources needed. A review with no decisions is just a status report.

steps.t: Record actions with owners and dates
steps.d: capture every decision and action with an owner and a due date, and track them to closure into the next review.

steps.t: Management reviews are held at planned intervals with minutes.
All required inputs (audits, complaints, CAPA, KPIs, feedback) are covered.
Outputs include decisions, actions and resource needs.
Actions from the previous review are followed up.
Quality objectives and KPIs are evaluated against target.

records: Management-review actions with owners and dates
tips: Management review is mandatory and auditors ALWAYS check it. A missing or token review signals leadership isn't really running the QMS - and that colours the whole audit.
It must be driven by analysed DATA, not vibes. 'Things seem fine' is not management review; trends, metrics and CAPA status are.
The output is decisions and actions, not minutes for their own sake. If nothing changed as a result, the review didn't do its job.
Don't let it become an annual panic. A standing agenda and data you already collect makes the review a 90-minute routine, not a week of scrambling.

lean.start: A short, data-driven review meeting with minutes and actions — quarterly is fine to start.
lean.evolve: Add richer dashboards, KPI trending and structured input packs as the system grows.
startDetail: The management-review inputs defined (audits, complaints, CAPA, PMS, NC, objectives, changes, feedback).
Data collected and analysed on QMS and product performance ahead of the meeting.
The review held at planned intervals with the right attendees, including top management.
Minutes recording decisions on the suitability, adequacy and effectiveness of the QMS.
Outputs captured as actions: improvements, resource needs and changes, with owners.
Actions tracked to closure and revisited at the next review.
evolveDetail: Live dashboards and KPI trending feeding the review.
A standard, structured input pack prepared each cycle.
Objective/target tracking with escalation on misses.

subs.why: Management review only works if it sees the whole picture. Gathering the required inputs turns it from a chat into a data-driven decision.
subs.what: Assemble the review inputs: audits, complaints, CAPA, KPIs, feedback, regulatory changes.

how.d: Audits, complaints, CAPA, KPIs, feedback, regulatory changes, supplier performance, prior actions.
Digestible data, not raw logs.
For follow-up at the review.
So nothing required is missed.

subs.why: Analysing trends - not just listing events - reveals whether the QMS is working and where to improve.
subs.what: Analyse quality data for trends in product, process and system performance.

how.d: Complaints, NCs, CAPA, KPIs - not just this period's count.
Are the quality objectives being met?
What is improving, what is worsening, what is new.
Turn data into 'so what'.

subs.why: The review is where top management judges QMS suitability and effectiveness and commits to improvements - a required, minuted event.
subs.what: Hold the management review, judge effectiveness, and decide actions and resources.

how.d: Their presence is required, not optional.
Is the QMS working and adequately resourced?
Improvements, changes to the QMS or product, resource needs.
The review record, with owners.

subs.why: Outputs nobody tracks are wishes. Following up the actions is what makes the review drive real change.
subs.what: Track the review's actions to completion and carry them into the next review.

how.d: For each review output.
Don't let outputs become wishes.
Confirm actions actually happened.
Report status at the next review.

activities.why: Internal audits are how the organisation checks itself before a Notified Body or the FDA does - ISO 13485 (8.2.4) requires planned audits at intervals based on status and importance, conducted by objective auditors, with findings driving correction and CAPA. A working internal-audit programme is direct evidence the QMS is monitored and improved, and it surfaces problems while they are still cheap to fix.
activities.what: Establish the internal-audit programme: a risk-based audit schedule covering all processes and applicable requirements, trained and independent auditors, an audit procedure (planning, conduct, reporting, follow-up), and the link from findings to correction and CAPA.
activities.how:
1. Build a risk-based annual audit programme (AUD-TPL-01).
2. Conduct audits with independent auditors; raise CAPAs.

steps.d: plan to audit every process across the cycle (usually a year), auditing the riskier and busier processes more often. A bare calendar with no rationale is itself a finding.
give auditors basic audit-skills training - gathering objective evidence, open questions, classifying findings. A competent external auditor is also fine.
assign auditors so nobody audits their own work. In a tiny team, swap (software lead audits document control, QMR audits design) or bring in someone external.
before each audit, define its scope and criteria and tell the process owner. Surprise audits are for inspectors, not for your own improvement system.
audit each process against the standard and your own procedures, gathering objective evidence (records, observations) - not opinions.
record findings as nonconformities (major/minor) or observations, with the evidence, and report to the process owner and management.
route findings into correction and CAPA and verify they're actually closed and effective. An audit with un-closed findings is worse than no audit.

steps.t: An internal-audit programme covers every QMS process over a defined cycle.
Auditors are independent of the area they audit.
Audit criteria and a checklist are defined for each audit.
Findings are recorded, graded and assigned to owners.
Corrective actions from audits are tracked to closure.
Audit results are fed into management review.

tips: Internal audit is your dress rehearsal. Every nonconformity YOU find and fix is one the Notified Body won't - run audits to find problems, not to look clean.
Independence is non-negotiable: nobody audits their own work. For small teams, cross-audit each other or use an external auditor for the area you own.
An audit programme with findings that never close is a red flag. Auditors check that your audits actually drive change, not just generate reports.
Audit against objective evidence, not impressions. 'I think it's fine' is not an audit; 'I sampled five records and two lacked approval' is.

lean.start: A simple annual audit plan and a checklist-based audit of each process, by someone not auditing their own work.
lean.evolve: Add risk-based scheduling, trained lead auditors and deeper sampling as the QMS matures.
startDetail: An audit programme/schedule covering every process and applicable requirement over a cycle.
Independent auditors — no one audits their own work.
An audit procedure covering planning, conduct, reporting and follow-up.
Checklists per process so audits are consistent and evidence-based.
Findings classified and routed to correction and CAPA.
Follow-up that verifies findings are actually closed.
evolveDetail: Risk-based scheduling so higher-risk processes are audited more often.
Trained/certified lead auditors and auditor-competence records.
Deeper sampling and process-based (vs clause-based) auditing.

subs.why: Internal audit is how you check your own system before a regulator does. A programme that covers every process over a defined cycle is what makes it systematic rather than random.
subs.what: Build an audit programme covering all QMS processes over a defined cycle, risk-prioritised.

how.t: Cover every process over the cycle
how.d: Each QMS process audited at least once per cycle (e.g. annual).
Audit riskier and previously-weak areas more often.
Competent in auditing and in the area.
Dates per process, kept as the audit programme.

subs.why: Preparation - clear criteria, a checklist, an auditor independent of the area - is what makes an audit objective and useful.
subs.what: Define the criteria and checklist for each audit and assign an independent auditor.

how.d: What the audit covers and what it checks against (SOPs, clauses).
To structure evidence-gathering.
Auditors do not audit their own work.
Scope and schedule agreed in advance.

subs.why: The audit gathers objective evidence; recording and grading findings is what makes them actionable.
subs.what: Conduct the audit, gather objective evidence, and record and grade the findings.

how.d: By sampling records, observation and interview.
Conformity to the SOPs and the standard.
Nonconformity (major/minor) vs observation.
With the evidence behind each finding.

subs.why: An audit with open findings achieves nothing. Tracking corrections to closure turns findings into improvement.
subs.what: Drive corrective action on findings and verify closure.

how.t: Assign owner & due date per finding
how.d: Someone accountable to close each.
Not just a quick patch - link to CAPA for systemic ones.
Confirm the cause is actually addressed.

how.t: Feed results & trends to management review
how.d: Audit outcomes inform the review.

subs.why: Before inviting a Notified Body, you audit the whole system yourself - a full internal audit is your dress rehearsal.
subs.what: Run a complete internal audit cycle covering the whole QMS.

how.d: A complete cycle covering every process - your dress rehearsal.
To find what the Notified Body would.
Better you find them than the NB.
Before the external audit.

subs.why: A management review just before certification confirms leadership has looked at the whole system and judged it ready.
subs.what: Hold a management review covering the full QMS and certification readiness.

how.d: All inputs, including the complete audit results.
Is the QMS effective and the evidence complete?
And the resources to do them.
A minuted readiness judgement.

subs.why: A documented readiness decision - all findings closed or risk-accepted - is what tells you it's time to invite the Notified Body.
subs.what: Close audit and review findings and make a documented certification-readiness decision.

how.d: Or formally risk-accept with justification.
The technical file and QMS are consistent.
Nothing missing before inviting the NB.
The certification-readiness sign-off.

activities.why: Regulations, standards and guidance change, and the device's own medical device file must stay current and complete - ISO 13485 (4.2.3) requires a medical device file per device type demonstrating conformity, and manufacturers must keep abreast of applicable requirements. Without active regulatory monitoring, the technical documentation silently goes out of date and conformity lapses.
activities.what: Set up regulatory monitoring and medical-device-file upkeep: a process to track changes in applicable regulations, standards and guidance, assess their impact, and keep the medical device file / technical documentation current, feeding changes into change management.
activities.how:
1. Maintain the watch-list (REG-LIS-01) and the external-documents register (DOC-LIS-06).
2. Assess the impact of changes and feed change control.

steps.d: list the regulations, standards and guidance that apply to your device and markets - this is your watch-list. You can't track what you haven't identified.

steps.t: Set up a way to hear about changes
steps.d: subscribe to the right sources (official journals, standards bodies, MDCG, competent authorities, notified-body bulletins) so changes don't blindside you.

steps.t: Review the landscape on a schedule
steps.d: check the watch-list periodically and log what changed. A simple monitoring log is enough - but it must be done regularly, not once.

steps.t: Assess the impact of each change
steps.d: when something changes, work out what it means for your device, its classification, your technical file and your QMS.
update the medical device file / technical documentation so it always demonstrates conformity to the CURRENT requirements, not last year's.
feed any required updates into change management (P2.11) so they're implemented in a controlled, evidenced way.

steps.t: A watch-list of applicable standards and regulations is maintained.
A periodic regulatory scan is performed and logged.
Regulatory changes are impact-assessed against the QMS and product.
The standard versions in use are current or their use is justified.

records: Updated medical device file / technical documentation
tips: Regulations don't stand still - MDR transition timelines, new MDCG guidance, updated standards. A device compliant two years ago may not be today if nobody's watching.
The medical device file is a living thing. The classic finding is a technical file frozen at launch while the device, the standards and the guidance all moved on.
You don't need to read everything - you need a watch-list and a habit. A quarterly scan of YOUR standards and authorities beats trying to boil the regulatory ocean.
Standards have transition periods. When a harmonised standard updates you usually have a window - track the deadline so you're not caught using a withdrawn version.

lean.start: A watch-list of your standards and regulations, a named PRRC/EU-rep, scanned quarterly and logged in one place.
lean.evolve: Add formal impact assessments and horizon-scanning subscriptions as your markets expand.
startDetail: A watch-list of the regulations, harmonised standards and guidance that apply to your device and markets.
A named PRRC (Person Responsible for Regulatory Compliance) with the required qualification, and an EU/UK authorised representative where needed.
A quarterly scan routine with a single log of what changed and the assessed impact.
A medical-device-file index per device type so conformity evidence stays findable and current.
A link into change management so regulatory changes trigger controlled updates.
evolveDetail: Formal, recorded impact assessments for each relevant change.
Standards/regulatory horizon-scanning subscriptions and alerts.
Per-market regulatory intelligence as you add geographies.

subs.why: Standards and regulations change, and you are responsible for keeping up. A watch-list of what applies to you is the basis for staying current.
subs.what: Compile a watch-list of the standards, regulations and guidance that apply to your device and markets.

how.d: Standards, regulations and guidance for your device and target markets.
The specific editions you rely on.
Who watches each source.
Which documents/processes depend on it.

subs.why: A scheduled scan stops you discovering a regulatory change too late. Logging it proves you are monitoring.
subs.what: Scan for changes to your watch-list on a schedule and log the results.

how.d: Quarterly is a sensible start; watch official sources and NB bulletins.
Sources and date - proof you are monitoring.
New or updated standards, regs and guidance.
Hand each change off to be assessed.

subs.why: A change only matters once you work out what it means for you. Impact-assessing each change turns monitoring into action.
subs.what: Assess each relevant change for its impact on your QMS, product and documentation.

how.d: On the QMS, product, technical file and labelling.
Transition dates usually apply - plan to meet them.
Actions, owners and timelines.
Where product or the file is affected.

subs.why: Standards and regulations are controlled documents too - you must use the correct versions and withdraw superseded ones.
subs.what: Control external documents so only current versions are in use.

how.d: Standards, regs and guidance you use, as controlled documents.
Know which version is in force.
So people use the right one.
From active use.

activities.why: Think of this as building the filing cabinet before you start filling it. Everything else you do - your manual, design files, risk file, audits - produces documents and records, and an auditor's very first question is always 'show me how you control them'. If procedures live in people's inboxes and records can be quietly edited, you cannot prove staff followed the current instructions or that your evidence is genuine - and that single gap can sink an audit. ISO 13485 says it plainly: documents must be reviewed and approved before use, carry a revision status, and old versions must be kept out of circulation (4.2.4); records must stay legible, findable and kept for a defined time (4.2.5). Doing this first saves you from re-doing everything later, because every activity that follows simply slots into the system you set up here. It is not glamorous, but it is the single highest-leverage thing you can do on day one.
activities.what: By the end of this activity you will have a real, working document system - not a concept. Concretely: (a) a simple naming scheme so every document has a unique ID, a type and one owner; (b) a controlled place to store them - a folder structure or a document tool - that separates 'released' from 'drafts' and controls who can edit; (c) clear rules for how long each kind of record is kept; and (d) your first released procedure, DOC-SOP-01 (Control of Documents and Records), plus the master templates every future document is built from. From here on, creating a controlled document is a five-minute routine instead of a debate.
activities.how:
1. Adopt the naming convention and class scheme (DOC-LIS-05).
2. Create the controlled folder structure with access rights; decide retention.
3. Release DOC-SOP-01 and the master templates.
activities.documents: DOC-SOP-01; DOC-TPL-01; DOC-TPL-02; DOC-TPL-03; DOC-FOR-01; DOC-LIS-01; DOC-LIS-03; DOC-LIS-04; DOC-LIS-05

steps.d: choose ONE controlled location - a structured shared drive is fine to start, you do not need an expensive eQMS yet. The rule that matters: one official place, never personal copies.
inside that location keep 'Released' (the official versions everyone uses) physically apart from 'Working / Drafts', so nobody ever works from an unapproved file by accident.

steps.t: Set who can edit vs read
steps.d: give edit rights on a released document only to its owner; everyone else gets read-only. This is what actually stops uncontrolled changes.

fix the ID pattern (Domain-Class-NN) and the document types - SOP (procedure), WI (work instruction), TPL (template), FOR (form), LIS (list/register), POL (policy), MAN (manual). Record it in DOC-LIS-05 so it stays consistent forever.
assign one owning role per document, so there is always a clear person responsible for keeping it current.

agree that every controlled document shows an ID, a revision (v1, v2...), an effective date and who approved it - usually in a header band. This is how anyone tells at a glance whether a document is current.
when a document is updated, the old version is moved to an archive and clearly marked obsolete/superseded, so it can never be mistaken for the live one.
for each type of record decide how long you keep it - take the longest of the device's lifetime, the legal/regulatory minimum and any contract. Write the rules down; you will be asked.
decide how you keep tabs on the standards and regulations you rely on (ISO 13485, MDR, etc.) so you always work to the current version - a simple external-documents list does it.

adapt and approve the Control of Documents & Records procedure - it writes down everything above (create -> review -> approve -> issue -> change -> withdraw, plus how records are protected). This is your first 'released' document and a good test of the system.
issue DOC-TPL-01/02/03 (header band, approval block, change-history) so every future document inherits the same controlled structure automatically.

steps.t: Log it in the Document List
steps.d: record the SOP and templates in your master Document List (DOC-LIS-04) with their status, so your index is true from day one.

show everyone where controlled documents live, how to spot released vs draft, and the golden rule: only ever use the controlled copy. Keep a short attendance record - that record is itself your first piece of evidence.
approve the procedure and provide the storage and access controls it needs - a small but real commitment that signals quality matters here.
confirm in writing that the QMR can control documents and records across every part of the company, so document control is not optional for any team.

steps.t: A master document list shows every controlled document with its current version and status.
Released documents are kept separate from drafts and obsolete versions.
Each controlled document has a named approver, an effective date and a review date.
Records are legible, retrievable and protected for the defined retention period.
Obsolete documents are withdrawn from use or clearly marked.

records: Approved DOC-SOP-01 with an initial revision-history entry
Document list / master index (DOC-LIS-04) seeded with the planned documents and their status
Controlled folder (or DMS) structure with a documented access-rights matrix
Retention and disposal schedule per record type
Awareness / briefing record confirming staff were trained on document control
tips: The #1 audit finding in this area is people working from uncontrolled copies - email attachments, a file on someone's desktop, a printout from last month. Make the controlled location the only source of truth and this whole problem disappears.

Don't over-build. A tidy folder structure with access rights and a header band fully satisfies the requirement - move to a paid eQMS later when volume justifies it. Auditors care that it is controlled, not that it is expensive.

Documents (your instructions) and records (your evidence of what happened) are controlled differently: instructions get revised; records must NOT be altered after the fact. Mixing the two up is a classic beginner mistake.
Set retention rules BEFORE you start generating records, not after - reconstructing 'how long do we keep this?' across years of files is painful and avoidable.
Quick self-test: ask someone to find the current version of any document, its approval, and the version before it. If they can do it in under a minute, your document control is working.

lean.start: A shared drive split into 'Released' and 'Drafts', a one-page DOC-SOP-01, and the master templates. That is already a compliant document system.
lean.evolve: Add an eQMS, granular access roles, automated version control and formal periodic document reviews as your document count and team grow.
startDetail: A naming scheme giving every document a unique ID, a type (SOP/WI/TPL/FOR…) and one named owner.
A controlled store that separates 'Released' from 'Drafts' and limits who can edit released files.
DOC-SOP-01 (Control of Documents & Records): review/approval before use, revision status, and withdrawal of obsolete versions.
Record-retention rules per record type — how long each is kept and where.
The master templates every future document is built from, so creating a controlled doc is a five-minute routine.
A document/register index so nothing is invisible to an auditor.
evolveDetail: An eQMS with enforced review/approval workflows and audit trails.
Granular role-based access and automated version control.
Scheduled periodic document reviews so content doesn't silently age.

subs.t: Set up the document hierarchy & storage
subs.why: Before you write a single SOP you need somewhere controlled to keep documents, so versions never get confused. A clear split between what is released, what is in draft and what is obsolete is the whole game of document control.
subs.what: Create the storage structure - a shared drive or eQMS - that separates Released, Drafts and Obsolete, with controlled access.

how.d: A well-structured shared drive with locked folders is fine to start; move to an eQMS when volume or audit pressure justifies it.
Released (the only versions anyone uses), Drafts (work in progress), Obsolete (superseded versions kept for history, clearly stamped).
A folder structure by process or document type so anyone can find a document without asking.
Everyone reads Released; owners edit Drafts; only the approver can move a document into Released.
Header or watermark each document with its status and version so a printout is never mistaken for current.

how.t: Decide where masters live & back up
how.d: Define the home of the master copies and how they are backed up - this feeds record control.

subs.why: The SOP that governs all other SOPs has to exist first. It is what an auditor checks to see your system is real, not ad hoc.
subs.what: Adapt DOC-SOP-01 to how you actually create, review, approve, version and withdraw documents.

how.d: Take DOC-SOP-01 and cut anything that does not apply at your size - a lean SOP people follow beats a thorough one they ignore.
Draft -> review -> approve -> release -> periodic review -> obsolete, naming who acts at each step.
A simple, consistent ID (e.g. DOC-SOP-01) and version rule (minor vs major) that everyone applies.
Who authors, who reviews, who approves and releases - and keep approver and author separate where it matters.
How a change is requested, assessed, approved and re-released, with a change history on each document.
How you control the standards/regulations you rely on, and how obsolete versions are withdrawn yet retained.

subs.why: Consistent headers and a standard layout make documents look controlled and make review faster. It also stops people inventing their own formats.
subs.what: Prepare the controlled templates - the document header/footer, the SOP template and the form template.

how.d: Document ID, title, version, effective date, approver and page x of y - on every controlled document.
From DOC-TPL-01/02, with standard sections: purpose, scope, responsibilities, procedure, records.
From DOC-TPL-03 so records carry the same identification and version control as documents.
A table on each template capturing version, date, author and a summary of the change.
Keep master templates read-only in Released so everyone starts from the same controlled base.

subs.why: Records are your evidence that the system actually ran. If they are not identifiable, retrievable and protected for long enough, you cannot prove conformity - or defend the device later.
subs.what: Define how records are identified, stored, protected, retrieved, retained and disposed of.

how.t: Define what counts as a record
how.d: The evidence each process produces (training logs, review minutes, test results) versus working documents.
Match the regulatory minimum - typically device lifetime plus the years your regulation requires; longer for implantables.
A logical, indexed structure so any record can be produced on request during an audit.
Backups for electronic records, controlled access, and protection from fire/water/damage for paper.
How records are destroyed once retention expires - especially those holding personal or health data.

subs.t: Roll out and brief the team
subs.why: A document system only works if people use it. A short briefing prevents the most common failure: staff keeping private copies that drift out of date.
subs.what: Brief everyone on where controlled documents live, how to propose a change, and the rule that only Released versions are used.

how.d: Walk the team through the three zones, the document-control SOP, and how to request a change.

how.t: Show how to find the current version
how.d: Finding the Released copy must be effortless - that is what removes the excuse for private copies.
Make it explicit: the only valid version is the Released one; local copies are uncontrolled and not allowed.

how.t: Explain how to propose a change
how.d: One clear route for raising a document change, so improvements flow through control, not around it.
Keep attendance or acknowledgement as a training record - your evidence of awareness.

subs.why: You cannot control documents you have not listed. The master list is the single place that says what documents exist, what version they are, and whether they are released - it is the backbone of document control.
subs.what: Create and populate the master Document List (DOC-LIS-04) with every planned document: ID, title, type, owner and status.

how.t: Seed it from this tool's library
how.d: Use the document library here as your planned document set - do not start from a blank sheet.

how.t: Capture the key fields per document
how.d: ID, title, type (SOP/WI/form/record), owner, version and status - one row each.
Planned -> drafting -> in review -> released -> obsolete, so the list shows real progress.

how.t: Make it the single source of truth
how.d: Every new or changed document updates this list first; nothing exists outside it.

how.t: Use it as your build backlog
how.d: The list doubles as what is left to write, review and release.

subs.why: A document with no owner rots. Assigning an owner and a review date is what keeps the system current instead of a one-off snapshot.
subs.what: Give each listed document a named owner and a review date.

how.d: One accountable person who keeps each document correct and current.

how.t: Set a review interval by risk
how.d: Higher-risk or fast-changing documents reviewed more often; a sensible default (e.g. 2-3 years) for the rest.
A concrete date per document, not just an interval.
Make overdue documents visible in the list so reviews actually happen.

subs.why: Quality and regulatory language is full of jargon. A shared glossary stops people meaning different things by the same word - a surprisingly common source of error and audit confusion.
subs.what: Compile a short glossary of the key terms and abbreviations your organisation uses.

how.d: Standards, roles, regulatory and device-specific terms your documents use.
In your own words - then use that exact meaning everywhere.
Where ISO/MDR define a term (e.g. 'nonconformity'), match it so you do not contradict the regulation.

how.t: Hold it as a controlled document
how.d: Version it and update as new terms appear.

subs.why: Mapping each document to the process and clause it serves shows your coverage at a glance - and reveals gaps (a clause with no document) before an auditor finds them.
subs.what: Link each document to the process it supports and the standard clauses it addresses.

how.t: Tag each document to its process
how.d: Which QMS process it belongs to (document control, design, CAPA...).

how.t: Tag each to the clauses it satisfies
how.d: The standard/regulation clause(s) it helps meet.
Any clause with no document, or process with no procedure, is a gap to close.
This mapping is your coverage proof - the same view this tool's matrix gives you.

activities.statement: Maintain the process map & external-documents register.
activities.why: A QMS is a living set of interacting processes and controlled documents. ISO 13485 4.1.2 requires the process interactions to be defined and maintained, and 4.2.3-4.2.4 require control of documents and of external documents (standards, regulations, guidance). Keeping the process map current and the external-documents register up to date is how the QMS stays coherent and how you notice when a standard changes.
activities.what: Keep the process map (process interactions) and the external-documents register current: track applicable standards, regulations and guidance, flag changes, and ensure the process interactions reflect how the organisation actually works.
activities.how:
1. Maintain the watch-list (REG-LIS-01) and the external-documents register (DOC-LIS-06).
2. Assess the impact of changes and feed change control.

steps.d: Process interactions reflect current reality (4.1.2).
Standards, regulations, guidance in the register.
New/updated standards trigger review and CAPA.

tips: Keep the process map matching reality, not the org chart you wish you had - auditors compare it to practice.
The external-documents register is your early-warning system for standard/regulation changes - keep it live.
A new edition of a harmonised standard is a change - route it through change control and impact assessment.
Link the register to where each external document is used, so changes are actionable.

lean.start: A current process map and an external-documents register listing the applicable standards/regulations.
lean.evolve: Change-monitoring on external documents feeding impact assessment and CAPA.
startDetail: A process map showing the QMS processes and how they interact, matching how you really work.
An external-documents register listing the applicable standards, regulations and guidance (with versions).
A routine to flag when an external document changes.
The process map kept current as processes evolve, not left to drift.
A link from external-document changes into impact assessment and change control.
evolveDetail: Active change-monitoring/subscriptions on your key standards and regulations.
Structured impact assessments when an external document changes.
Process-map versioning tied to the QMS change log.

subs.why: The process interactions define how the QMS actually works.
subs.what: Keep the process map current with practice.

how.d: Matches how work is done.
New processes captured.

subs.why: Applicable standards and regulations change and must be tracked.
subs.what: Maintain the external-documents register.

how.d: Standards, regs, guidance.
Which edition applies.

subs.why: A changed standard can require QMS or product updates.
subs.what: Detect changes and route them to impact assessment.

how.d: New editions/regulations.
Change control / CAPA.

activities.why: People perform the QMS, so ISO 13485 requires that staff whose work affects quality are competent on the basis of education, training, skills and experience (6.2), and the regulations add specific competence expectations such as the PRRC. Defining roles, the competence each requires and keeping training records is how you both ensure and prove that the right people do the right work. Starting the records now means every later activity builds an evidence trail of who was trained on what.
activities.what: Define the QMS roles and the competence each requires, identify training needs, and set up personnel / training records capturing qualifications, training delivered and effectiveness. The output is a role-and-competence framework and a live training-record system.
activities.how:
1. Write job descriptions with required competence (HRT-TPL-01).
2. Open a training record per person (HRT-FOR-01); deliver onboarding.

steps.t: List the roles your QMS needs
steps.d: name the roles (QMR, designer, software lead, complaint handler, PRRC...). In a small company one person may hold several - that's fine, just make it explicit.

steps.t: Define what each role must know
steps.d: for each role, write the education, training, skills and experience it requires to do the job safely. Be specific enough to actually assess someone against it.
compare each person's real competence to their role's requirement and note the gaps honestly - this is for you, not for show.
for each gap, plan training, mentoring or hiring, and schedule it. A gap with no plan is a finding waiting to happen.

steps.t: Open a training file per person
steps.d: create a controlled record per person holding qualifications, the training they've had, dates, and whether it worked.
if EU-bound, document that your PRRC meets the Art 15 qualification requirements - a real expectation, not a formality.
record that training actually produced competence (a short check, a supervised task) - not merely that someone sat in a room.
make sure each person understands how their work affects the device and patients, and record that awareness.

steps.t: An organisation chart shows roles and reporting lines and is dated.
Each QMS-critical role has defined responsibilities and required competencies.
Training records, CVs or certificates are held for each staff member.
Job descriptions exist for quality-critical roles.

records: Per-person training records with effectiveness evaluation
tips: 'Competent on the basis of education, training, skills AND experience' - all four. A degree alone isn't competence; nor is a one-off course. Show the blend.
Effectiveness of training is the part everyone forgets - and the part auditors specifically check. Bake a quick 'did it work?' check into every training.
Keep training records current as people join and leave. The fastest finding there is: a new starter doing controlled work with no training record.
For tiny teams, be honest about single points of failure. If only one person can do a critical task, that's a competence AND a business-continuity risk.

lean.start: A simple role/competence table and a folder holding CVs and training certificates. That already meets ISO 13485 clause 6.2.
lean.evolve: Add structured competence assessment, training-effectiveness checks and a formal training matrix as the team grows.
startDetail: A role-and-competence table: each QMS role and the education/training/skills/experience it requires.
Job descriptions for the quality-critical roles, including the PRRC where applicable.
A training-needs list mapping people to the procedures and skills they must be trained on.
A personnel/training record folder holding CVs, qualifications and dated training evidence.
A record of who has been trained on each released procedure — the start of your evidence trail.
evolveDetail: A live training matrix linking roles → required training → status per person.
Competence assessment and training-effectiveness checks (not just attendance).
Onboarding and periodic re-training cycles tied to document changes.

subs.why: An organisation chart makes responsibilities and reporting lines explicit - the first thing an auditor asks for, and the basis for assigning QMS roles.
subs.what: Create a dated organisation chart showing roles and reporting lines.

how.d: Who reports to whom, including the management representative (QMR).
Make the QMR, and who owns each QMS process, visible on the chart.
Hold it as a controlled, dated document - auditors check it is current.
Revise it when people or reporting lines change; a stale chart undermines trust.

subs.why: Each QMS-critical role needs defined responsibilities and the competence it requires - so the right people do the right work and you can show staff are qualified.
subs.what: For each role, document its responsibilities and the competence (education, training, skills, experience) it requires.

how.t: List the roles, especially quality-critical ones
how.d: Roles whose work affects product quality or regulatory compliance.
What each role does and what it can decide - including authority to halt nonconforming product.
The education, training, skills and experience needed to perform the role safely.
One per role capturing the above, kept as controlled documents.
Where required, the Person Responsible for Regulatory Compliance (PRRC) and the QMR.

subs.why: Comparing required competence to what people actually have shows the training gaps - the basis of a realistic training plan.
subs.what: Assess each person against their role's required competence and identify training needs.

how.d: For each person against their role's required competence.
Concrete missing training, skills or experience - not a vague score.
Who needs what, by when - prioritising safety- and quality-critical gaps.

how.t: Decide how you'll check it worked
how.d: How you evaluate effectiveness (test, observation, sign-off), not just that training happened.

subs.why: You must be able to show each person is competent for their work. A per-staff file of CVs, job descriptions and training evidence is how you prove it.
subs.what: Create a personnel file per staff member holding CV, job description, competence and training evidence - stored securely.

how.d: One place holding CV, job description, competence evidence and training records.
Qualifications, certificates and on-the-job sign-offs that prove competence.
It holds personal data - restrict access and protect it (links to your GDPR duties).
Add new training and competence evidence as it happens, not at audit time.

subs.why: New staff are a common weak point - if they are not trained into the QMS, the system breaks at the edges. A simple onboarding routine fixes that.
subs.what: Define how new staff are introduced to the QMS and trained for their role before working unsupervised.

how.d: QMS basics, the quality policy, and where controlled documents live.
The procedures and competence the specific role needs.
Especially for safety- and quality-critical tasks - no solo work until competent.
Keep the onboarding and sign-offs as training records.

activities.why: Competence is not a one-off - ISO 13485 (6.2) requires the organisation to evaluate the effectiveness of training and the ongoing competence of staff, and to maintain awareness. Maturing competence means moving from 'training delivered' to 'competence demonstrated and effective', which is what keeps quality stable as people and processes evolve.
activities.what: Mature the competence system: evaluate the effectiveness of training already delivered, reassess competence against current role requirements, address gaps, and refresh awareness - keeping the training records and competence matrix current.
activities.how:
1. Evaluate effectiveness (HRT-FOR-02) and re-train where needed.
2. Maintain a current competence/training matrix (HRT-FOR-04).

steps.d: for training already delivered, evaluate whether it produced real competence (a supervised task, a short assessment) - not just that it happened.
as processes and the device evolve, re-assess each person's competence against their CURRENT role and spot new gaps.
deliver further training or mentoring for the gaps you find, and schedule it. A known gap with no plan is a finding.
refresh everyone's awareness of quality, safety and regulatory responsibilities - it fades, especially as new people join.

steps.t: Keep the records and matrix current
steps.d: update the competence matrix and training records so they reflect today's team and today's roles.

steps.t: Training effectiveness is evaluated, not just attendance.
Re-training and refresh needs are identified periodically.
Cover or succession for critical roles is considered.

records: Records of further training and awareness
tips: 'Training delivered' is not 'competence achieved'. This activity is about proving training WORKED - exactly what auditors check and most companies skip.
Competence drifts as the device and processes change. Someone competent for v1 may have gaps for v2 - re-assessment keeps the wheels on.
New starters are the most common competence finding. A robust onboarding-to-competence path (and its records) saves you here.
Watch single points of failure. If only one person can run a critical process, that's both a competence gap to close and a continuity risk (P3.4).

lean.start: A quick check that training worked and an up-to-date competence table.
lean.evolve: Add periodic re-assessment, development plans and succession cover as the team grows.
startDetail: An effectiveness check on training already delivered (did competence actually improve?).
Competence reassessed against current role requirements, not just the original ones.
Gaps identified and addressed with targeted training or reassignment.
Awareness refreshed where procedures or risks have changed.
The training records and competence matrix brought fully up to date.
evolveDetail: Periodic competence re-assessment on a defined cycle.
Individual development plans linked to role progression.
Succession/backup cover for critical roles (e.g. PRRC).

subs.why: Attendance is not competence. Evaluating whether training actually worked is what makes your competence claims credible.
subs.what: Evaluate whether training achieved the required competence, not just that it happened.

how.d: The competence the training was meant to deliver.
Can they now perform the task safely?
Assessment, observation, or work results.
Not just that training happened.

subs.why: The competence matrix is your live picture of who can do what. Keeping it current is how you spot and cover gaps.
subs.what: Keep the competence matrix current as people, roles and skills change.

how.d: Who is competent for what, live.
Quality-critical tasks without competent cover.
Update as the team and work change.
The matrix drives the training plan.

subs.why: Skills decay and requirements evolve. Periodically identifying re-training keeps competence from quietly lapsing.
subs.what: Identify re-training and development needs on a periodic basis.

how.d: Against current requirements and after changes.
For skills that have decayed or requirements that changed.
Quality, regulatory and security awareness on a cycle.
Growth for key staff.

subs.why: Single points of failure in people are a real risk. Planning cover for critical roles keeps the QMS running when someone leaves.
subs.what: Plan cover or succession for critical roles.

how.d: Where losing one person would hurt the QMS.
So critical tasks have cover.
For the QMR and other critical roles.
In both people and knowledge.

activities.why: Infrastructure - premises, utilities, equipment - and the computerised systems that run the QMS and production must be identified and controlled, because if any of them fails or behaves unexpectedly, product quality and data integrity are at risk. ISO 13485 requires the infrastructure needed for conformity to be determined and maintained (6.3) and software used in the QMS to be validated for its intended use (4.1.6). An inventory is the prerequisite for maintenance, validation and the later computerised-system validation (CSV).
activities.what: Build an inventory of the infrastructure and computerised systems that affect product quality or QMS data - equipment, utilities, IT systems, QMS / production software - recording owner, purpose, criticality and whether maintenance or validation is required. The output is a controlled asset / system inventory that drives maintenance and CSV.
activities.how:
1. List facilities, IT/cloud and equipment (INF-LIS-05).
2. List computerised systems and tools (INF-LIS-01).

steps.d: walk through your operation and list the premises, utilities and equipment that could affect product quality (workshop, fridge, test rig, calibrated tools...).
list every computerised system that runs the QMS or production - document control, eQMS, design tools, and the spreadsheets you use to make quality decisions.
note each item's intended use and the data it holds, so you can judge how much it actually matters.
classify each by impact on product quality and data integrity (high/medium/low). This tells you where to spend maintenance and validation effort.
for equipment, record what needs maintaining or calibrating and how often, so nothing critical drifts unnoticed.
mark which software systems need validation under 4.1.6 and hand that list to the CSV activity (P2.8). A spreadsheet that makes a quality decision counts.
manage the inventory as a controlled record so it stays current as you add or retire systems and equipment.

steps.t: An inventory of equipment and computerised systems exists with owner and criticality.
Each critical item has a maintenance or calibration plan.
Software systems affecting quality or data are flagged for validation.

records: Infrastructure / equipment inventory with maintenance needs
Computerised-system inventory with intended use and criticality
List of systems requiring validation (input to CSV)
tips: That spreadsheet you use to decide pass/fail? It's a computerised system that may need validating. People miss the humble tools, not the obvious ones.
You don't have to validate everything to the same depth - rate by risk. A high-impact production system gets rigorous validation; a low-impact convenience tool gets a light touch.
Calibration lapses are a classic finding. If a measuring tool affects a quality decision, it needs a calibration status you can show.
Cloud / SaaS tools still need control - you can often leverage the vendor's evidence, but you must decide and document that, not just assume it.

lean.start: A spreadsheet of your equipment and software with owner and criticality. The inventory is the prerequisite for maintenance and CSV.
lean.evolve: Add maintenance/calibration schedules, validation status and full asset-lifecycle tracking as operations scale.
startDetail: An asset/system inventory: equipment, utilities, IT systems and QMS/production software.
For each entry: owner, purpose, and criticality to product quality or data integrity.
A flag for whether maintenance/calibration and/or validation (CSV) is required.
The work-environment conditions that affect product quality identified (e.g. cleanliness, ESD).
Basic backup and access arrangements for systems holding QMS records.
evolveDetail: Scheduled maintenance and calibration with due-date tracking.
Recorded validation status (CSV) against each computerised system.
Full asset-lifecycle management from acquisition to retirement.

subs.why: You can only control what you know you have. Listing the infrastructure and equipment that affect product is the basis for maintenance, calibration and validation decisions.
subs.what: Build an inventory of facilities and equipment that affect product quality, with owner and criticality.

how.t: List items that affect product quality
how.d: Equipment, tools, facilities and utilities that touch the product or its quality.
So it can be identified, tracked and referenced in records.
Who is responsible, where it is, and how critical it is to quality.
In service, out of service, or due for maintenance/calibration.

subs.why: Software and computerised tools that affect product or data carry their own validation duties. You need the list before you can decide what to validate.
subs.what: List the computerised systems and software that affect product quality or data integrity.

how.d: From design tools to QMS software to production and test systems.
Product quality, data integrity, both, or neither.
Mark those holding records you must keep or that affect product - these need validation (CSV).
So you can manage updates and supplier risk.

subs.why: Not everything matters equally. Rating criticality lets you put effort where the risk is, instead of treating a label printer like a sterilizer.
subs.what: Assign each item a criticality rating and a responsible owner.

how.d: High / medium / low effect on product quality or data integrity.
A short reason, so the rating is defensible at audit.
One person responsible for each critical item.
Concentrate maintenance, calibration and validation on the high-criticality items.

subs.why: The inventory should drive action: what needs servicing, what needs calibrating, what needs validating. Flagging it now sets up the later activities.
subs.what: Mark which items need maintenance, calibration or validation, and feed them into the relevant activities.

how.d: Items needing scheduled upkeep, with an interval.
Instruments that measure or monitor product - these feed equipment control (7.6).
Software/systems (CSV) and special processes (process validation).
Who does each task and how often.
Push these into the maintenance/calibration plans so they actually get done.

activities.why: Computerised systems that run the QMS, design or production must do what they are supposed to, reliably and with data integrity. ISO 13485 requires validation of QMS software (4.1.6) and production / monitoring software (7.5.6, 7.6), and where electronic records or signatures are used, 21 CFR Part 11 applies. A risk-based CSV approach (IEC/TR 80002-2, GAMP 5) focuses effort where system failure would most affect product quality or data.
activities.what: Validate the computerised systems flagged in the inventory (P1.6) using a risk-based approach: validation planning, per-system requirements and risk assessment, installation / operational / performance qualification or equivalent testing, and ongoing control of changes - with data-integrity (ALCOA+) and Part 11 controls where applicable.
activities.how:
1. Risk-assess each system (INF-TPL-01).
2. Plan, test and report validation (INF-TPL-02/03/05).
activities.documents: INF-SOP-01; INF-LIS-01; INF-TPL-01; INF-TPL-02; INF-TPL-03; INF-TPL-05

steps.d: take the systems flagged for validation in the infrastructure inventory (P1.6) - that's your CSV worklist.
rank systems by their impact on product quality and data integrity, and decide how deeply to validate each (risk-based, GAMP 5 / IEC-TR 80002-2). Not everything needs the same rigour.
for each system, document what it's used for and the requirements it must meet - you can't test against a spec you never wrote.
assess what could go wrong if the system failed or gave wrong data, and set acceptance criteria accordingly.
test that it's installed right (IQ), operates correctly across its range (OQ) and performs in real use (PQ) - or leverage the vendor's evidence where justified.
apply access control and audit trails so records can't be quietly altered, and meet 21 CFR Part 11 if you use electronic records/signatures (ALCOA+ principles).
when a validated system changes (an update, a new config), assess and revalidate as needed - validation isn't a one-time event.

steps.t: Systems affecting product quality or data are identified and risk-ranked.
Validation depth matches each system's risk; vendor evidence is leveraged.
Audit trails are enabled where systems hold regulated data.

records: Validation plan and per-system risk assessments
Validation summaries and change / revalidation records
tips: Risk-based is the key word. You don't validate a calculator and a production controller to the same depth - match the effort to the consequence of failure.
You can often leverage the supplier's testing for commercial software - but you must decide and DOCUMENT that you're relying on it, and validate YOUR specific use. Don't just assume.
Data integrity (ALCOA+: attributable, legible, contemporaneous, original, accurate) is a hot audit topic. Audit trails that can be switched off, or shared logins, are classic findings.
Software updates can silently invalidate your validation. Have a change-control trigger so a vendor's auto-update doesn't quietly break a validated state.

lean.start: Validate only the systems that affect product quality or data, to a depth matched to their risk — leverage vendor evidence.
lean.evolve: Add periodic revalidation, audit trails and Part 11 controls as your computerised systems multiply.
startDetail: The systems from the inventory that affect product quality or data integrity identified as in-scope.
A risk-based validation plan: more rigour for higher-impact systems, less for low-risk.
Per-system intended-use and requirements captured as the basis for testing.
Installation / operational / performance testing (IQ/OQ/PQ) or an equivalent, leveraging vendor evidence.
Data-integrity (ALCOA+) and, where applicable, Part 11 controls checked.
Change control so validated systems stay validated.
evolveDetail: Periodic revalidation on a risk-based schedule.
Audit trails and access controls hardened across systems.
Full Part 11 / Annex 11 compliance where records are regulated.

subs.why: You do not validate every tool - only the computerised systems that affect product quality or data integrity, to a depth set by their risk. Ranking them first stops you wasting effort or missing a critical one.
subs.what: From the system inventory, identify which systems require validation and rank them by risk.

how.d: Only those affecting product quality or regulated data need validation.
Higher risk (release decisions, patient data) = deeper validation.
Light for low-risk tools; full IQ/OQ/PQ for high-risk ones.
Use supplier validation where you can justify trusting it.

subs.why: A short validation plan scaled to risk keeps the effort proportionate and gives you a clear pass/fail target - rather than testing everything or nothing.
subs.what: Write a validation plan per system: scope, approach, acceptance criteria, scaled to risk.

how.d: What you actually need the system to do correctly.
What is tested and how, scaled to risk.
What 'validated' means for this system - the pass/fail.
Use supplier qualification, then add your own use-case (PQ) testing.

subs.why: Running the planned tests against your real configuration and use cases produces the evidence that the system does what you rely on it to do.
subs.what: Execute the validation against the plan and record the results.

how.d: As you actually use it, with representative data.
Especially the functions that affect product or data.
Objective evidence per the plan.
Document, assess and resolve anything that fails.

subs.why: A system validated once drifts as it is updated. Defining revalidation triggers and audit trails keeps it trustworthy over its life.
subs.what: Define when revalidation is triggered and maintain audit trails for systems holding regulated data.

how.d: Updates, configuration changes, or a periodic interval.
Where the system holds regulated records (e.g. Part 11 data integrity).
Assess each change before applying it.
Re-test the affected functions and update the record.

activities.why: If you make decisions about product on a measurement - a gauge, a tester, a calibrated jig - then a drifting instrument quietly passes bad product or rejects good product. Calibration is how you trust your own measurements, and auditors check it because it underpins every other check you make.
activities.what: Set up a simple calibration system: list the equipment that affects product conformity, calibrate it against traceable standards on a schedule, and control what happens when something is found out of calibration.

steps.d: Take INF-SOP-02 and scope it to the instruments that actually affect product decisions - not every tool in the building.
List each item in INF-LIS-03 with its calibration interval, range and traceable reference standard.
