Calibrate or verify each instrument against a traceable standard before use and at the defined interval; record results on INF-FOR-01.
Mark calibration status so anyone can see an instrument is in date, and protect instruments from adjustment that would invalidate it.
If an instrument is found out of calibration, assess and record the impact on product already measured with it, and act on anything affected.

steps.t: A calibration register lists the due date for each instrument that affects product.
Out-of-tolerance findings trigger an impact assessment on product already measured.

refs: 7.6 Control of monitoring and measuring equipment

tips: The out-of-tolerance impact assessment is the step people forget - a failed instrument means questioning everything it measured since it last passed.
Calibrate to traceable standards (national/ISO), and keep the certificate - 'we checked it ourselves' is not traceability.
Scope it tightly: software-only products usually have little or none of this; record that rather than inventing it.

lean.start: A spreadsheet of the few instruments that affect product, with due dates, plus their calibration certificates in a folder.
lean.evolve: Add calibration-management software, automated due-date alerts and in-house calibration capability as the equipment base grows.
startDetail: A list of the measuring/monitoring equipment that affects product conformity.
Calibration against traceable reference standards on a defined schedule.
Due-date tracking so nothing drifts out of calibration unnoticed.
A rule for out-of-calibration findings: assess product measured since the last good calibration.
Calibration certificates and records retained and findable.
evolveDetail: Calibration-management software with automated due-date alerts.
In-house calibration capability with its own traceability.
Measurement-systems analysis for critical measurements.

subs.why: Calibration is how you trust your own measurements - start with the instruments that actually affect product decisions.
subs.what: Adapt INF-SOP-02 and list the relevant instruments with intervals and traceable references.

how.t: Scope to instruments that decide product
how.d: Only those whose measurement affects a product decision need calibration.
Each instrument with range, interval and reference standard (INF-LIS-03).
And make overdue items visible.
References traceable to national/international standards.

subs.why: Calibrating against traceable standards and labelling status keeps drift from quietly passing bad product.
subs.what: Calibrate or verify each instrument against a traceable standard on schedule and mark its status.

how.d: On schedule, keeping the certificate.
What in-tolerance means for each instrument.
Including as-found readings before any adjustment (INF-FOR-01).
So in-date / out-of-service is visible at a glance.

subs.why: A failed instrument means questioning everything it measured since it last passed - the step people forget.
subs.what: When an instrument is found out of calibration, assess and act on the impact on product already measured.

how.d: Stop using it immediately.
Back to its last good calibration - the step people forget.
Re-measure, recall or accept with justification.
Document the impact assessment and fix the instrument.

activities.why: What you buy becomes part of your device, so ISO 13485 (7.4) and the FDA QMSR require suppliers to be evaluated and selected on their ability to meet requirements, purchasing information to define what is required, and incoming product to be verified. Poorly controlled suppliers are a leading cause of nonconformity and recalls; for critical suppliers, controls must be proportionate to the risk.
activities.what: Establish the purchasing and supplier-control process: criteria for evaluating and selecting suppliers, supplier approval and a controlled supplier list, quality / technical agreements for critical suppliers, defined purchasing information, verification of purchased product, and ongoing supplier monitoring and re-evaluation.
activities.how:
1. Evaluate and approve suppliers (PUR-FOR-01; PUR-LIS-01).
2. Conclude DPAs/QAAs; define incoming verification.
activities.documents: PUR-SOP-01; PUR-LIS-01; PUR-FOR-01; PUR-TPL-01; PUR-FOR-02; PUR-FOR-03

steps.d: list every supplier whose product or service can affect your device - components, sterilisation, contract manufacturing, test labs, key software. These are the ones you must control.
a critical-component maker matters far more than your stationery supplier. Rank by impact on the device so you spend control effort where it counts.
decide how you judge a supplier (certifications, samples, audit, track record) proportionate to their risk, and write it down.
assess suppliers against those criteria, approve the ones that pass, and keep an approved-supplier list. Only buy critical items from approved suppliers.

steps.t: Sign quality agreements for critical ones
steps.d: for critical suppliers, put a quality/technical agreement in place that obliges them to tell you BEFORE they change anything - silent supplier changes cause recalls.
make purchasing information unambiguous - exactly what you need, including quality requirements - so you get what the device actually requires.
check incoming product or services meet requirements by a method matched to risk (inspection, certificate of conformity, supplier audit).
track supplier performance, route supplier nonconformities through CAPA, and re-evaluate suppliers periodically on a risk basis (links to P3.6).

steps.t: An approved-supplier list holds evaluation evidence for critical suppliers.
Quality agreements are in place with critical suppliers.

records: Supplier evaluation / approval records and approved-supplier list
Quality / technical agreements for critical suppliers
tips: What you buy becomes part of your device - your supplier's nonconformity becomes yours, and so does the recall. Control critical suppliers like an extension of your own process.
The clause that saves you most pain is supplier change notification. A supplier 'improving' a material without telling you has triggered countless field actions - get it in the agreement.
Don't audit everyone equally. A risk-based approach (paper review for low-risk, on-site audit for critical) is both compliant and survivable for a small team.
A certificate on file is not verification of every shipment. Decide what incoming check each risk level needs, and actually do it.

lean.start: An approved-supplier list and a simple evaluation for your critical suppliers only.
lean.evolve: Add quality agreements, scorecards and on-site audits for critical suppliers as the supply chain grows.
startDetail: Criteria for evaluating and selecting suppliers, weighted to the risk each supplier carries.
An approved-supplier list — the controlled record of who you may buy from.
A first evaluation of the critical suppliers only (don't boil the ocean).
Defined purchasing information so suppliers know the exact requirements.
Verification of purchased product (incoming inspection or evidence review).
Quality / technical agreements for the critical suppliers, and a basis for ongoing monitoring.
evolveDetail: Quality/technical agreements extended across critical suppliers.
Supplier scorecards and periodic performance review.
On-site supplier audits for the highest-risk suppliers.

subs.why: Not all suppliers carry the same risk. Categorising them and setting evaluation criteria focuses control on the suppliers that can actually hurt your product or data.
subs.what: Categorise suppliers by the risk they pose and define the evaluation and control criteria for each category.

how.d: Critical (affect product or data) vs non-critical.
What a supplier must meet to be used.

how.t: Scale the control to the risk
how.d: More oversight and evidence for critical suppliers.
So nothing critical sits with an unmanaged supplier.

subs.why: Approving suppliers on evidence - not habit - is how you keep poor inputs out of your product. The approved-supplier list is the record that proves it.
subs.what: Evaluate and approve your critical suppliers and maintain the approved-supplier list.

how.d: Assess capability, quality system and track record.
Record why each supplier is approved - not habit.
Keep the ASL current; buy critical items only from approved suppliers.
The basis and interval for staying on the list.

subs.t: Put quality & data agreements in place
subs.why: A quality agreement nails down who is responsible for what - change notification, records, audits. For data processors it is also a GDPR requirement.
subs.what: Put quality agreements (and data-processing agreements where relevant) in place with critical suppliers.

how.d: Who owns quality, records and audit rights.
They must tell you before they change a product or process you rely on.
GDPR Art. 28 terms where a supplier handles personal data.
How problems are raised and what records they keep for you.

subs.why: Approval is not forever. Monitoring incoming quality and re-evaluating suppliers catches a drifting supplier before it becomes your nonconformity.
subs.what: Monitor supplier performance and re-evaluate them on a schedule and on change.

how.d: Check what suppliers deliver against requirements.
Nonconformities, on-time delivery, responsiveness.
Periodically and whenever a supplier changes.
Escalate, re-qualify or remove from the ASL.

activities.why: Approving a supplier once is not enough - ISO 13485 (7.4.1) requires suppliers to be monitored and re-evaluated, with action when they fail to meet requirements. Ongoing supplier surveillance catches drift in quality, change in ownership or process, and emerging supply risk before it reaches the product.
activities.what: Run ongoing supplier surveillance and re-evaluation: monitor supplier performance against agreed metrics, conduct periodic re-evaluation or audits on a risk basis, manage supplier changes and nonconformities, and update supplier status.
activities.how:
1. Monitor supplier performance.
2. Re-evaluate by criticality and update the list (PUR-LIS-01).

steps.d: monitor each critical supplier's quality and delivery against the metrics you agreed - on-time, in-spec, responsiveness. A scorecard makes drift visible.
re-evaluate or re-audit critical suppliers periodically on a risk basis - approval isn't forever.
if a supplier changes process, site or ownership, reassess them. Those changes are exactly when quality slips.
raise supplier nonconformities, push corrective action, and adjust their approved status (probation, removal) if they don't improve.
keep the approved-supplier list up to date so buyers always know who's cleared to supply critical items.

steps.t: Critical-supplier performance is reviewed on a schedule.
Suppliers are re-approved when they materially change.
Supplier nonconformities are tracked to resolution.

tips: Suppliers drift. The one that was excellent at qualification can slide - performance monitoring catches it before a bad batch reaches your device.
A supplier changing ownership or moving a factory is a classic trigger for quality problems. Make it a mandatory re-assessment, not an afterthought.
Match surveillance to risk. Your critical steriliser gets close attention; a low-risk supplier gets a light touch. Auditing all suppliers equally exhausts a small team for no benefit.
If a supplier keeps missing requirements, escalate - probation or removal. An approved list that never changes despite poor performance isn't a control.

lean.start: A simple performance check on your critical suppliers and re-approval when they change.
lean.evolve: Add scorecards, scheduled re-audits and supplier-development programmes as the supply base grows.
startDetail: Supplier performance monitored against agreed metrics (quality, delivery, responsiveness).
Periodic re-evaluation or re-audit on a risk basis — critical suppliers more often.
Supplier changes and nonconformities managed and recorded.
Supplier status on the approved list updated from what surveillance shows.
A trigger to CAPA / re-qualification when a supplier's performance drops.
evolveDetail: Supplier scorecards with quantified performance trends.
Scheduled re-audits driven by risk and performance.
Supplier-development programmes for strategic suppliers.

subs.why: Approval is a snapshot; performance drifts. Monitoring critical suppliers catches a slipping supplier before it becomes your problem.
subs.what: Monitor the performance of critical suppliers against agreed measures.

how.d: Quality, on-time delivery, responsiveness.
A simple recurring rating.
What their deliveries actually show.
Before they become your nonconformity.

subs.why: Re-evaluating suppliers on a schedule and when they change keeps approval meaningful over time.
subs.what: Re-evaluate critical suppliers periodically and when they materially change.

how.d: Critical suppliers on a defined interval.
When the supplier changes product, process or ownership.
Evidence for keeping (or removing) them.
Reflect the outcome.

subs.why: Supplier nonconformities are early warnings. Tracking them and feeding CAPA keeps poor inputs from recurring.
subs.what: Track supplier nonconformities and drive corrective action.

how.d: From incoming checks and from use.
Hold them to a response.
Confirm it worked.
And reflect repeat offenders in re-evaluation.

activities.why: Customer-related processes must be controlled too - ISO 13485 (7.2) requires the organisation to determine and review the requirements related to the product (including regulatory and customer requirements and the ability to meet them) before commitment, and to manage customer communication. Maturing sales, tendering and support ensures the organisation only commits to what it can lawfully and capably deliver, and that field feedback flows back.
activities.what: Mature the customer-related processes: determination and review of product / regulatory / customer requirements before order acceptance or tender, controlled customer communication (including advisory notices and feedback), and aftermarket support - integrated with complaints, PMS and CAPA.
activities.how:
1. Operate contract/feasibility review (SAL-SOP-01).
2. Route support feedback to complaints/PMS (SUP-SOP-01).

steps.t: Check you can deliver before you commit
steps.d: before accepting an order or bidding a tender, review the product, regulatory, customer and delivery requirements and confirm you can actually meet them (7.2.1/7.2.2). Don't promise what the QMS can't back.

steps.t: Only sell what is authorised, where it is authorised
steps.d: market the device only in markets where it's cleared/certified, and only for its intended purpose. Off-label promotion and pre-clearance selling are serious regulatory breaches.
manage enquiries, contracts, complaints and field communications through defined channels, so nothing important falls through the cracks (7.2.3).

steps.t: Be ready to issue advisory notices
steps.d: have a route to push a Field Safety Notice / advisory notice to customers FAST if a safety issue arises - speed matters when patients are at risk.
route customer feedback and field issues into complaints, PMS and CAPA, so what sales and support hear actually improves the device.

steps.t: Order / contract review confirms requirements can be met before acceptance.
A customer-feedback channel is defined and monitored.
An advisory-notice / field-action capability is defined.

records: Feedback routed to complaints / PMS / CAPA
tips: Selling before you're cleared, or promoting an off-label use, is one of the fastest routes to serious regulatory trouble. Sales enthusiasm must stay inside the authorised intended purpose.
Contract/requirement review before commitment isn't bureaucracy - it stops you promising a delivery, spec or market you can't lawfully or capably meet.
Your sales and support people hear the earliest signals from the field. If that feedback doesn't reach complaints/PMS, you're throwing away your best early-warning system.
Have the Field Safety Notice mechanism ready BEFORE you need it. When a safety issue hits, the speed of your advisory notice can be the difference that matters.

lean.start: A simple order/contract review to confirm you can deliver, plus one channel for customer feedback.
lean.evolve: Add tender processes, advisory-notice workflows and structured aftermarket support as sales grow.
startDetail: An order/contract review confirming product, regulatory and customer requirements can be met before acceptance.
A tender-review step where you bid, so commitments aren't made you can't meet.
A controlled customer-communication channel (enquiries, advisory notices, feedback).
A single feedback channel that routes into complaints, PMS and CAPA.
Aftermarket-support expectations defined for the customer.
evolveDetail: Formal tender / bid processes with requirement traceability.
Advisory-notice and FSN customer-communication workflows.
Structured aftermarket support with SLAs and feedback analysis.

subs.why: Before you accept an order you must be sure you can actually meet its requirements - contract review is that check.
subs.what: Establish a simple order/contract review to confirm requirements can be met before acceptance.

how.d: Can we actually deliver what is asked, including regulatory ones?
Before accepting the order.
Agree changes before acceptance.
Evidence the check happened.

subs.t: Set up the customer feedback channel
subs.why: Feedback is a required PMS input and a source of improvement. One clear channel is enough to start.
subs.what: Set up a channel to capture customer feedback and route it into PMS.

how.d: A known route for customer feedback.
All feedback is a PMS input.
Feedback feeds surveillance and improvement.
Act on it and respond to the customer.

subs.t: Set up advisory notices & field actions
subs.why: When a fielded device needs a correction, you must be able to reach users fast. Defining the capability before you need it is essential.
subs.what: Define how you issue advisory notices and execute field actions.

how.d: How to issue an advisory notice / field safety notice.
Know who has which devices/lots, so you can reach them.
Field actions are often reportable.
Rehearse so you can reach users quickly when it counts.

activities.why: Support and servicing keep the device safe and performing in the field, and they are a controlled activity: ISO 13485 7.5.4 requires servicing to be planned and performed to specified requirements, with records. Servicing and support interactions are also a rich source of complaints, use problems and field data that must feed back into PMS, complaints and risk - so support is both a duty of care and a feedback channel.
activities.what: Establish the support and servicing process: service requirements and instructions, competent servicing with records, and the feedback path that routes support/service findings into complaints, PMS and risk.
activities.how:
1. Operate contract/feasibility review (SAL-SOP-01).
2. Route support feedback to complaints/PMS (SUP-SOP-01).

steps.d: What servicing is needed, by whom, to what spec (7.5.4).
Competent servicing/support with documented records.
Support/service data into complaints, PMS and risk.

tips: Servicing is a controlled activity - plan it and keep records; ad-hoc servicing is an audit finding.
Support and service interactions are a goldmine of field data - route them into PMS and complaints.
A recurring service issue is often a design or risk signal - escalate it, don't just keep fixing.
Define who is competent to service - untrained servicing is a safety and liability risk.

lean.start: A servicing SOP with requirements and records, and a path from support into complaints/PMS.
lean.evolve: Analysed service data driving design improvements and preventive action.
startDetail: Service requirements and instructions defined for the device.
Competent servicing performed, with service records kept.
A feedback path routing support/service findings into complaints, PMS and risk.
A way to tell a complaint apart from routine service, so reportable events aren't missed.
Spare-parts / configuration control where servicing changes the device.
evolveDetail: Service data analysed for recurring failures feeding design improvement.
Preventive-maintenance programmes where applicable.
Field-service performance metrics and trend review.

subs.why: Controlled servicing keeps devices safe in the field.
subs.what: Specify servicing needs, competence and instructions.

how.d: What, by whom, to what spec.
Who may service.

subs.why: Records are required and enable traceability.
subs.what: Provide servicing/support with documented records.

how.d: What was done.
Tracked resolution.

subs.why: Support/service data is a primary post-market signal.
subs.what: Route findings into complaints, PMS and risk.

Design/risk implications.

activities.statement: ISMS scope, risk assessment & access control.
activities.why: Connected and software devices face cybersecurity threats that are both a security and a safety problem. ISO 27001 provides the management system to govern information-security risk, and IEC 81001-5-1 (with MDCG 2019-16) requires security activities built into the product lifecycle - threat modelling, secure design, verification and vulnerability handling. Without these, devices ship with exploitable weaknesses and the organisation cannot protect its own and patients' data.
activities.what: Stand up the information security management system (ISMS) and the product-security lifecycle: an ISMS scope, policy, risk assessment and Statement of Applicability with controls, plus product-security activities - threat modelling, secure design and verification, SBOM and vulnerability management - integrated with design controls and risk management.
activities.how:
1. Define ISMS scope & SoA (ISM-TPL-01); assess infosec risk (ISM-TPL-02).
2. Threat-model the product; build the SBOM (SEC-LIS-01); plan security testing.
activities.documents: ISM-SOP-01; ISM-TPL-01; ISM-TPL-02; ISM-LIS-01; ISM-LIS-02; ISM-FOR-01

steps.d: decide what the information security management system covers (which systems, data, sites). A tight scope you actually run beats a broad one you don't.
a short, leadership-backed statement of how you manage information security - the anchor for everything below.
identify and assess information-security risks to your data and systems, and decide how you'll treat each (mitigate, accept, transfer).
document which ISO 27001 Annex A controls you apply and why (and why you exclude any). The SoA is the heart of an ISO 27001 audit.
for the device itself, model how an attacker could compromise it, and design security IN (IEC 81001-5-1) - not bolt it on later.
turn the threat model into concrete security requirements the software must meet, traceable to the threats.
keep a Software Bill of Materials - every component and version - so when a vulnerability hits a library you know in minutes whether you're affected.
establish how you monitor for, triage and patch vulnerabilities, and how someone can report one to you (coordinated disclosure).
build the security controls into the software and verify them in the software lifecycle, traceable to the threats they address.

steps.t: A security risk assessment covers the device and its data flows.
An SBOM (software bill of materials) is maintained.
Security controls are documented (Statement of Applicability or equivalent).

records: ISMS scope, policy and risk assessment
tips: For a connected device, security IS safety - a vulnerability can lead to patient harm. That's why MDR and the FDA now demand it, and why it lives in your QMS.
An SBOM is non-negotiable now. When the next Log4j-style vulnerability lands, companies that know their components patch in hours; the ones that don't scramble for weeks.
The Statement of Applicability is the document ISO 27001 auditors live in. Every Annex A control gets an 'applied because...' or 'excluded because...' - vague entries get picked apart.
Don't bolt security on at the end. Threat-model during design - retrofitting security into a finished product is far more expensive and far less effective.

lean.start: A short security policy, a basic risk assessment, and an SBOM — a lean ISMS scoped to what matters.
lean.evolve: Add the full Statement of Applicability, threat-modelling depth and certification (ISO 27001 / SOC 2) as you scale.
startDetail: An ISMS scope and a short information-security policy.
A basic information-security risk assessment over your key assets and data.
A Statement of Applicability listing the controls you apply (and why others don't).
Access control, backups and the other foundational controls in place.
An SBOM and the start of product-security activities (threat modelling, secure design).
The link into design controls and risk management so security risks reach the risk file.
evolveDetail: A complete, maintained Statement of Applicability.
Deeper threat modelling and secure-development verification.
External certification (ISO 27001 / SOC 2) as customers demand it.

subs.t: Define the ISMS scope & security policy
subs.why: A connected medical device or a company holding health data needs deliberate information security. Scoping a lean ISMS and writing a short policy sets the boundary and the intent without boiling the ocean.
subs.what: Define the scope of your information security management system and a short security policy.

how.d: Which systems, data flows and products it covers - keep it lean and real.
A signed statement of security intent and commitment.
Who owns security and who acts on incidents.
Measurable security objectives to steer the effort.

subs.why: Security risk assessment finds where you are exposed - the threats to the device and its data - so controls go where the real risk is.
subs.what: Assess security risks to the device and its data flows and decide on controls.

how.d: What is valuable - the device, its data, the supporting systems.
How an attacker could compromise confidentiality, integrity or availability.
Including impact on patient safety, not just on data.
From Annex A / secure-by-design measures, matched to the risk.
Security risks with safety impact also belong in the ISO 14971 file.

subs.why: An SBOM tells you what is in your software so you can react when a component is found vulnerable. Secure-by-design controls build protection into the product rather than bolting it on.
subs.what: Produce a software bill of materials and implement secure-by-design controls in the device.

how.d: Every software component and version, including SOUP, in a machine-readable form.
Authentication, encryption, least privilege, secure defaults, integrity checks.
A safe, authenticated way to patch it in the field.
Protect how the software is built and delivered.
Security controls that are also risk controls trace into the risk file.

subs.why: Security is never 'done'. Monitoring for vulnerabilities and having a tested incident-response plan is what keeps a fielded device safe over its life.
subs.what: Set up vulnerability monitoring and a security incident-response plan, linked to your CAPA and reporting duties.

how.t: Monitor your SBOM for new CVEs
how.d: Watch components for newly disclosed vulnerabilities over the device's life.
Detect, contain, eradicate, recover, communicate - with named roles.
A tabletop exercise so it works when it counts.
A security incident may be reportable and usually triggers corrective action.

activities.statement: Establish product security: SBOM, threat model & pen-test.
activities.why: Product security is now a safety requirement for connected and software devices. IEC 81001-5-1 and MDCG 2019-16 expect a security risk management process, a software bill of materials (SBOM) with SOUP/third-party components tracked, a threat model and security controls, penetration testing, and a coordinated vulnerability disclosure policy. Security weaknesses can become patient-safety hazards, so security and safety risk management must connect.
activities.what: Establish the product security process: build the SBOM/SOUP list, run security risk management and threat modelling, test via penetration testing, and publish a coordinated vulnerability disclosure policy - feeding the safety risk file.
activities.how:
1. Define ISMS scope & SoA (ISM-TPL-01); assess infosec risk (ISM-TPL-02).
2. Threat-model the product; build the SBOM (SEC-LIS-01); plan security testing.

steps.d: Component inventory (SOUP) and the threats/controls.
Security risk report; penetration testing.
Coordinated vulnerability disclosure route for researchers.

tips: Maintain an SBOM from day one - you cannot manage vulnerabilities in components you have not listed.
Connect security risk to safety risk (ISO 14971) - a vulnerability can be a patient-safety hazard.
Penetration test the real system, not a mock-up - and re-test after significant changes.
Publish a coordinated vulnerability disclosure policy - regulators expect a way for researchers to report.

lean.start: An SBOM, a threat model with controls, and a basic security risk assessment plus a disclosure route.
lean.evolve: Full security risk management, penetration testing and a mature CVD process feeding post-market surveillance.
startDetail: An SBOM / SOUP list of the components in the product.
A threat model identifying the attack surface and the controls that mitigate it.
A security risk assessment feeding into the safety risk file.
Security testing appropriate to the product (at least basic penetration testing).
A coordinated vulnerability-disclosure route so researchers can report safely.
Secure-design requirements captured as design inputs.
evolveDetail: Full product-security risk management integrated with safety risk.
Regular penetration testing and vulnerability management.
A mature coordinated-vulnerability-disclosure process feeding PMS and patching.

subs.why: You defend what you can see; the SBOM and threat model make risks visible.
subs.what: Inventory components (SOUP) and model the threats and controls.

how.d: SBOM/SOUP with versions.
Attack surface and controls.

subs.why: Testing and risk analysis turn the threat model into evidence.
subs.what: Assess security risk and run penetration testing.

how.d: Security risks that affect safety.
On the real system.

subs.why: A disclosure channel is expected and catches issues you would miss.
subs.what: Publish the CVD policy.

how.d: For external researchers.
Triage and response commitments.

activities.why: Cybersecurity is a lifecycle obligation. Once a connected or software device is on the market, MDCG 2019-16 and IEC 81001-5-1 expect ongoing security surveillance: monitoring new vulnerabilities (against your SBOM), coordinated vulnerability disclosure, assessing exploitability, and deploying patches and updates under change control. A newly disclosed vulnerability can turn a safe device unsafe - post-market security is how you keep it safe and demonstrate you are managing it.
activities.what: Operate post-market security surveillance per the plan: monitor threat intelligence and new vulnerabilities against the SBOM, run coordinated vulnerability disclosure, assess and prioritise exploitability, and deploy security updates under change control - feeding risk and, where safety-relevant, vigilance.
activities.how: [TO AUTHOR in Phase 2]

steps.d: Threat intel and CVEs against your component inventory.
Exploitability and patient-safety impact of each finding.
Security updates deployed; safety impact assessed; disclose coordinately.

tips: Monitor against your SBOM - you cannot track vulnerabilities in components you never listed.
Set up coordinated vulnerability disclosure (a channel for researchers to report) - regulators expect it.
A security patch is a change - run it through change control and assess safety impact before deploying.
A safety-relevant vulnerability can be a vigilance / field-action trigger, not just an IT ticket.

lean.start: A surveillance plan monitoring the SBOM for new vulnerabilities and a route to patch under change control.
lean.evolve: A mature PSIRT with coordinated disclosure, exploitability triage and safety-linked patch governance.
startDetail: A security-surveillance plan: the threat-intelligence and vulnerability feeds you watch.
New vulnerabilities matched against the SBOM to see what actually affects your product.
Exploitability assessed and prioritised — not every CVE is urgent.
A coordinated vulnerability-disclosure route kept open for external reporters.
Security updates deployed under change control, with regression/verification.
Safety-relevant vulnerabilities fed into risk and, where needed, vigilance.
evolveDetail: A product security incident response team (PSIRT) with defined SLAs.
Automated SBOM-to-CVE matching and exploitability triage.
Safety-linked patch governance tying security fixes to risk and vigilance.

subs.why: Surveillance is only possible against a known component inventory.
subs.what: Track threat intelligence and CVEs affecting your components.

how.d: New CVEs mapped to your build.
Advisories and disclosures.

subs.why: Not every vulnerability is exploitable or safety-relevant; prioritise on impact.
subs.what: Assess exploitability and patient-safety impact.

how.d: In your device's context.
Link to risk / vigilance where relevant.

subs.why: Uncontrolled patches can introduce new risk; coordinated disclosure builds trust.
subs.what: Deploy security updates through change control with disclosure.

how.d: Safety impact assessed.
Advisory to users where needed.

activities.why: A modern medical-device organisation processes personal and health data and runs connected systems, so data protection and information security are not optional add-ons - they are legal obligations under GDPR and, for connected or software devices, safety-relevant. Establishing a baseline now - the data you hold, foundational security controls and staff awareness - prevents breaches that would be both a regulatory and a safety problem, and seeds the full ISMS (P2.5) and data-protection operations (P2.6) built in Phase 2. GDPR requires security of processing (Art 32) and accountability (Art 24); ISO 27001 requires awareness (7.3) and a risk-based approach.
activities.what: Put in place the data-protection and security baseline: identify what personal / health data is processed and on what basis, apply foundational security controls, and run staff security & privacy awareness. The output is a documented data inventory, baseline controls and an awareness record the full ISMS and data-protection operations build on.
activities.how:
1. Issue the privacy baseline (DPR-SOP-01) and a privacy notice.
2. Run initial security-awareness training (HRT-FOR-04).

steps.t: Find out what data you hold
steps.d: list the personal and (especially) health data you process - what, why, where it flows, and the legal basis. You can't protect what you haven't mapped.
capture that mapping as the seed of your GDPR records of processing (Art 30) - it grows into P2.6.
apply the basics: who can access what, strong authentication, least privilege. Most breaches are an access-control failure, not exotic hacking.
set up backups for critical data and test that you can actually restore them. An untested backup is a hope, not a control.
make sure health/personal data is encrypted when it moves across networks - a cheap, high-impact baseline control.
agree who does what if data is lost or breached, including the 72-hour GDPR notification clock. Decide this calmly now, not mid-incident.
give everyone short security & privacy awareness (phishing, passwords, handling health data) and record attendance. People are the front line.
link these obligations to the QMS so they're maintained and audited like any other process, feeding the full ISMS (P2.5) and data-protection ops (P2.6) in Phase 2.

steps.t: An inventory of the personal data processed is documented.
Access to personal data is restricted on a need-to-know basis.
Backups are taken and a restore has been tested.
Staff have received basic data-protection awareness.

tips: Health data is 'special category' under GDPR - the bar is high and the fines are real. Treat it as the most sensitive thing you hold, because legally it is.
The 72-hour breach-notification clock is brutal. Decide NOW who assesses a breach and who notifies - improvising it during an incident is how deadlines get missed.
Security is a patient-safety issue for connected devices, not just an IT issue. A compromised device can harm someone - that's why it lives in your QMS.
Don't boil the ocean. A baseline (access control, backups, encryption, awareness) covers most risk now; the full ISMS comes in Phase 2. Start, then mature.

lean.start: A list of the personal data you hold, basic access control and backups, and a short staff awareness session.
lean.evolve: Build the full records of processing, DPIAs, processor agreements and a full ISMS as data and connectivity grow.
startDetail: A data inventory: what personal/health data is processed, where it lives, and the legal basis.
Foundational security controls over that data (access control, encryption, backups).
A privacy notice telling data subjects what you do with their data.
Retention rules — how long each data type is kept and when it is deleted.
A short staff privacy/security awareness session, with a record of attendance.
evolveDetail: Complete records of processing activities (ROPA).
DPIAs for high-risk processing and Art 28 processor agreements.
A full ISMS and privacy management system as data use scales.

subs.t: Map the personal & health data you hold
subs.why: You cannot protect data you have not mapped. Knowing what personal or health data you hold and where it flows is the first step of both GDPR compliance and security.
subs.what: Identify the personal and health data you process and how it flows through your systems.

how.d: What you collect and hold, flagging special-category (health) data.
Where it comes from, where it is stored, where it goes, and who can see it.
Why you process each dataset and your legal basis - health data needs a strong one.
Third parties that touch the data, and any transfers outside the EEA.

subs.t: Set up access control & basic security
subs.why: Most breaches are mundane: weak access control and unpatched systems. Basic measures - need-to-know access, strong authentication, updates - cover most of the real risk early.
subs.what: Put in place need-to-know access control and basic security measures for systems holding personal data.

how.d: Individual accounts, least privilege, no shared logins.
Strong passwords and MFA on systems holding personal/health data.
At rest and in transit where it matters.
Update the systems that hold or process the data.
So you can detect and investigate misuse.

subs.t: Set up backups & test a restore
subs.why: A backup you have never restored is a guess. Testing a restore is the difference between resilience and a nasty surprise on the worst day.
subs.what: Establish regular backups for critical data and verify a restore actually works.

how.t: Back up critical data on a schedule
how.d: Matched to how much data you can afford to lose (your recovery point).
Prove you can actually recover - a backup never restored is a guess.
Protected from the same failure (fire, ransomware).
How long copies are kept, consistent with your record-retention rules.

subs.why: People are the front line of security. A short awareness session prevents the phishing click and the careless data handling that cause most incidents.
subs.what: Give staff basic data-protection and security awareness, and record it.

how.d: Phishing, safe data handling, passwords, and how to report an incident.
Tailor the depth to who handles what data.
A one-off is not enough - refresh periodically.
Keep it as a training record (and GDPR awareness evidence).

activities.why: Beyond the baseline (P1.9), GDPR imposes concrete operational duties when you process personal and health data: maintain records of processing (Art 30), run data protection impact assessments for high-risk processing such as health data (Art 35), put processor agreements in place (Art 28), and build data protection in by design and default (Art 25). Health data is a special category, so the bar is high; failing here risks fines and loss of trust.
activities.what: Make data protection operational: maintain the records of processing activities, run DPIAs for high-risk processing, establish Art 28 processor/controller agreements, implement data-subject-rights handling, and embed privacy by design into the product and processes.
activities.how:
1. Build the ROPA (DPR-LIS-01); run DPIAs for high-risk processing (DPR-TPL-02).
2. Put DPAs/BAAs in place with processors (DPR-TPL-03).

steps.d: turn the P1.9 data map into a proper GDPR record of processing (Art 30): purposes, data categories, recipients, transfers and retention, for each processing activity.
identify processing that's high-risk (health data, large-scale, profiling) - this is what triggers a DPIA.
for high-risk processing, run a data protection impact assessment (Art 35): describe it, assess the risk to people, define mitigations. Health-data processing almost always needs one.
every vendor that processes personal data for you (cloud, analytics, support) needs a signed Art 28 data-processing agreement. No DPA, no sharing.
be clear who is controller and who is processor for each data flow - it determines who is responsible for what.
build a process to handle access, rectification, erasure, portability and objection requests within the legal deadlines (usually one month).
make data protection by design and default a standard requirement in your product and process design (Art 25), not an afterthought.

steps.t: Records of processing activities are maintained.
Processor / controller agreements are in place for third parties.
A DPIA is performed where processing is high-risk.

tips: Health data is special-category data - the strictest tier under GDPR. If you process it, a DPIA is almost certainly required, and 'we didn't think we needed one' is not a defence.
Every processor needs a signed Art 28 agreement BEFORE data flows. Adopting a new analytics or support tool without one is a common, easily-avoided breach.
Data-subject requests have hard legal deadlines (usually a month). Build the process now - scrambling to fulfil an erasure request manually across systems is painful and risky.
Privacy by design is cheaper than privacy by retrofit. Collecting less data and minimising by default removes whole categories of risk for free.

lean.start: Records of processing and processor agreements for your actual data flows, plus a DPIA where it is clearly needed.
lean.evolve: Add automated data-subject-request handling, privacy-by-design checklists and a privacy management system as data use grows.
startDetail: Records of processing activities (ROPA) maintained for your real data flows.

evolveDetail: Automated data-subject-request intake and tracking.

subs.why: GDPR requires you to document your processing. Keeping the records of processing current is the operational heart of data protection and the first thing a regulator asks for.
subs.what: Maintain records of processing activities for the personal and health data you handle.

how.d: Purpose, categories of data and people, recipients, retention and transfers (Art. 30).
For each activity - health (special-category) data needs an Art. 9 condition too.
Update whenever processing, systems or processors change.

subs.why: Anyone who processes personal data on your behalf must be bound by a contract. Missing processor agreements are a common, easily-found compliance gap.
subs.what: Put data-processing agreements in place with every processor that handles your data.

how.d: Cloud, analytics, support, sub-processors - anyone touching personal data.

subs.what: Run data protection impact assessments where processing is high-risk.

subs.why: People have rights over their data, and breaches must be handled on a clock. Having a working process for both keeps you compliant when it counts.
subs.what: Set up processes to handle data-subject requests and personal-data breaches within the required timeframes.

activities.statement: Establish the incident response plan & register.
activities.why: When a security incident happens, an unprepared team improvises and makes it worse. An incident response plan defines roles, detection, containment, eradication, recovery and post-incident review, with an incident register and reporting forms. For devices, incidents can be safety-relevant (feeding vigilance) and, for in-scope operators, subject to NIS2 reporting timelines. Preparedness is what turns a crisis into a managed event.
activities.what: Establish the incident response process: the plan (roles, phases, escalation), the incident register and reporting forms, and the post-incident review that feeds improvements and, where relevant, vigilance and regulatory reporting.
activities.how:
1. Operate CAPA (CAP-SOP-01) with root-cause and effectiveness checks.
2. Stand up incident & breach response (IRP-SOP-01) incl. the 72h breach path.

steps.d: Roles, detection, containment, eradication, recovery, escalation.
Incident register and security incident report forms.
Lessons learned feeding CAPA / vigilance.

tips: Assign roles before an incident - during one is too late to decide who does what.
A safety-relevant security incident can be a vigilance / field-action trigger, not just an IT event.
Know your reporting clocks (NIS2, GDPR 72h, vigilance) before you need them.
Run the post-incident review honestly - the register is only useful if it drives change.

lean.start: A response plan with roles and phases, an incident register, and a post-incident review step.
lean.evolve: Tested playbooks, tabletop exercises and reporting integrated with vigilance and privacy.

subs.why: A clear plan is what prevents an incident becoming a disaster.
subs.what: Define roles, phases and escalation.

subs.what: Review incidents and feed CAPA/vigilance.

activities.why: A personal-data breach carries hard legal deadlines: GDPR Art 33 requires notifying the supervisory authority within 72 hours of becoming aware where there is a risk to individuals, and Art 34 requires notifying affected data subjects where the risk is high. Missing the clock or under-assessing the risk is a common source of fines. For a health device this intersects with security incident response and, sometimes, vigilance.
activities.what: Establish the personal-data breach process: assessment of risk to individuals, the 72-hour authority notification (Art 33) and, where high-risk, data-subject notification (Art 34), using the breach notification form and integrated with incident response.
activities.how:
1. Operate CAPA (CAP-SOP-01) with root-cause and effectiveness checks.
2. Stand up incident & breach response (IRP-SOP-01) incl. the 72h breach path.

steps.d: Risk to individuals' rights and freedoms.
Supervisory authority per Art 33.
Per Art 34, without undue delay.

tips: The 72-hour clock starts when you become aware, not when you finish investigating - assess quickly.
Not every breach needs notifying, but you must document why if you decide not to.
High-risk breaches also require telling the individuals - plan the communication.
Wire this into incident response so a security incident automatically triggers the privacy assessment.

lean.start: A breach assessment and notification form with the Art 33/34 decision path and the 72h clock understood.
lean.evolve: Drilled process integrated with incident response and vigilance, with template notifications ready.
startDetail: A breach-assessment step: is personal data involved and what is the risk to individuals?

evolveDetail: A drilled breach process rehearsed against realistic scenarios.

activities.why: If an outage, cyber-incident or disaster takes out the systems or premises the QMS and production depend on, the organisation must be able to keep operating or recover quickly - ISO 27001 (A.5.29-30) requires information-security continuity and ICT readiness for business continuity. For connected and software devices, continuity is also a patient-safety and obligation-to-supply concern.
activities.what: Establish business continuity and disaster recovery: a business-impact analysis of critical processes and systems, continuity and recovery plans with defined RTO/RPO, backup and redundancy measures, and testing of the plans.
activities.how:
1. Do the business impact analysis (BCP-TPL-01): RTO/RPO.
2. Write and test the BC/DR plan (BCP-TPL-02; BCP-FOR-01).

steps.d: identify the processes and systems you can't operate without (production, document control, the eQMS, key data). These are what continuity protects.
for each critical thing, decide the most downtime you can tolerate (Recovery Time Objective) and the most data you can afford to lose (Recovery Point Objective).

steps.t: Plan how you keep going or recover
steps.d: write continuity and disaster-recovery procedures - what you do if a system, site or supplier goes down - proportionate to the impact.
put backups, redundancy and alternatives in place to meet your RTO/RPO. A single point of failure with no fallback is a continuity gap.
run an exercise - restore a backup, simulate an outage. An untested plan fails exactly when you need it. Feed the lessons into CAPA.
link continuity to the ISMS and incident management (P2.5, P2.10) so it's governed and audited like any other control.

steps.t: Critical systems and their recovery requirements are identified.
Backups are tested and a continuity plan is documented.
The continuity plan has been exercised at least once.

tips: An untested backup is a hope, not a control. The only way to know you can recover is to actually restore - test it before you need it for real.
Continuity is also an obligation to supply. If patients depend on your device, an outage that stops you supplying it is a safety and regulatory issue, not just an inconvenience.
RTO and RPO turn 'we should be resilient' into measurable targets you can design and test against. Without them, continuity plans drift into wishful thinking.
Don't gold-plate. A small SaaS-based company's continuity plan can be a page or two - the point is that you've thought it through and tested it, not that it's thick.

lean.start: Tested backups and a one-page 'what we do if X goes down' plan for your critical systems.
lean.evolve: Add a full business-impact analysis, RTO/RPO targets and regular continuity exercises as you scale.
startDetail: The critical processes and systems identified (a lightweight business-impact analysis).

evolveDetail: A full business-impact analysis across the organisation.
Formal RTO/RPO targets per system with monitoring.
Regular continuity exercises and post-exercise improvements.

activities.why: If your device is electrically active or connects to mains or patient circuits, electrical and energy safety is a basic essential requirement - get it wrong and you can harm a patient directly. This is design-verification work: you prove safety against the recognised standard before you ship.
activities.what: Plan and run the active/electrical safety testing your device needs against IEC 60601-1 and the relevant collateral and particular standards, and keep the evidence in your technical file.

steps.d: Take AES-SOP-01 and scope it to your device's energy types (mains, battery, applied parts, RF).
Pin down the IEC 60601-1 edition plus the right collateral (-1-x) and particular (-2-xx) standards for your device type.
Use AES-TPL-01 to define the test plan and acceptance criteria.
Test in-house or at an accredited lab; record results on AES-FOR-01.
Log the test reports in AES-LIS-01 and link them to your GSPR / essential-requirements evidence.

tips: Accredited-lab slots book up - schedule electrical safety testing early or it delays your whole launch.
A design change after testing can invalidate it; assess the re-test need under change control.
The general standard alone is rarely enough - identify the particular standard (60601-2-xx) for your device type.

lean.start: For a low-energy device: the applicable standard's required tests run once at an accredited lab, plus the declaration, filed.
lean.evolve: Add in-house pre-compliance testing and broader particular-standard coverage as the product range grows.

A test plan mapping each required test to your device's features and energy sources.
Testing run at an accredited lab (for a low-energy device, typically one pass).
The test report and declaration filed in the technical file as design-verification evidence.
Any deviations or applied justifications recorded against the standard clauses.
evolveDetail: In-house pre-compliance testing to catch failures before the accredited lab.
Broader collateral / particular-standard coverage as features expand.

activities.why: If any part of your device touches the patient - skin, mucosa, blood, tissue - the materials themselves can cause harm. Biological evaluation is how you show, risk-based, that body contact is safe. It only applies if you have patient contact; if not, record that and move on.
activities.what: Run a risk-based biological evaluation per ISO 10993-1: categorise the body contact, assess each biological endpoint from materials and existing data, and test only where a real gap remains.

steps.d: Take BIO-SOP-01 and categorise the nature and duration of body contact for your device.
Use BIO-TPL-01 to map the biological endpoints by contact category; gather material, supplier and literature data before testing.
Commission biological tests only where evidence is missing; record results on BIO-FOR-01.
Write the biological evaluation report (BIO-TPL-02) and log the studies in BIO-LIS-01.

refs: Biological evaluation within a risk-management process

tips: Biocompatibility is risk-based, not a fixed test list - chemical characterisation plus existing data can close many endpoints without animal testing.
Material or supplier changes can reopen the evaluation - tie it to change control.
Start early: biological testing has long lead times and can gate your submission.

lean.start: An evaluation plan that leans on material data and literature, testing only the genuine gaps.
lean.evolve: Add chemical characterisation, toxicological risk assessment and broader endpoint coverage as materials and claims expand.

activities.why: If your device incorporates a medicinal substance (a drug-device combination), you carry extra obligations - the medicinal part must be assessed, often with a medicines authority consulted. Skip this and your conformity route is invalid.
activities.what: Determine the combination-product status and apply the right controls to the medicinal constituent: characterise the substance and its role (ancillary vs principal mode of action), apply the medicinal-quality controls it needs, and manage the consultation/assessment route with a medicines authority or notified body (e.g. the EU MDR Article 117 opinion), keeping the evidence in the technical file.

steps.d: Take CMB-SOP-01 and confirm the combination-product classification and route.
Document the medicinal part's quality, safety and benefit.
Use CMB-TPL-01 to prepare the consultation for the competent / medicines authority where required.
Record the assessment and authority decisions on CMB-FOR-01.

refs: Article 117 (medicinal products with an integral device)

tips: Combination-product routes add real time and a second regulator - plan for it from the start.
Get the classification right early; misclassifying a combination product unravels the whole submission.
Keep the device and medicinal evidence consistent across both assessments.

activities.why: If you make devices to a specific practitioner's prescription for an individual patient (custom-made or patient-matched), you follow a distinct conformity route with its own statement and records - not the standard CE route. Using the wrong route is a compliance failure.
activities.what: Set up the custom-made device controls under the distinct conformity route: capture and control the practitioner's prescription, draw up the Annex XIII statement issued with each device (in place of a CE mark), keep the per-device design and manufacturing records, and maintain patient/device traceability and the retention the regulation requires.

steps.d: Take CMD-SOP-01 and confirm custom-made vs patient-matched status and the applicable route.
Record the practitioner's prescription and manufacture to it.
Issue the Annex XIII statement (CMD-TPL-01) for each device.
Hold per-device records on CMD-FOR-01 and patient/device traceability in CMD-LIS-01, retained for the required period.

tips: Custom-made is not a shortcut around safety - GSPRs still apply, only the conformity route differs.
Mass 'patient-matched' production may not qualify as custom-made - check the definition before relying on it.
Keep the prescription and the device statement together for every unit; auditors trace them.

lean.start: A prescription-capture form, a statement template and a simple per-device log, retained per the rules.
lean.evolve: Add digital prescription workflows and patient-matched design automation as volume grows.

activities.why: If you make physical product - in-house or through a contract manufacturer - you need a controlled, repeatable way to build every unit to the released specification, with full traceability. This is where your design becomes a real product, and where an auditor checks that what you ship is genuinely what you designed and validated.
activities.what: Stand up your production process: adapt the production SOP to how you actually manufacture, write the work instructions and the device history record, then build under controlled conditions with lot/serial traceability and in-process checks before release.
activities.documents: PRO-SOP-01; PRO-SOP-03; PRO-TPL-01; PRO-TPL-02; PRO-TPL-03; PRO-TPL-04; PRO-TPL-05; PRO-TPL-06; PRO-TPL-07

steps.t: Adapt the production SOP to your scope
steps.d: Take PRO-SOP-01 and cut it to how you really make the product - in-house, fully outsourced, or a mix. If a contract manufacturer builds it, your SOP mainly governs how you control, monitor and release their work.

steps.t: Create the work instructions & device history record
steps.d: Use PRO-TPL-01/02/03 to write the build instructions and the production / device-history record that travels with each batch or unit.
Check the work order, approved instructions, materials, equipment status and operator competence before you start.
Build to the released specification, keeping identification and lot/serial traceability of materials and product at every step.
Perform the in-process controls at the defined points; record results against the acceptance criteria and segregate anything nonconforming.
Reconcile quantities, complete the device history record, have QC review it for completeness and conformity, and let the authorised person release or reject the batch.

steps.t: Each batch or unit has a completed device history record.
Lot/serial traceability is demonstrable from any finished unit.

tips: Outsourcing manufacturing does not outsource responsibility - you still own release and traceability; nail it down in a quality agreement with the maker.
Traceability is what auditors test hardest: take any finished unit and you should be able to trace every material, step and check back from it.
Don't write instructions you won't follow - a short instruction that matches reality beats a detailed one that doesn't.

lean.start: One clear work instruction and a one-page device-history record per build, with lot/serial traceability. That is already a controlled production process.
lean.evolve: Add MES/ERP tooling, statistical process control, automated traceability and supplier scorecards as volume and the number of products grow.

A device history record (DHR) capturing what was built, by whom, to which spec/version.

activities.why: If your device is supplied sterile, sterility and the sterile barrier are safety-critical and cannot be inspected into the product - they have to be validated and controlled every batch. This activity only applies if you actually sterilize; if you don't, record that as a justified non-application and move on.
activities.what: Adopt your sterilization and sterile-barrier packaging controls: choose and validate the sterilization process (e.g. EO to ISO 11135, radiation to ISO 11137, moist heat to ISO 17665) and the packaging (ISO 11607), then control every routine cycle with parametric or dosimetric release records — because sterility can't be inspected into the product after the fact.

steps.d: Take STE-SOP-01, choose your method (EO, radiation, steam, or outsourced) and scope the controls. If you outsource sterilization, your SOP governs the contractor and the incoming/outgoing checks.
Use STE-TPL-01/02 to specify the sterile-barrier system and the sterilization method and parameters.
Validate the sterilization process (to the relevant ISO 11135/11137/17665) and the packaging seal integrity and shelf life (EN ISO 11607).
Run every batch to the validated parameters, with cycle monitoring and the release records on STE-FOR-01.
Release sterile product only against the cycle records and required indicators/test results; segregate any failed cycle.

steps.t: Sterilization and the sterile-barrier packaging are validated.
Each sterile batch is released on its cycle records.

tips: Packaging validation is not optional - a validated sterilization with an unproven seal still fails the patient.
Outsourced sterilization still needs your oversight: a quality agreement, parameter review and release on the contractor's records.
Shelf-life / ageing evidence takes real time - start it early or it becomes your launch bottleneck.

lean.start: If you outsource sterilization: a quality agreement, the contractor's validation, and release on their cycle records. That is a controlled sterile process.
lean.evolve: Bring monitoring in-house, add dosimetric release and richer ageing studies as volume and product range grow.

activities.why: Sooner or later something contaminated turns up - a return from the field, a spill, a suspected-infected unit. You need a safe, pre-agreed way to contain it so it protects people and can never quietly re-enter usable stock. Without it, a single contaminated return can put staff at risk and pollute good inventory.
activities.what: Adopt a procedure to identify, segregate and safely handle contaminated or suspect product — most often a return from the field or a spill: contain it so it protects staff, decide its disposition (decontaminate, quarantine, destroy), record it, and make sure it can never quietly re-enter usable stock, feeding any signal into complaints and CAPA.

steps.d: Take PSA-SOP-01 and tailor it to your realistic sources of contamination (field returns, production spills, servicing).
Immediately identify, physically segregate and clearly label affected product as contaminated / quarantined.
Assess the nature, extent and risk; obtain authorisation for decontamination, rework or controlled disposal.
Carry out the decontamination or disposal so that contaminated product cannot re-enter usable stock, and protect the people doing it.
Record the event and route any systemic cause into CAPA before closing it out.

tips: The non-negotiable rule: contaminated returns can never re-join usable stock - a separate, labelled quarantine area enforces that.
Tell staff how to receive and handle a contaminated return safely before one arrives, not after.
Recurring contamination is a CAPA trigger, not just a clean-up job.

1.q: Which document must be established FIRST, before any other QMS document?
1.e: Document & record control is the foundation every other activity relies on - activity P1.1.

1.q: Two activities sharing the same 'Wave' within a phase means they:
a: Must be done in strict sequence
1.e: A wave groups activities with no dependency between them, so they can run in parallel.

1.q: What is the focus of Phase 1?
a: Prepare for the Notified Body audit
Ensure the core processes operate correctly & safely
1.e: Phase 1 gets the primary process running correctly and safely before anything else.

1.q: ISO 13485 clause 5 makes which of these an explicit requirement, not optional good practice?
a: Management commitment, a quality policy, measurable objectives and a management representative
1.e: Clause 5 requires top-management commitment, the quality policy, measurable objectives and a named QMR (P1.2).

a: The QMS scope, the interaction of processes and any justified exclusions
1.e: ISO 13485 4.2.2 - the manual frames the scope, process interactions and any exclusions (P1.4).

1.q: Under ISO 13485 6.2, staff whose work affects quality must be competent on the basis of:
1.e: Competence is evidence-based: education, training, skills and experience (P1.5).

1.q: Why is design & development planning a critical-path activity in Phase 1?
a: It is the backbone of the technical file and feeds Phase 2's design controls
1.e: Planned D&D is where safety and performance are built in - it drives the technical file (P1.7).

2.q: The integrated risk file opened in Phase 2 covers which branches?
2.e: One integrated file: ISO 14971 (safety) + AAMI TIR57 (security) + EU AI Act Art 9 (AI) - activity P2.1.

a: Inputs traced to outputs and to verification
2.e: Design inputs -> outputs -> verification & validation is the chain an auditor follows.

2.q: Which lifecycle runs in PARALLEL with general design controls in Phase 2?
2.e: Software development (IEC 62304) runs alongside design controls - same wave.

2.q: ISO 14971 requires risk controls to be applied in which order of priority?
a: Inherent safety by design, then protective measures, then information for safety
2.e: The risk-control hierarchy is inherent safe design, then protective measures, then information for safety (P2.1).

2.q: IEC 62366-1 usability engineering is required because most device incidents are caused by:
2.e: Use error is the leading source of harm, so usability engineering must prove safe use (P2.4).

2.q: Under the EU AI Act, an AI-based medical device is typically classified as:
2.e: Medical-device AI is usually high-risk, triggering data governance, transparency, oversight and robustness duties (P2.7).

2.q: A corrective action (CAPA) may only be closed once you have:
2.e: ISO 13485 8.5.2 requires verification of effectiveness before a CAPA is closed (P2.10).

3.q: Which governance activity is established in Phase 3?
3.e: Phase 3 stands up internal audit, management review and the policy cycle.

3.q: Management review must be driven by:
a: Analysed quality, security and AI data
3.e: Top management steers the QMS using analysed data and trends.

3.q: What is the focus of Phase 3?
3.e: Phase 3 makes the system governed and led.

a: The owner of the audited process
Objective - they must not audit their own work
3.e: ISO 13485 8.2.4 requires objective auditors who do not audit their own work (P3.1).

3.q: In business continuity planning, RTO and RPO define:
a: The maximum tolerable downtime and data loss
3.e: Recovery Time / Recovery Point Objectives bound acceptable downtime and data loss (P3.4).

3.q: Why must the medical device file be kept continuously current?
a: Because regulations, standards and guidance change and conformity must stay demonstrated
3.e: Regulatory monitoring keeps the technical documentation conformant as requirements evolve (P3.3).

4.q: In which phase is the Declaration of Conformity issued?
4.e: Compiling the technical file and issuing the DoC is the market gate in Phase 4 (P4.3).

4.q: The Notified Body conformity assessment is typically split into:
4.e: Stage 1 checks documentation readiness; Stage 2 audits effective operation on-site (P4.6, P4.7).

4.q: FMEA on all processes belongs to which Quality System Essential?
4.e: FMEA is a continual-improvement activity that prevents nonconformities (Phase 4).

4.q: For an IVD, the counterpart of the MDR clinical evaluation is the:
4.e: IVDs require a performance evaluation - scientific validity, analytical and clinical performance (P4.1).

a: Vigilance, the risk file, the clinical evaluation and CAPA
4.e: PMS closes the loop into vigilance, the risk file, the clinical evaluation and CAPA (P4.2).

4.q: In the technical file, the GSPR checklist must:
a: Link every general safety & performance requirement to its evidence
4.e: Each general safety and performance requirement is mapped to traceable evidence (P4.3).

4.q: The manufacturer may affix the CE marking only after:
a: The Notified Body issues the certificate
4.e: CE marking follows certificate issue at the end of Stage 2 (P4.7).
